PREFACE TO FOURTH EDITION .
The Codes of Practice editedby me have met with a generous
receptionfrom the Professionand I have endeavored to make some
substantialimprovement in each edition.
This editionhas been revised,and printed from new platesand
contains allamendments and acts relatingto the Code including
those passed by theGeneral Assembly of 1906.
The annotations include all cases in the firsttwelve numbers of
28 Kentucky Law Reporter,-cited as "R ",-bringing notes down
to March 15,1906.
When a caseispublished in the Ky .Reports and the Ky . Law
Reporter , both publicationsare cited. The Law Reporter begins
with 78 Ky .and 116 Ky .isthe lastReport issued,sinceitsissual
the Law Reporter alone is cited.
In the Appendix will be found the rules of the Court of Appeals
and the sectionsof the Kentucky Statutes relating:
1. To thejurisdictionof the courtsof the State.
2. To courtshaving sixjudges.
3. To courtshaving continuous sessions.
4. To selectionof specialjudges.
5. To change of venue in civiland criminal cases.
NEW CASTLE,KY ., June , 1906. JOHN D. CARROLL .
entries.citation: Civil and Criminal Codes of Practice of Kentucky, 1906, p. 8

PREFACE TO THIRD EDITION
This edition contains all amendments and notes of decisions to
July 1,1900, includingcasesreportedin 101 Ky .and 21 Ky . Law
Reporter. The book has been entirelyreprintedand revised,and
allnotes willbe found in appropriateplaces. The number of the
sectionson each page are placedat top of page,and other improve-
ments have been made in the arrangement .
In theAppendix willbe found the rulesof the Court of Appeals
and the sections of the Kentucky Statutes relating:
1. To the jurisdictionof the courts of the State.
2. To courtshaving four judges.
3. To courtshaving continuous sessions.
4. To selectionof specialjudges.
5. To change of venue in civiland criminal cases.
Also the laws of the United States concerning removal of ac-
tionsand prosecutionsto the Federal courts.
NEW CASTLE, KY ., Sept. 1900.
JOHN D. CARROLL .

entries.ocr_text:
PREFACE TO SECOND EDITION .
This editionof the Codes containsallamendments ,and notesof
decisionsto January, 1895, includingcasesreportedin 95 Ky .
and 16 Ky .Law Reporter. Unreporteddecisionsof theCourt of
Appeals found in the Ky .Law Reporters bearing upon sectionsof
the Codes have also been inserted .
An index in the middle of a book seems out of place,and I have
put indexesto bothCodes,as wellas forms, at theend of the
volume .
The catchwords tosections,theindexesand thenoteshave been
revised, rewrittenand made more complete, and many forms not
in former editions have been added .
In an appendixwillbe foundthesectionsof theKentucky Stat-
utes relating:
1. To the jurisdictionof allcourts in the State.
2. To courts having four judges.
3. To courtshaving continuous sessions.
4. To selectionof specialjudges.
5. To change of venue in civiland criminal cases.
Also the laws of the United States relatingto the Removal of
Actions and Prosecutions to the United States Courts, and the
Rules adopted by the Court of Appeals of Kentucky .
In the citation of cases I have not assumed to criticise or com-
ment , nor undertakento say what theCourt ofAppeals willde-
cide when it comes to construe sectionsnot heretoforepassed
upon , but have contented myself with statingthe law as I have
found itin thecases. My observationhas been thatthe court does
not always adopt the views of the commentator , and that expres-
sions of opinion are more misleading than useful.
The Kentucky Statutescontains the general laws of the State
not found in the Codes , and the relation between the Statutes
and Codes isso intimatethat I have inevery instancewhere either
a sectionof the Codes or a note theretocontainedany referenceto
the Statutes,or any sectionof the Statuteshad any bearing on the
Codes,citedand notedthesectionof theStatutesapplicable.
JOHN D. CARROLL .
NEW CASTLE , KY .,March ,1895.

entries.ocr_text:
PREFACE TO FIRST EDITION .
The originalCode of CivilPracticewas adopted by the Legisla-
ture in March ,1851 ;thisCode was revisedand amended ,a Crimi-
nalCode prepared,and,in1854,theCodes ofPracticeinciviland
criminal cases were adopted .
The presentCodes were prepared, under an act of the Legisla-
ture, by Richard A. Buckner and Joshua F. Bullitt, Commis-
sioners, and Alvin Duvall, umpire ; were adopted by the Legisla-
ture in 1876,and took effectJanuary 1,1877.
This editioncontainsallamendments to date, and a number of
acts relatingdirectlyto the subject-matter of the Codes . I have
added at the end of the CivilCode an appendix giving original
and appellatejurisdictionof courts in civilcases; and article2,
chapter 66,General Statutesrelatingto rent- mode and manner
of recovery .
The notes of decisionsof the Court of Appeals have, as a rule,
been confined to cases decided since 1851 - but a number of notes
of decisions prior to that date have been inserted- and include
cases reported in 84 Ky ., bringing notes down to January
term,1887.
The sectionsin brackets referto corresponding sectionsin the
Codes of 18543; following the index of the Criminal Code is a table
showing correspondingsectionsof thatCode and Code of 1854.
I trustthat the notes, referencesand forms willbe found com-
plete, the arrangement convenient,and that, as a whole,the book
will meet with favor and prove usefulto the profession.
I desireto acknowledgemy deep obligationsto Judge Joseph
Barbour forvaluablesuggestionsand advicefrequentlyasked for,
and carefullygiven. To E. W. Hines,Esq ., I am indebtedfor
his courtesyin furnishingme advance pages of 84 Ky .
NEW CASTLE ,KY .,September ,1888.
JOHN D. CARROLL .

THE
CIVIL CODE OF PRACTICE .
TITLE I.
PRELIMINARY PROVISIONS .
§ 1. Civil cases defined .
§ 2. Civil actions defined.
§ 3. Special proceedings.
§ 1. Civilcasesdefined. Civil cases are actions or special
proceedings.
§ 2. Civil actionsdefined. A civilaction is a demand , by plead-
ings,in a court of justice,for the enforcement of an allegedright
of a plaintiffagainst a defendant. ("Action" further defined,
sec. 732-34.)
§ 3. Specialproceedings. Every othercivilcase isa specialpro-
ceeding.
§ 2. (1) Civilactions. See note 1 to
вес. 3 .
(2) Provisional remedy , such as an at-
tachment or injunction, isnot a causeof
action, it is incidentalto the actionin
which it is sought, and can only be ob-
tained in the court in which the action
was brought or ispending at the time it
is sought. Moore v. Sheppard, 1 Met .
97 .
§3 . (1) Special proceedings. The
word "action" embraces special proceed-
ings. Secs . 732-34. In the absence of
any specialauthority for a summary
remedy the party must resortto a civil
action for the enforcement and protec-
tionof his privaterights. Gay v.Mor-
gan, 4 Bush 606,and it is only in cases
where a specificremedy isprovidedby
statute that rights can be enforced
otherwisethan by civilaction. Stephens
v.Miller,80 Ky .47,3 R. 523; Kinney v.
O'Bannon ,6 Bush 692 ; Combs v . Com .,24
R. 1310. For some of the cases in which
summary proceedingsmay be resorted
to, see sec. 444. Veach v. Wickersham ,
11 Bush 261 .
(2) Claimants' bond for property lev-
iedon under execution- motion forjudg-

classification.title: Civil Code of Practice - Preliminary Provisions
classification.summary: This section defines civil cases as either actions or special proceedings. A civil action is defined as a legal demand made through pleadings in a court of justice to enforce an alleged right. Special proceedings encompass all other civil cases. The text also clarifies that provisional remedies like attachments or injunctions are incidental to an action and not causes of action themselves. It further elaborates on special proceedings, noting that in the absence of specific statutory authority for a summary remedy, a civil action is the standard recourse for enforcing private rights. Examples of special proceedings include claims on bonds for property levied under execution, bastardy cases, and proceedings to enforce forfeited recognizances or bonds.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text defines fundamental legal terms related to civil procedure in Kentucky. It outlines the distinction between civil actions and special proceedings and provides examples of each. There is no mention of race, and the definitions and procedures described are general to all civil litigation, not specific to any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled Civil Code of Practice.

classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the forms of civil actions, distinguishing between 'ordinary' and 'equitable' actions. It details how actions are classified, how errors in classification can be corrected, and the procedures for transferring actions between different dockets. The text focuses on procedural aspects of civil litigation and does not contain any provisions related to racial segregation or discrimination.

It defines and differentiates between 'ordinary' and 'equitable' actions, outlining rules for their classification, transfer between courts, and correction of errors. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It details the process of delivering case papers and orders, and specifies conditions under which a defendant may be required to post a bond to ensure payment of any judgment if they move to transfer issues to an equity docket. It also covers the waiver of errors related to the form of action or court decisions if not properly objected to.

There is no mention of race, nor any indication of differential treatment based on race. The statute appears to be a general procedural rule within the civil code. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title III, Part III, Sections 22-24
classification.summary: These sections of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outline general rules for parties involved in legal actions. Section 22 allows persons with a shared interest in an action and its relief to be joined as plaintiffs. Section 23 permits individuals claiming an adverse interest or necessary for complete determination to be made defendants. Section 24 mandates that persons united in interest must be joined as either plaintiffs or defendants, with provisions for making a reluctant plaintiff a defendant.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details general procedural rules for civil litigation regarding the joinder of parties. It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly refers to race, segregation, or any other characteristic typically associated with Jim Crow laws. The rules are applicable to all individuals regardless of race. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Numerous parties- one or more may sue or defend for all.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions under which one or more parties can sue or defend on behalf of a larger group. This applies when the question involves a common or general interest of many persons, or when the parties are too numerous to be brought before the court within a reasonable time. It also includes various sub-points detailing specific scenarios and necessary parties in different legal actions, such as those involving counties, distributees, heirs, land ownership, and mortgages.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text, § 25 of the Civil and Criminal Code 1906, deals with procedural rules regarding parties in legal actions. It addresses how to handle situations with numerous parties or common interests. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws from 1906. The confidence is high because the text is purely procedural and lacks any racial component. Human review is not needed.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title III, Partiesto ActionsGenerally
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines rules for parties involved in legal actions. It details how individuals severally liable on contracts, parties to bills of exchange, promissory notes, common orders, checks, and sureties can be included in the same lawsuit at the plaintiff's option. It also addresses actions against decedents and persons jointly bound on contracts, and the effect of judgments against one party. The text further elaborates on situations where one or more parties can sue for the benefit of all, particularly when parties are numerous or it's impractical to bring them all before the court. It covers various scenarios including actions against cities, church property, and taxpayers with common grievances.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text from the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky deals with procedural aspects of civil litigation, specifically concerning parties to actions and joint liability. It outlines how multiple parties can be included in a single lawsuit and the conditions under which one party can sue on behalf of a group. There is no mention of race, and the provisions are general to all litigants. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the text is purely procedural and lacks any discriminatory language or intent. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of statutes.

classification.title: Claimant of property may be made party - practice.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedure for a person claiming an interest in property involved in a legal action (recovery of real/personal property, or subjection to a demand under attachment/lien) to be made a party to the suit. The claimant must file a verified petition stating their claim. The court may then order them to be made a defendant, and their petition will be treated as their answer. Non-residents must provide security for costs. The statute also includes case law annotations regarding when vendors, mortgagees, assignees, and other claimants should or can be made parties to an action, and the timing and requirements for filing such petitions.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes civil procedure for adding parties to lawsuits involving property claims. It does not mention race, nor does it establish any discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906. The confidence is high because the text clearly pertains to civil procedure and lacks any racial component.

classification.title: Claimant of property may be made party- practice.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedure for a defendant in an action (contract or personal property recovery) to bring a third-party claimant into the lawsuit. If a defendant files an affidavit stating a non-party claims the subject of the action without collusion, the court can order the property to be secured, paid into court, or delivered to the claimant. The claimant is then required to appear and maintain or relinquish their claim. If they appear, they can become the defendant, and the original defendant is discharged from liability. If they fail to appear after service, they are barred from their claim.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text (Sections 30 and 31) deals with civil procedure, specifically how to handle situations where a third party claims ownership of property that is the subject of a lawsuit. It outlines a legal mechanism for resolving competing claims to property. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or restrict any racial group. The statute is procedural and applies generally to any parties involved in such legal disputes. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is presented as part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: These sections of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky deal with procedural aspects of legal actions, specifically concerning the substitution of parties in lawsuits involving property seized under execution or distress warrants. Section 32 allows a party in whose favor an execution or distress warrant was issued to be substituted as the defendant in an action against an officer, provided security for costs is given. Section 33 outlines how a tenant or their assignee can bring an action to recover property seized under a distress warrant, and how the property can be taken from the officer under an order for delivery.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details legal procedures related to property seizure and recovery through distress warrants and executions. It outlines how parties can be substituted in legal actions and how property can be reclaimed. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The statute appears to be a purely procedural matter within the civil code. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law, and the confidence is high. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code from 1906.

classification.title: Married women - actions by and against.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions under which married women can sue and be sued, either alone or with their husbands. It specifies that a wife may sue alone in actions between husband and wife, concerning her separate or general property, or for personal suffering or injury to her person or character if her husband refuses to unite. In all other actions, the wife and husband must join. The statute also addresses situations where the husband deserts his wife or if either party is of unsound mind or imprisoned. It clarifies that the common law rule is in force except in the cases mentioned, and a next friend cannot prosecute a suit for an infant wife against the wishes of her infant husband.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the legal rights of married women to initiate or defend legal actions. It does not contain any explicit or implicit references to race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is presented as part of a compiled legal code (Civil and Criminal Code 1906).

guardian ad litem to representinfant.

fact that an infant sues both by his
guardian and next friend may be
grounds for specialdemurrer,but isnot

the rightsof the infanthave been preju-
diced. Henning v.Barringer, 10 R. 674;
and see Tabor v . Cline, 18 R. 9 .

classification.title: § 36 Persons Under Disability- Provision Concerning.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for legal defense for individuals under disability, including infants, persons of unsound mind, and adult prisoners. It specifies that their defense must be made by a guardian, committee, or guardian ad litem, and that no judgment can be rendered against them until such defense is made or a report stating inability to defend is filed. It also includes several case law annotations related to infant plaintiffs and defendants.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses legal procedures for individuals under disability, such as infants and those of unsound mind. It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly discriminates based on race. The focus is on legal representation and due process for vulnerable populations. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Persons Under Disability- Provision Concerning
classification.summary: This statute outlines the qualifications and liabilities of a 'next friend' who sues on behalf of a person under disability. It also details the appointment and duties of a 'guardian ad litem' for defendants under disability. The statute focuses on procedural aspects of legal representation for minors and incapacitated individuals.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with legal procedures for individuals under disability, such as minors or those deemed incapacitated. It specifies requirements for legal representatives ('next friend' and 'guardian ad litem') and their liability for costs. There is no mention of race or any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

of. A guardian ad litem must be a regular, practic-

3. To prepare case and procure evidence. It shall be the
duty of the guardian ad litem to attend properly to the prepa-
rationof the case; and in an ordinary action he may cause as

ject to the controlof the court; and in an equitableaction he
may take depositions, not, however , exceeding three, without
leave of the court .

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 38: Guardian ad Litem Provisions
classification.summary: This statute outlines the appointment, duties, and fees of a guardian ad litem in legal proceedings. A guardian ad litem must be a practicing attorney, appointed by the court or clerk, to represent the interests of a defendant under disability (such as an infant). Their duties include preparing the case, procuring evidence, and attending to the defendant's interests. The court determines their reasonable fee, paid by the plaintiff and taxed as costs. The statute also details conditions for appointment, the guardian's ability to appeal, and limitations on waiving rights.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the appointment and duties of a guardian ad litem, a legal role designed to protect the interests of individuals under disability, such as minors. The text does not contain any explicit or implicit references to race, nor does it suggest any discriminatory application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the procedure for commencing a civil action. It details that an action is initiated by filing a petition or a statement of facts with the clerk of the proper court, and then by issuing a summons or making a warning order.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically detailing the procedural steps for initiating a civil lawsuit. It outlines the filing of a petition and the issuance of a summons or warning order. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title IV: Manner of Commencing an Action
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the procedures for issuing and serving a summons to commence a legal action. It details the form and requisites of a summons, the ability to issue summons to any county, the requirement for a copy for each defendant, and the time fixed for the return of the summons. It also includes case law annotations related to summons and the commencement of actions.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural aspects of initiating a lawsuit through the issuance and service of a summons. It focuses on the mechanics of legal process, such as the form of the summons, where it can be served, and the timing of its return. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute appears to be a general procedural rule applicable to all litigants. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title IV, Service of Summons - Actual Service
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code details the procedures for serving summons in legal proceedings. It outlines how summons should be delivered, the conditions under which a false return is considered conclusive or can be impeached, and rules regarding service on holidays or Sundays. It also covers aspects like the authority of officers to execute summons, the requirements for a valid return, and the presumption of proper service. Specific subsections address fraud in service procurement, the effect of mistakes in dates, and the service of process on corporations and infant defendants.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural aspects of serving legal summons. It focuses on the mechanics of delivery, the validity of returns, and the conditions under which service can be challenged. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is identified as 'codes' because it is part of a compiled Civil and Criminal Code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Sections 49-51
classification.summary: These sections of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky detail the procedures for the service of summons in legal actions. Section 49 addresses the amendment and penalties for erroneous returns of service. Section 50 outlines the process for acknowledging service by endorsement on the summons. Section 51 specifies how summons should be served on counties, cities, corporations, and carriers, including provisions for special bailiffs and waiver of service.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details procedural aspects of legal summons service within the Kentucky Civil and Criminal Code of 1906. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. The statutes focus solely on the mechanics of legal notification and service. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title IV, Service of Summons - Actual Service
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code outlines the procedures for serving legal summons on various entities, including municipal corporations, private corporations, railroads, and common carriers. It specifies who can be served on behalf of these entities, such as mayors, chief officers, agents, or marshals, and in which counties service can be made. It also includes provisions for lessees or operators of railroads when the owner cannot be served directly.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural aspects of serving legal summons on various types of corporations and entities. It focuses on the mechanics of legal notification and does not contain any language, explicit or implicit, that suggests racial discrimination or segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled Civil and Criminal Code. The confidence is high because the text is purely procedural and lacks any racial indicators. Human review is not needed.

classification.title: Service of Summons - Actual Service
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the procedures for serving summons in legal actions. It details how to serve corporations, non-residents engaged in business in the state, and infants under fourteen years of age. For infants, it specifies service on a father, guardian, mother, or person in charge, with provisions for appointing a guardian ad litem if necessary. It also includes case law annotations related to these procedures.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural aspects of serving legal summons in Kentucky. It covers service on various entities and individuals, including corporations, non-residents, and minors. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Service of Summons - Actual Service. § 53 [82] Person of unsound mind
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for serving a summons on a defendant who is of unsound mind. It specifies that the summons must be served on the defendant and on a designated representative (committee, father, guardian, wife, or person in charge) if they reside in the county. Special provisions are made for married women of unsound mind whose husbands are plaintiffs or not. It also includes an exception where service may be omitted if a physician certifies that it would be injurious to the defendant's health, with the certificate to be returned with the summons. The text also includes case law annotations regarding service on infants and persons of unsound mind.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the legal process for serving a summons on individuals deemed to be of unsound mind. It focuses on procedural aspects of legal service and does not contain any language or provisions that suggest racial discrimination or segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 54-56
classification.summary: These sections of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code outline the procedures for serving legal summonses on specific categories of defendants: prisoners, communities of Shakers, and non-residents (excluding infants, persons of unsound mind, and prisoners). For prisoners, service is made through the penitentiary keeper. For Shakers, it involves posting a notice at their meeting house and delivering a copy to a member. For non-residents, service is by personal delivery by someone known to the defendant, with specific affidavit requirements.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute sections detail the procedural aspects of serving legal documents to different types of defendants, including prisoners, Shakers, and non-residents. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The classifications are based on legal status (prisoner, non-resident) or religious/communal affiliation (Shakers). Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906 - Title IV, Article 2: Constructive Service
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the procedures for constructive service of summons in legal actions. It details the conditions under which a defendant can be considered summoned without direct personal service, including non-residency, departure from the state to defraud creditors, absence from the state for a specified period, leaving the county to avoid service, concealment, or when the defendant's name and residence are unknown. It also specifies the process for issuing a warning order and the effect of such an order.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with legal procedures for serving summons when a defendant cannot be personally reached. It outlines conditions like non-residency, absence, or concealment. There is no mention of race or any discriminatory practices based on race within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws. Confidence is high because the text is clear and does not contain any racial indicators.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 58
classification.summary: This statute outlines the requirements for an affidavit to obtain a warning order, which is a form of constructive service in legal proceedings. It details what the affidavit must state, including the grounds for the order, the defendant's location (or ignorance thereof), and the nearest post-office. It also specifies who can make the affidavit (plaintiff, agent, attorney, guardian, etc.) and under what conditions.

This involves affidavits detailing the defendant's absence or non-residency. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or effect based on race. The statute is purely procedural and does not appear to be a Jim Crow law. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for is_jim_crow, with a high confidence score, and the category is null. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws. The racial indicator is 'none'.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Section 59: Attorney Appointment for Constructive Service
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for appointing an attorney to represent a defendant who has been constructively served (i.e., not personally notified of the lawsuit). The clerk appoints an attorney, who must attempt to inform the defendant of the action by mail and report back to the court. The attorney's powers are limited unless employed by the defendant or if the defendant is under disability (other than coverture or infancy and coverture). If the attorney cannot inform the defendant or learns of a disability, they must report to the court and make an affirmative defense if possible.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the procedural appointment of attorneys for defendants who have not been personally served. It outlines the duties and limitations of these appointed attorneys. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute appears to be a general procedural rule for civil litigation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the rules for determining the county in which an action must or may be brought. It specifies venue requirements for actions concerning real property, fines, forfeitures, wills, decedent's estates, wards against guardians, specific state entities, prisoners or inmates of asylums, and injuries to persons or property. It also covers procedural aspects like attachments and enforcement of contracts.

The statutes described (Sections 61-82) pertain to procedural rules for civil litigation, such as where lawsuits concerning real property, estates, or injuries must be filed. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title V, § 63
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the rules for determining the county in which an action must or may be brought. It covers various scenarios including actions to recover fines or forfeitures, actions involving land (sales, injuries, liens, rescission of contracts), and actions concerning decedents' estates. The general principle is that actions should be brought in the county where the cause of action arose, with specific exceptions and rules for actions related to real property.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details rules of civil and criminal procedure regarding venue (the county in which an action must or may be brought). It discusses various legal actions such as recovering fines, dealing with land disputes, and settling estates. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute appears to be a procedural law governing legal proceedings generally, not a law designed to enforce racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence score and no need for human review.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Sections 64-65
classification.summary: These sections of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outline the proper county in which various legal actions must be brought. This includes actions concerning boundaries, public officers, official bonds, wills, and the settlement of estates of deceased persons or assigned estates. Specific provisions detail where actions related to real estate or liens must be filed.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details rules of civil procedure regarding venue (the county in which an action must be brought). It covers a range of legal matters such as actions against public officers, wills, and estate settlements. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The language is purely procedural and legalistic, focusing on where lawsuits can be filed. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906 - Title V: County in Which Action Must or May Be Brought
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the specific county in which various legal actions must be brought. This includes actions related to real estate, distribution or partition of a decedent's estate, and actions by a ward against a guardian. It details jurisdictional requirements based on the location of property, the qualification of personal representatives or guardians, and the location of administration.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details rules of civil procedure regarding the county in which legal actions must be brought. It focuses on jurisdictional and venue requirements for cases involving real estate, estates, and guardianships. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Sections 68-71
classification.summary: These sections of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outline the proper county in which various civil actions must be brought. This includes actions against Sinking Fund Commissioners or the Board of Education (county of the seat of government), prisoners or inmates of asylums (county of residence when confined), actions upon a 'no property found' return (county of judgment, defendant's residence, or where summoned), and actions against banks or insurance companies (county of principal office/business or where transaction occurred).
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the rules for determining the proper county in which civil lawsuits should be filed. It covers various defendants and types of actions, such as those involving government entities, incarcerated individuals, or corporations. There is no mention of race, and the provisions appear to be general procedural rules applicable to all citizens. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically sections 72 and 73, outlines the rules for determining the county in which an action must or may be brought against corporations and common carriers. It details venue rules for actions based on contracts, torts, and carriage of property, and includes case law citations to interpret these provisions.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text (Sections 72 and 73 of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906) deals with the jurisdiction and venue for civil actions against corporations and common carriers. It specifies where lawsuits can be filed based on the nature of the action (contract or tort) and the location of the corporation's or carrier's business or where the event occurred. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code. Confidence is high because the text is clear and directly addresses procedural matters without any racial implications.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Section 74
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the venue (county) in which various civil actions must be brought. It covers actions against carriers for property damage or personal injury, and general actions for injury to the person or character, including libel. For libel actions, it specifies venue based on the plaintiff's residence, the publication location, or the location of the event the publication relates to. It also includes a provision for defenses in assault and battery cases.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the rules for determining the proper county in which civil lawsuits should be filed. It addresses various types of civil actions, including those against carriers and for personal or character injury. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The statute appears to be a procedural rule for civil litigation and does not fall under the scope of Jim Crow laws. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence. The category is null as it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code from 1906. The racial indicator is 'none' as race is not mentioned or implied.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906 - Sections 75-78: County in Which Action Must or May Be Brought
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code outlines the rules for determining the proper county in which legal actions must or may be brought. It covers general rules for defendants who can be constructively summoned, specific rules for alimony or divorce actions, and rules for actions against contractors for public works. It also touches upon transitory actions and provides case law examples for various legal scenarios.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural rules for determining the venue (county) of legal actions. It specifies where different types of lawsuits, such as those involving constructive summons, divorce, or public works contracts, must be filed. There is no mention of race, and the rules appear to apply generally to all individuals and entities. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is presented as part of a compiled code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Section 79
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the rules for determining the county in which a civil action must or may be brought, particularly concerning the jurisdiction over defendants based on their residence or where they are summoned. It details conditions for rendering judgments against single defendants and discusses various scenarios related to jurisdiction, consent, and service of process.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text pertains to civil procedure, specifically the rules governing where lawsuits can be filed and how jurisdiction is established over defendants. It discusses concepts like residence, summons, and consent to jurisdiction. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines rules regarding the jurisdiction and service of summons in civil actions, particularly when multiple defendants are involved and some are served outside the county where the action is brought. It specifies conditions under which a judgment can be rendered against defendants served outside the county, focusing on procedural aspects and objections to jurisdiction. It also addresses situations involving bankrupt defendants.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details rules of civil procedure concerning jurisdiction and the service of summons on multiple defendants. It focuses on legal technicalities of where an action can be brought and against whom a judgment can be rendered based on where defendants are served and reside. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled Civil and Criminal Code.

classification.title: Causes of action that may be joined
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions under which multiple causes of action can be united in a single lawsuit. These conditions include that each cause of action must affect all parties, be brought in the same county, and be prosecuted by the same kind of action. It then lists six categories of actions that can be joined: upon contracts, concerning real property, concerning personal property, partition of real or personal property, injuries to character, and injuries to person and property.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically sections 82-83, which deal with the joinder of actions in legal proceedings. The content of these sections focuses on procedural rules for civil litigation, detailing how different types of legal claims can be combined in a single lawsuit. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code. The confidence is high because the text is purely procedural and lacks any racial indicators. Human review is not needed.

It outlines various scenarios where different types of claims can or cannot be brought together in a single lawsuit, citing numerous case examples. The content focuses on procedural aspects of litigation, such as actions involving guardians, debtors, distributees, ejectment, land disputes, libel, slander, liens, personal injury, and settlements of estates. It does not contain any provisions related to racial segregation or discrimination.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Sections 84-86
classification.summary: These sections of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky deal with the joinder of actions in legal pleadings. Section 84 allows a party to strike a cause of action from their pleading. Section 85 empowers the court to require a party to elect which cause of action to prosecute if multiple causes are improperly joined, and to strike out improperly joined causes upon refusal. Section 86 states that objections to the misjoinder of causes of action are waived unless made pursuant to section 85.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details rules of civil procedure regarding the joinder and separation of legal actions within a lawsuit. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or effect on any racial group. The language is purely procedural and applies to all litigants. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of statutes.

classification.title: Pleadings in General
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky defines pleadings in civil actions, outlining their form, sufficiency, and allowed types. It also details rules regarding the construction of pleadings, the necessity of allegation and proof, and the use of pleadings as evidence.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from Chapter I of Title VII of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky, specifically concerning 'Pleadings in General' for civil actions. The content focuses entirely on the procedural aspects of legal filings, such as defining pleadings, their form, sufficiency, and how they are to be constructed and proven. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Chapter II: Petition
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the requirements for legal petitions. It details the forms of pleadings allowed, the necessity of stating facts that constitute a cause of action, and the demand for specific relief. It also mentions the possibility of a general prayer for other relief and the conditions under which such relief can be granted.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural rules for legal petitions within the Kentucky Civil and Criminal Code of 1906. It focuses on the structure and content of legal filings, such as stating facts, demanding relief, and the types of pleadings allowed. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 90 - Notes to Title VII Petition
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky provides annotations and case law references related to Title VII, specifically concerning petitions. It covers various legal points such as the sufficiency of averments, the effect of exhibits, and requirements for pleading specific types of claims like false imprisonment, guaranty, guardian's settlements, heir responsibility for ancestor's debt, husband's liability for wife's debt, indorsers of notes, interest recovery, judgments, and the law of other states. It also touches upon legal conclusions and legislative power regarding pleading forms.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is a section of annotations and case law references for a legal code, specifically concerning the requirements for drafting a petition in civil and criminal cases. It details procedural legal points and does not contain any language or provisions that suggest racial discrimination or segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

v . Cooke , 2 Met . 275 , it is held that the
failure to state date of note sued on and

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 90
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the requirements for a legal petition. It details what must be included in the petition, such as the facts constituting the cause of action, the need for clear and concise language, and the prayer for relief. It also discusses consistency in pleadings and the implications of omissions or specific demands in the prayer.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural requirements for filing a legal petition in civil and criminal cases. It focuses on the structure, content, and consistency of legal pleadings. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest a discriminatory purpose or effect. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title VII Notes to § 90
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 provides notes and case law references related to legal petitions. It covers various aspects of pleading requirements, including stating the amount of debt, specifying due dates for notes, averring presentation and demand, setting forth specific words in slander cases, avoiding splitting causes of action, actions against government agencies, surety actions, tavernkeeper liens, tax titles, quieting title, trespass, vendor's liens, warranty in land sales, and requirements for setting out writings in petitions. It does not contain any provisions related to racial segregation or discrimination.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically notes related to legal petitions. A thorough review of the text reveals no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices. The content focuses solely on procedural aspects of legal filings and case law interpretations. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Chapter III: Demurrer
classification.summary: This chapter of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky defines and distinguishes between special and general demurrers in legal proceedings. It outlines the specific grounds for a special demurrer, such as lack of jurisdiction, lack of legal capacity to sue, pending actions, or defect of parties. It also details which objections are waived if not specifically raised in a demurrer, with the exception of objections to the court's jurisdiction over the subject matter. The text includes case law citations to illustrate these points.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the legal procedural concept of a 'demurrer' within the Kentucky Civil and Criminal Code of 1906. A demurrer is a formal objection to a pleading that challenges its legal sufficiency. The text outlines the types of demurrers (special and general) and the specific grounds upon which a special demurrer can be filed, such as lack of jurisdiction or defect of parties. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

It outlines the effect of failing to demur and discusses various legal scenarios and precedents related to demurrers, including waiver of objections, pending actions, and admissions by demurrer. The text does not contain any provisions related to racial segregation or discrimination.

A demurrer is a formal objection to a legal pleading. The text details the conditions under which a demurrer can be filed, its effects, and related legal principles and case law. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 93
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically § 93, provides notes and case law interpretations related to demurrers in legal proceedings. It covers various aspects of demurrers, including when they are available, how they can be waived, their effect on jurisdiction, and their implications for costs and appeals. The text is a compilation of judicial decisions that clarify procedural rules.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses legal procedural rules related to demurrers. It does not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race, nor does it relate to any of the typical categories associated with Jim Crow laws (education, housing, marriage, public accommodations, incarceration, voting, labor). Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for is_jim_crow, with a confidence of 1.0, and the category is null. The document type is identified as 'codes' because it is part of a compiled code from 1906. The racial indicator is 'none' as there is no racial dimension detected. Human review is not needed.

Title VII ] [§ 96Counter-Claim, Set-Off, Cross-Petition.
§ 96 [126,128] Counter-claim,set-off,cross-petition. 1. Counter-
claim defined. A counter -claim is a cause of action in favor of a
defendantagainsta plaintiff, or againsthim and another,which
arises out of the contract, or transactions, statedin the petition
as the foundation of the plaintiff'sclaim, or which is connected
with the subjectof the action. (See further, secs. 99,372. See
as to noticewhen lienon realproperty is asserted,Ky .Stat., sec.
2358a .)
2. Set-offdefined. A set-offisa cause of actionarisingupon
a contract,judgment or award in favor of a defendant against
a plaintiff, or against him and another; and it can not be
pleaded except in an action upon a contract, judgment or
award . (See further,secs.97,372,377. Sce as to noticewhen
lienon realproperty isasserted,Ky .Stat.,sec.2358a.)
3. Cross-petitiondefined. A cross-petitionis the commence-
ment of an action by a defendant against a co-defendant, or a
person who is not a party to the action, or againstboth ;or by
a plaintiffagainsta co-plaintiff,or a person who is not a party
to the action,or againstboth;and isnot allowed to a defeudant,
except upon a cause of action which affects, or is affectedby ,
the originalcause of action; nor to a plaintiff, except upon a
cause of action which affects, or is affectedby, a setoff or
counter-claim. (In what pleadings counter-claim and set- off
allowed, sec. 111 ; trialof, sec. 372 ;judgment on, sec. 387 ; in
what pleading cross-petitionallowed,secs.111 ; 692. See as to
notice when lien on real property is asserted, Ky . Stat., sec.
2358a .)
510 , 14 R. 628 ; nor is denial that de-
fendant is indebted as alleged, or that
account is just sufficient. Thruston v.
Oldham , 6 Bush 16.
(42 ) Traverse of record- puts in is-
sue affirmativematter in pleading the
same as a writtentraverse. Ky . Union
R. R. v . Conner , 17 R. 426 .
(43 ) Unlawful contract- when relied
on as defense must be specificallyset
out . Powell v. Flanary , 109 Ky . 342,
22 R. 908 .
COUNTER -CLAIM .
§ 96. (1) Counter -claim. "First, those
causes of action which arise out of the
contract set forth in the plaintiff'speti-
tion as the foundation of his claim;
secondly, those causes of actionarising
out of the transaction set forth in the
petition as the foundationof the plain-
tiff'sclaim; and , thirdly, those causes of
action connected with the subject of the
suit." Newman's Pleadingand Practice,
page 602. Counter-claim is an action
(sec. 732-34) and may be presentedin
a reply against a set-off (sec. 98-3).
The caption of the answer should con-
tain the words " answer and counter-
claim" (sec. 97-4). Nutter v . Johnson ,
80 Ky .426, 4 R. 305 ; Cason v . Cason , 79
Ky . 558, 3 R. 367. The only restriction
the Code makes as to a counter-claim is,
that it shallbe a cause of action arising
out of the contract or transaction set
forth in the petitionas the foundation of
plaintiff'sdemand ; or that it be con-
nected with the subject of the action.
It is not required that the counter-claim
itself shall be founded in contract or
arise out of the contract set forth in
the petition, but it is sufficientthat it
arises out of the transactions set forth
in the petition or be connected with the
subjectof the action. Tinsley v. Tin-
sley, 15 B. M. 454; Forbes v. Cooper, 88
Ky . 285, 10 R. 865, and see Rennebaum
v . Atkinson , 103 Ky . 555, 20 R. 254 ; 97
85

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically § 96, discusses the rules and precedents regarding counter-claims, set-offs, and cross-petitions in legal proceedings. It outlines when a counter-claim can be filed, its relationship to the dismissal of an action, and provides various case examples illustrating its application in different civil litigation scenarios, such as contract disputes, warranty breaches, and property actions. The text does not contain any provisions related to racial segregation or discrimination.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text pertains to civil procedure, specifically the rules governing counter-claims in legal actions. It details how counter-claims are filed, their relationship to the dismissal of an action, and provides numerous case examples to illustrate their application. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of statutes from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title VII: Counter-Claim, Set-Off, Cross-Petition
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky details the legal procedures and conditions for filing counter-claims, set-offs, and cross-petitions in civil litigation. It outlines various scenarios and precedents for when such claims are permissible, including those related to injunction bonds, void executions, divorce proceedings, supersedeas bonds, partnership debts, and breaches of warranty. It also specifies the requisites for a pleading to be treated as a counter-claim and the process for their trial. The section also covers set-offs in cases involving administrators and assignees.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically Title VII, which deals with legal procedural mechanisms like counter-claims and set-offs. A thorough review of the text reveals no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The content is purely procedural and relates to how defendants can assert claims against plaintiffs within a lawsuit. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 96 - Set-off
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 details the legal principles and conditions under which a set-off (a counter-claim or deduction from a debt) can be pleaded in legal actions. It covers various scenarios including assignments of notes for set-off purposes, actions by the Commonwealth, claims against decedent's estates, and the requirement for mutual demands. The text primarily focuses on procedural aspects of civil litigation and does not contain any provisions related to racial segregation or discrimination.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses legal procedures related to set-offs in civil and criminal cases. It outlines conditions for pleading set-offs, such as the assignment of notes, actions involving the Commonwealth, and claims against estates. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 96-Notes to Counter-Claim, Set-Off, Cross-Petition
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky provides annotations and case law interpretations regarding the legal concepts of set-off and counter-claim in civil litigation. It details various scenarios and conditions under which a party can use a claim against another party as a set-off or counter-claim, including limitations, waivers, and procedural requirements. It also touches upon cross-petitions.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically section 96, which deals with procedural aspects of civil litigation, namely set-offs and counter-claims. classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code outlines the permissible contents of a 'reply' in legal pleadings. A reply can include a traverse, facts constituting estoppel or avoidance, a counter-claim against a set-off, or a cross-petition. It also includes annotations on how defects in an answer can be cured by a reply, the implications of failing to reply to a plea of contributory negligence, and the sufficiency of denials in a reply.

classification.title: General Rules of Pleading
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines general rules for legal pleadings. It mandates that parties must form a material issue before trial, that pleadings must be in English, concise, in writing, and signed. It also details requirements for verification of pleadings, specifying when it is not required and providing case law examples for various pleading scenarios such as redundancy, necessary traverses, and multiple obligations. The text focuses on procedural aspects of legal filings.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details general rules of pleading in civil and criminal cases. It focuses on the procedural requirements for filing legal documents, such as the language, format, signing, and verification of pleadings. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest a discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of statutes.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines general rules of pleading, specifically requiring that any action founded on a written instrument (note, bond, bill, etc.) must have that instrument filed as part of the pleading. If the instrument cannot be produced, the reason must be stated. For actions based on an account, a copy must be filed. The text also includes case law annotations related to these pleading requirements.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details general rules of pleading in civil and criminal cases, focusing on the requirement to file written evidence or accounts upon which an action is based. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any other element typically associated with Jim Crow laws. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence score. The category is null because it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of statutes. The racial indicator is 'none' as race is not mentioned.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Section 121-123
classification.summary: These sections of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky deal with general rules of pleading. Section 121 addresses the striking of irrelevant or redundant matter from a pleading. Section 122 outlines how judgments or determinations of courts or officers should be pleaded, stating it's sufficient to allege they were duly given or made without detailing jurisdictional facts. Section 123 discusses unnecessary allegations in actions for libel or slander, stating it's not necessary to plead extrinsic facts to show the application of defamatory matter to the plaintiff.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details rules of pleading in civil and criminal cases. It covers procedural aspects like striking irrelevant text, pleading judgments, and allegations in libel and slander cases. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any other element typically associated with Jim Crow laws. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for is_jim_crow, with a confidence of 1.0, and the category is null. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code. The racial indicator is 'none'.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title VII, Sections 124-125
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines general rules of pleading for slander/libel and actions for the recovery of land. For slander/libel, it details what a defendant may state in their answer, including truth and mitigating circumstances. For land recovery actions, it specifies requirements for the petition's description of the land and the defendant's answer regarding their claim, as well as rules for the burden of proof and costs.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details general legal procedures for civil actions, specifically concerning slander/libel and land recovery. It outlines rules for how defendants can respond to lawsuits and what evidence is admissible. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 126
classification.summary: This statute outlines the rules of pleading in civil and criminal actions, specifically addressing when allegations in a pleading are considered admitted if not specifically traversed. It details exceptions where allegations must be proven even if not traversed, such as against defendants under disability (except coverture), against constructively summoned defendants who haven't appeared, and concerning the value or amount of damages unless accompanied by an allegation of express or implied promise to pay. The accompanying notes provide case law interpretations of these rules.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns general rules of pleading and evidence in legal actions. It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly refers to race, nor does it establish any discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906. The confidence is high because the statute's content is clearly procedural and lacks any racial component. No human review is needed.

It also outlines rules for filing writings relied upon as evidence and discusses various case law examples related to pleading, proof, and judgments, including situations involving infants, non-residents, payment, and practice. It does not contain any provisions related to racial segregation or discrimination.

There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is identified as 'codes' because it is part of a compiled legal code (Civil and Criminal Code 1906).

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically § 129, deals with variances between pleadings and proof in legal actions. It states that a variance is only material if it misleads a party to their prejudice in maintaining their action or defense. If a party claims to be misled, they must demonstrate this to the court, which may then order an amendment to the pleading under just terms. The section also includes notes on the construction of this section and related legal precedents.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text, § 129 of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, pertains to legal procedural rules regarding variances between pleadings and proof. It outlines conditions under which such variances are considered material and how amendments can be made. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws. The confidence is high because the text is purely procedural and lacks any racial indicators. Human review is not needed.

classification.title: Mistakes in Pleadings - Amendments
classification.summary: This statute, section 130 of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, addresses how courts should handle variances between the allegations made in legal pleadings and the evidence presented during a trial. It allows for amendments to pleadings if the variance is not material, or even if it is material, the court may permit amendments to correct the discrepancy. The statute provides several case examples illustrating what constitutes a material or immaterial variance in different legal contexts, such as contract disputes, negligence claims, and surety actions.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically section 130, which deals with procedural rules regarding mistakes and amendments in legal pleadings. The content focuses entirely on the mechanics of legal proceedings, such as how to handle discrepancies between what is alleged in a lawsuit and what is proven by evidence. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code. The confidence is high because the text is purely procedural and lacks any racial indicators. Human review is not needed.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Sections 131-132
classification.summary: These sections of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky deal with procedural rules regarding variance and amendments in legal pleadings. Section 131 addresses situations where the proof does not match the allegations in a claim or defense, distinguishing between variance and failure of proof, and allowing for judgments against only those defendants proven to be bound in contract actions. Section 132 outlines the plaintiff's ability to amend their petition before an answer is filed, including notice requirements and when new summons may be necessary for amended petitions that introduce new causes of action.

There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically sections 133 and 134, deals with amendments to pleadings in legal proceedings. It outlines the court's power to allow amendments to correct mistakes, add or strike parties, or conform pleadings to facts proved, provided these amendments do not substantially change the claim or defense and are in furtherance of justice. It also states that errors or defects that do not affect the substantial rights of the adverse party should be disregarded. The notes provide case examples illustrating the application of these amendment rules.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text (Sections 133 and 134 of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906) pertains to the procedural rules for amending legal pleadings in court. It discusses how courts can allow or require amendments to correct errors, add parties, or conform pleadings to evidence. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code. The confidence is high because the text is purely procedural and lacks any racial indicators. Human review is not needed.

It outlines rules and case law regarding when amendments can be filed, how they affect the case, and how objections to them are handled. It covers scenarios such as pleas of limitation, incorrect defendant names, amended petitions, amended answers, and the effect of amendments on the timing of legal actions. It also touches upon the withdrawal of pleadings.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically sections 139 and 140, deals with the procedural aspects of interrogatories in legal proceedings. Section 139 addresses the verification of amendments, allowing for amendments without verification unless a new cause of action or defense is introduced. Section 140 outlines the use of interrogatories in equitable actions, stating that answers can be read as depositions and that the answering party is not confined to mere responses. It also touches upon the time to answer and the effect of delay on trial, as well as when interrogatories are allowed in ordinary actions.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title VIII: Arrest and Bail
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the procedures and conditions for obtaining an order of arrest against a defendant in a civil case. It details the requirements for the plaintiff's affidavit, the execution of a bond to protect the defendant from wrongful arrest, the form and requisites of the order of arrest, and the return day for the order. It also includes case law interpretations regarding what constitutes sufficient grounds for arrest, such as a defendant's intent to depart the state without leaving sufficient property to satisfy the claim, and clarifies that temporary absence for business or visits does not warrant an arrest order. A specific provision allows wives seeking alimony and maintenance to obtain an order of arrest against their husbands without posting a bond.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details civil procedure related to arrest and bail. It outlines the legal process for obtaining an order of arrest in civil cases, including the necessary affidavits, bonds, and court orders. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The case law cited also focuses on the interpretation of the civil code regarding departure from the state and sufficiency of property, not on racial distinctions. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Claim and Delivery of Personal Property
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the procedures for the claim and delivery of personal property. It details the requirements for executing a bond, the process of property appraisement by disinterested housekeepers, allowances for the sheriff's expenses in keeping the property, and the proceedings when a third party claims the property seized by the sheriff. It specifies that the claimant must indemnify the sheriff against the claim with a bond if they wish their claim to be valid against the sheriff.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title VIII, Section 194: Attachment - Grounds of Attachment
classification.summary: This statute outlines the grounds for issuing an attachment in civil actions in Kentucky in 1906. These grounds primarily relate to the defendant's residency status (foreign corporation or non-resident), their actions regarding property (removal, disposal with fraudulent intent), or their attempts to evade legal process (avoiding summons). It specifies conditions under which attachments can be granted for actions involving money, contracts, judgments, awards, or the recovery of personal property.

It focuses on financial and procedural aspects of debt recovery and does not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code from 1906. The confidence is high because the text is clear and unambiguous in its lack of racial content. The category is null because the statute is not a Jim Crow law.

It also specifies where attachments can be obtained and the types of contracts they apply to.

Proceedings by attachment are summary

(19) Leaving county to avoid service.
Where a debtorleavesthe county of his

for the same wrong , subsection 4 of this
