classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 198
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 details the conditions and limitations for recovering damages related to attachment proceedings. It specifies that damages are recoverable if an attachment order is wrongfully obtained, and outlines what types of damages can be claimed, such as loss of use of property, deterioration, and costs incurred in defending the attachment. It also clarifies that damages for the main action are not recoverable on the attachment bond.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 204
classification.summary: This statute outlines the duties of an officer upon whom an order of attachment is executed. It requires the officer to provide an inventory and appraisement of the attached property, along with details of the distress or process under which it is held, to the officer executing the attachment. The officer must also hold any excess property or proceeds subject to court order. Failure to comply can be punished as contempt. The text also includes several notes and case citations related to attachments, such as the attachment of real estate, garnishees, and the powers of officers.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text, § 204 of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, details the procedural requirements for executing an order of attachment. It focuses on the duties of officers involved in the attachment process, including providing inventories and holding property. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The statute appears to be a purely procedural legal regulation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

out of it ratably .

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Section 212: Lien created by attachment
classification.summary: This statute, Section 212 of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code, details how an attachment order creates a lien on a defendant's property in the county. It specifies that the lien is effective from the time the order is delivered to the sheriff, similar to how an execution would bind property. The statute also includes several case law annotations clarifying the nature and precedence of attachment liens in various scenarios, such as subsequent levies, mortgages, equitable interests, acknowledged service, assignments, property in other states, and specific types of attachable debts or interests.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 218: Preservation, use or sale of attached property
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for the preservation, use, or sale of property that has been attached in a legal action. It grants authority to the court, judge, or presiding judge of the county court to make orders regarding the property. This includes orders for preservation, use, sale if beneficial due to perishability or cost of keeping, and collection of attached funds or choses in action. A receiver can be appointed for these purposes. Sales must be public after advertisement and on terms of credit with security, as directed by the court. Moneys received by the sheriff from sales or garnishees are to be held and paid under specific requirements.
classification.reasoning: The statute provided details the legal procedures for handling attached property in civil cases. It describes how courts can order the preservation, use, or sale of such property, and the roles of officers like the sheriff and receivers. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906. The confidence is high because the statute's content is purely procedural and lacks any racial element.

classification.title: Bond of defendant to discharge attachment
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process by which a defendant can discharge an attachment on their property by executing a bond to the plaintiff, guaranteeing the performance of the court's judgment. It details the requirements for the bond, its effect on the attachment, and how it can be enforced.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically concerning proceedings upon attachments. Section 221 details the requirements and effects of a defendant's bond to discharge an attachment. This statute deals with civil procedure related to property seizure and does not contain any explicit or implicit racial components. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906 - Attachment Proceedings
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 details procedures related to attachments and garnishments. It outlines how bonds can be executed, how a garnishee can pay money owed to a defendant, and the requirements for a garnishee to appear and answer. It also includes case law annotations related to these procedures.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses legal procedures related to attachments and garnishments in civil and criminal proceedings. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The language and subject matter are purely procedural and legalistic, pertaining to the handling of debts and property in legal disputes. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

§ 225. (1) Actionterminatedas togar-
nishee. When a mere garnishee against
whom no action has been instituted files
an answer denying hisliability,the case
is terminated so far as his rights are to
be affected, and the court has no right

factorydisclosure, the court may hear

the money sought to be recoveredfrom
him belongs to one not a party , the court

after garnishment change his liability
and thereby avoid attachment . Biggs v .

gent- the court should make such orders
in reference thereto as will protect the
rights of the plaintiff, reserving control
of the case for such ordersas may be
necessary. Roberts v. Drinkard, 3 Met .
309; Sheriff v . Buckner , 1 Litt. 126 ;
Keiser v. Shaw , 104 Ky . 119, 20 R. 568 .
(5) Garnishee- how proceeded against.
Where the garnishee in a general attach-
ment has been summoned and appears
and discloses the amount of his indebted-
ness to the defendant , or where he makes
default by failing to appear , and the
court,as itmay do hearsproof as to the
amount of his indebtedness, he may be
ordered to pay the amount into court or
to a receiver,or may be allowed to retain

garnisheefailsto appear, eitherin per-
son or by affidavit,the courtmay coerce

or may hear proof of his indebtedness.
Smith v .Gower , 3 Met . 171.
(6) Judgment against garnishee- effect
of. Judgment against a garnishee is
equivalent to payment to his creditor,
and if the judgment is rendered before
he has noticeof the assignment of his
note by his creditor, it willbe a valid de-
fenseagainstthe assignee, although his
assignment was priorto the judgment.

(7 ) When the obligor in a note having
notice of its assignment is summoned as

party,he willbe liableto the assignee,

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 225
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for garnishment proceedings, specifically detailing how a court can order the delivery of a defendant's property or payment of debts held by a garnishee (a third party who owes money to or holds property of the defendant). It covers situations where the garnishee is an individual or a corporation, and the process for examining the garnishee, ordering payment or delivery of property, and enforcing bonds.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically section 225, which deals with garnishment proceedings. This section describes the legal process by which a creditor can seize debts or property owed to a debtor by a third party (the garnishee). There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The statute is purely procedural and relates to civil debt collection. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Section 229
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the procedures and effects of attachments in legal actions. It details what happens when a judgment is rendered for the defendant (property returned, garnishee dismissed) or for the plaintiff (application of money, proceeds of debts, and sale of property). It also includes case law annotations regarding claimant rights, counter-claims, land sales, appeals, and the order of property sales.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details civil procedure related to attachments and judgments. It does not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race. The text focuses solely on the legal mechanics of property seizure and satisfaction of debts within the legal system. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It details requirements for affidavits before sale, the return of surplus property to the defendant, court compulsion of property delivery or payment of proceeds, and the sheriff's authority to repossess property. It also covers the referencing of multiple attachments on the same property to a commissioner.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The procedures described are standard legal mechanisms for handling seized property in civil litigation and do not appear to be related to Jim Crow laws. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with high confidence, and the category is null.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 237: Action before debt matures - when allowed.
classification.summary: This statute allows for an equitable action for indemnity to be brought before a debt or liability upon a contract becomes due. This can be initiated by a creditor against their debtor, a surety against their principal, or by a jointly liable party against another. The grounds for such an action include the defendant intending to defraud creditors by departing the state and concealing or removing property, or if grounds for attachment exist as mentioned in other sections of the code.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906. Section 237 outlines conditions under which an action can be brought before a debt matures, focusing on financial and legal procedures related to potential fraud or evasion of debt. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The statute deals with civil procedure and financial obligations. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Temporary injunction- cause for.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions under which a temporary injunction can be granted in civil litigation. It specifies that an injunction may be issued if the plaintiff is entitled to relief that involves restraining an act causing irreparable injury, or if the defendant is acting in violation of the plaintiff's rights in a way that would render a future judgment ineffectual. It also notes that injunctions can be granted when specifically authorized by statute and provides examples of such cases. The text further clarifies that injunctions are incidental to the main action and must be obtained in the court where the action is pending, and discusses jurisdiction and specific instances where injunctions have been granted or denied.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the legal process for obtaining a temporary injunction in civil cases. It focuses on the conditions and circumstances under which such injunctions can be granted, such as preventing irreparable harm or rendering a judgment ineffectual. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would inherently or implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is presented as part of a compiled legal code (Civil and Criminal Code 1906).

classification.title: Injunction- When and How Obtained.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures and conditions under which an injunction can be granted in civil and criminal cases. It specifies which officers are authorized to grant injunctions, the requirements for an affidavit from the applicant, and various circumstances under which injunctions may or may not be granted, such as preventing nuisances, prize-fighting, prosecutions under invalid ordinances, and trespass. It also clarifies that the existence of a remedy at law does not preclude an injunction if the legal remedy is inadequate, particularly in cases involving criminal proceedings.

The text focuses on procedural aspects, the authority of different judicial officers, and various legal scenarios where injunctions are applicable. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 274-61
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the procedures and requirements for obtaining injunctions and temporary restraining orders. It details when such orders can be granted by clerks, county judges, or justices, the necessity of filing a petition before seeking an injunction, and the conditions under which judges can issue orders outside their own circuit or county. It also specifies that defendants who have answered must be notified, and that injunctions are generally granted upon reasonable written notice. Exceptions are made for situations where irreparable injury would result from delay, allowing for temporary restraining orders, which may be mandatory in nature but not by clerks, county judges, or two justices. These restraining orders require a bond and prompt service on the enjoined parties.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the legal procedures for obtaining injunctions and temporary restraining orders. It focuses on the administrative and procedural aspects of these legal tools, such as notice requirements, filing petitions, and the roles of different judicial officers. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 286. Disobedience of how punished.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures and limitations for obtaining injunctions to stay legal proceedings and judgments. It details how disobedience of an injunction is punished as contempt and specifies which courts have jurisdiction to grant or hear injunctions related to judgments from various lower courts (justice, quarterly, county, circuit, and court of appeals). It also addresses situations where injunctions can be granted, such as for void judgments or judgments rendered without proper service, and when they cannot be granted, such as when a defense could have been made in the original action.
classification.reasoning: The statute discusses the legal process of injunctions and court jurisdiction. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The statute appears to be a procedural rule of civil law. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Injunction--Dissolution and Reinstatement
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures and consequences for dissolving or modifying injunctions in Kentucky civil cases. It details limitations on motions to dissolve, the assessment of damages upon dissolution (including monetary penalties up to ten percent and compensation for delayed property delivery), and the possibility of superseding a judgment that dissolves an injunction. It also clarifies when judgments dissolving injunctions are considered final and appealable.

The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906. The confidence is high because the content is purely procedural and lacks any racial dimension.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 296-7
classification.summary: These sections of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outline the procedures and timelines for the dissolution, modification, and reinstatement of injunctions. They detail how a plaintiff can seek to reinstate an injunction that has been dissolved or modified, and how a party enjoined can apply to a judge of the Court of Appeals for dissolution or modification. The process involves specific time limits for applications and the filing of orders.

classification.title: Receiver
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions under which a court may appoint a receiver to take charge of property or funds involved in an action. It details the powers of a receiver, who can be appointed, and the requirements for their oath and bond. It also specifies that an order appointing or refusing to appoint a receiver is considered a final order for appeal purposes. The statute includes several case annotations discussing the appointment and powers of receivers in various legal contexts.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 pertains to the appointment and powers of receivers in legal proceedings. It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly discriminates based on race, nor does it relate to any of the typical categories associated with Jim Crow laws (education, housing, marriage, public accommodations, incarceration, voting, labor). Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 299-300
classification.summary: These sections of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outline the conditions under which a receiver can be appointed for mortgaged property. Section 299 details that a receiver may be appointed if the property is in danger of being lost, removed, or injured, or if the mortgage conditions are not met and the property is insufficient to cover the debt. Section 300 specifies who cannot be appointed as a receiver, excluding parties to the action, their attorneys, or interested persons, with exceptions for personal representatives, guardians, curators, and committees of persons of unsound mind. The accompanying annotations discuss various legal interpretations and applications of these sections, including the court's discretion, circumstances for appointment, and liability.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text and its annotations concern the appointment of receivers in civil legal actions, specifically related to mortgaged property. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The statute deals with property law and civil procedure. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code from 1906. The confidence is high because the content is clearly unrelated to racial segregation or discrimination.

classification.title: Deposit in Court
classification.summary: This chapter outlines procedures for depositing money or property into court. It details how courts can order such deposits, enforce these orders through the sheriff, and manage the deposited assets, including placing money in banks or loaning it out. It also clarifies that clerks are not authorized to receive money on replevin bonds, judgments, or executions.

These sections describe how a court can order such deposits, how the sheriff can be involved in managing these assets, and how the funds can be handled (e.g., placed in a bank or loaned). There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The statute appears to be a procedural rule within the civil code. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 310-312
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code defines when an issue of fact arises and how issues of law and fact are tried. It specifies that issues of law are tried by the court, and issues of fact in ordinary actions are also tried by the court unless a jury trial is demanded. It also notes exceptions for injuries to person or character, which the court may try, and references other sections regarding the court's ability to try actions and state law and facts separately.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details the procedural aspects of trials in Kentucky courts, specifically defining what constitutes an 'issue of fact' and how 'issues of law' and 'issues of fact' are to be tried. It outlines the roles of the court and the jury in different types of actions. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute is purely procedural and does not appear to be related to Jim Crow laws. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence score, and the category is null.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

Title IX ] [§ 317Trial- Trial by Jury .
1. Statement of case for plaintiff. The plaintiffmust briefly
statehis claim and the evidenceby which he expectsto sustain
it.
2. Statement for defense. The defendant must then briefly
statehis defenseand the evidencehe expectsto offerin support
of it.
3. Introduction of evidence. The party on whom reststhe
burden of proof,in the whole action,must firstproduce his evi-
dence; the adverse party willthen produce his evidence. (Who
has burden of proof, sec. 526 ; see further as to the order of
proof,sec. 592.)
4. Evidence in rebuttal. The partieswillthen be confinedto
rebutting evidence, unless the court, for good reasons in
furtheranceof justice, permit them to offerevidence in chief.
5. Instructions- kinds of verdict. When the evidence is
concluded, but before the argument to the jury, either party
may require the court to direct the jury to find a separate-
general verdictwith the general verdict [or to find a special
verdict. If a specialverdictbe so required, the questions of
law may be reservedby the court untilafterverdict, but ] if a
general verdictbe required, eitherparty may ask written in-
structionsto the jury on pointsof law ,which shallbe given or
refused by the court before the commencement of the argu-
ment to the jury.
6. Order of argument . The parties may then submit or
argue the case to the jury. In the argument , the party having
the burthen of proof shall have the conclusion and the adverse
party the opening. If therebe more than one speech on either
side, or if severaldefendants having separate defenses appear
by differentcounsel, the court shallarrange the relativeorder
of argument . (Words in brackets strickenout by act 1886 ;
see amd . to sec.327 ; who has burthen of proof,sec.526 ; order
of proof regulatedby court, sec.592.)
15 R. 184; but to prohibit counsel from
arguingcase is error. Behume v. Cald-
well, 2 Bibb 76; Wilken v. Exterkamp ,
102Ky . 142; 19 R. 1132.
(2) It is improperargument foran at-
torneyto go outsideof the evidenceand
thelaw of the case,and when itappears
thatthe improper argument may have
influencedthe jury the court will re-
versefor this cause, but the argument
must be objectedto when it ismade ; if
not the error willbe waived. L. & N.
R. R. v. Smith , 27 R. 257 ; I. C. R. R. v .

113Ky .561,24 R. 375; L. H. & St.Louis
R.R. v.Morgan , 110 Ky .740,23 R. 121;
I. C. R. R. v . Whittaker , 22 R. 395 ; in
Warren v. Nash , 24 R. 479, the court
holds that error in respect to permitting
improper argument may be shown by
affidavits filed with motion for new trial
that are made part of the record.
(3) Pleadings can not be read to the
jury or commented on in argument un-
less introduced as evidence . L. & N. R.
R. v.Hull, 113 Ky . 561,24 R. 375.

CriminalCode,as to improper argument.
(5) Burden of proof- party having is
entitled to conclusion of argument .

INSTRUCTIONS .
(7) Abstract principles of law . It is
error to give instructionswhich contain
mere abstractprinciplesof law; as to
say to the jury "that where thereis a
conflictin the testimony of witnesses,the
one side being of an affirmativeand the
other of a negative character, the affirm-
ativecharacterof testimonyispreferred
and is entitledto the greater weight

(8) After argument begun or after
case has been submitted to jury addi-
tional instructions may be given or an
erroneous instruction corrected . Sears

(9) Assuming certainfacts. Instruc-
tions which assume the existence of cer-
tain factsthat the jury have a rightto
determine,shouldnot be given.Murphy
v. May , 9 Bush 33 ; but an uncontra-
dicted fact may be assumed in an in-
struction. L. & N. R. R. v. Earl,94 Ky .
368, 15 R. 184; and the court may in-
structthe jury that certainmaterialal-
legationsof the pleadingswhich are not

admitted should be pointed out in the

2 Met . 297 .
(10) Burden of proof. It is error to in-
structthe jury as to which party the
burden of proof is on or to use the ex-
pression, "preponderance of evidence."
Ragsdale v. Ezell, 99 Ky . 236, 18 R. 146.
(11) Compensatory damages . See L.
& N. R. R. v. Clark , 105 Ky . 571, 20 R.
1375 ; Canton C. & H. T. P. Co. v . McIn-

R. v . Taaffee . 106 Ky .535 , 21 R. 64 ; L. &

P. Co. v . McIntire , 105 Ky . 185, 20 R.
1107; 101 Ky . 122, 19 R. 308.
(13) Credibility of witnesses. It is
error to give an instructionthat jury
are judges of the credibilityof witnesses
and of the weight to be attached to their
evidence ; but the court will not reverse
on thisground if such an instructionwas
not prejudicial. Transatlantic Ins.Co. v .
Bamberger , 11 R. 101; Peoplesv. Com .,
(14) It is error to instructthe jury
"that if they believeany witness has
sworn falselyand knowingly to any ma-
terialfact, they are bound , or have the
right, to disregardall his testimony."
The jury are the exclusivejudgesof the
testimony. Lettonv.Young,2 Met.558;
Hall v . Renfroe , 3 Met . 52 .
(15) Duty of court. In civilcases the
court is only requiredto give such in-
structionsas are offeredby the parties,
but if an instruction offered is defective
in form or substance the court should
prepare or direct the preparation of a
proper instructionon thispoint, and if

(16 ) Et cetera . In L. & N. R. R. v .

errorto use the expression"etc." in an
instructionfixingthe measure of dam-
ages .
(17) Instructionsmust be in writing
when so requested by either party.
Traders ' Bank v . Henry , 105 Ky . 707; 20
R. 1506; Ferguson v. Fox, 1 Met .83; L.
& N. R. R. v.Banks ,17 R. 1065; Webster
v .Green , 22 R. 1456.
(18) Law to be decidedby the court.
The courtshoulddecideallquestionsof
law, and only submit the factsto the
jury . Maltus v . Shields, 2 Met .553.
(19) Malicedefined- Ohio ValleyTel.
Co. v . Meyer , 22 R. 36 ; Ahrens & Ott
Mfg . Co. v. Hoeher, 106 Ky . 692, 21 R.
299 .
(20) Negligence- gross neglect- ordi-
nary care- ordinarynegligence. Proper
instructionsdefining. L. & N. R. R. v.
McCoy ,81 Ky .403,5 R. 397 ; Needham v.
L. & N. R. R.,85 Ky .423, 9 R. 61; L.&
N. R. R. v.Greer, 16 R. 667; C. & O.R.
R. v .Lang , 100 Ky .221, 19 R. 65 ; L. & N.
R. R. v. Kelly, 100 Ky . 421, 19 R. 69;
100 Κy . 173, 18 R. 693; 105 Ky . 185,20
R. 1107, 23 R. 408 .
(21) Peremptory instructions. When
thereis evidencetending to establisha
matter in issue, the court should not
grant a peremptoryinstruction,although
the judge shouldbe of the opinionthat
if the jury found adversely to the re-
quest for such instruction, he would be
compelledto grant a new trial. Buford

Title IX ] [§ 318Trial- Trial by Jury .
§ 318 [348] View ofpropertyorplaceby jury. Whenever , in the
opinion of the court, it is proper for the jury to have a view of
real property which is the subjectof litigation,or of the place in
which any material fact occurred, it may order them to be con-
ducted in a body , under the charge of an officer, to the place,
which shall be shown to them by some person appointed by the
court for that purpose. While the jury are thus absent no per-
son, other than the person so appointed, shallspeak to them on
any subjectconnected with the trial.
• v . L. & N. R. R. Co.,82 Ky .286,6 R. 263 ;
Curran v.Stein, 110Ky .99,22 R. 1575;
Thompson v. Thompson , 17 B. M. 22;
Payne ClothingCo. v.Payne ,21 R. 1226;
11 Bush 265 ; and after a motion for a
peremptory instructionhas been made
the courtmay allow the plaintiffto in-
troduce other evidence . Ballowe v . Hill-
man , 18 R. 677.
(22 ) When the court gives a peremp-
tory instructionit is the duty of the
jury to make a verdictin accordance
with it. Curran v.Stein, 110 Ky .99,22
R. 1575 .
(23) To authorize an instructionas in
case of a non -suit,itshouldappear that,
admittingthetestintestimonytobe true,and
every inferencethat is fairlydeducible
from it, the plaintiffhas stillfailedto
support his claim. Shay v. R. & L. T.
P. Co., 1 Bush 108.
(24) While it is usual for the defend-
ant , inmoving for a peremptory instruc-
tion, to do so upon the plaintiff'sevi-
dence alone,yet the courtmay ,afterall
the evidencehas been heard upon both
sides,directthe jury to findfor the de-fendant,ifallthe evidenceisinhisfavor.
Wilsey v. L. & N. R. R. Co.,83 Ky . 511,
7 R. 498 .
(25 ) If the court overrules a motion
for peremptory instructionsat conclusion
of plaintiff'stestimony, and afterward
becomes satisfiedthat this was error,
it may , upon conclusionof allthe evi-
dence , set aside former ruling and in-
structjury to findfor defendant.Nance
v . Newport News R. R., 13 R. 554; or
may reservedecisionon motion made at
conclusionofplaintiff'sevidenceuntilallthe evidenceis in. Owens v. Meredith,
25 R. 1485 .
(26 ) The rule is, "that a party may
move upon the evidenceof his antago-
nist, assuming that evidenceand every
fact which itconducesto prove as true,
and ask the court for a peremptory in-
struction; but he can not make such a
motion on his own evidence alone, or on
his evidencemingled with, or added to,
that of the oppositeparty, unless his
own evidenceis composed of record, or
uncontested unofficial documents or writ-
ings." United Shakers v. Underwood , 11
Bush 265.(27) Prominent facts. Instructions
should not be given which give promi-
nence to certainportionsof the evidence,
when the whole of it should have been
leftto be consideredand weighed by the
jury . Flood v . Pragoff, 79 Ky . 607, 3
R. 372; Stokes v. Shippen, 13 Bush 180.
STATEMENT OF CASE .
(28 ) The purpose of a statement is to
enableeach party to presentto the jury
the factsexpectedto be proven,and itis
not proper to discussthe law applicable
to the case or to read from law books .
Hughes v.GeneralElectricCo., 107 Ky .
485 , 21 R. 1202.
§ 318. View of premises. Court may
permit jury to view placewhere an acci-
dent occurred, even afterthey have re-
tiredto considertheirverdict,when the
jury so request. L.,N. A. & C. R. R. v.
Schick, 94 Ky . 191, 14 R. 833 ; and see
Ky .C. R. R. v. Smith,93 Ky .449, 14 R.
455 ; where it is held that where jury
was sent to view premises at closeof
testimony it was not error to refuse to
permit them to go at a particulartime
during trial.
(2 ) In M. & B. S. R. R. v . Trustees, 18
R. 1111, it was held not reversiblethat
jury viewed premises afterdark, it ap-
pearingthatthey couldseewellwith the
aid of lanterns furnished .
(3) Whether or not thejury may view
thepremisesisa matterlargelyin thediscretion of the trial court. H. & C.
Gravel R. v. Cosby, 103 Ky . 182, 19 R.
1851; and itsaction willnot be disturbed
unless the discretion has been abused .
CentralAsylum v.Hauns ,21 R. 22;Green
v .M. & B. S. R. R.,25 R. 1623 ; Valley T.
P. Road v . Lyons , 22 R. 646 ; Todd v ..
Cooke ,23 R. 1528.

§ 319-21] [Title IXTrial- Trial by Jury .
§ 319 [349] Duty ofjuryand officeraftersubmission- jurymay be
kepttogether. When the case is finallysubmittedto the jury,
they may decide in court or retirefor deliberation. If they re-
tire, they must be kept togetherin some convenient place, under
the charge of an officer, until they agree upon a verdict or are
dischargedby the court- subjectto the discretionof the court to
permit them to separatetemporarilyat night and at theirmeals.
The officerhaving them under his charge shall not sufferany
communication to be made to them , nor make any himself, ex-
cept to ask them if they have agreed upon theirverdict, unless
by order of the court; and he shallnot, before their verdictis.
rendered, communicate to any person the stateof theirdelibera-
tions, or the verdictagreed upon .
§ 320 [350] Admonitionby courttojury. If the jury be per-
mitted to separate, eitherduring the trialor after the case is
submitted to them , they must be admonished by the court thatit
is theirduty not to converse with, nor sufferthemselves to be
addressed by, any other person on any subjectof the trial; and
that, during the trial, it is theirduty not to form or express an
opinion thereon, untilthe cause is finallysubmitted to them .
§ 321 [351] Information as to law or evidence aftersubmission- how
given. Afterthejuryhave retiredfordeliberation,iftherebe a
disagreementbetweenthem as toany partof thetestimony,or if
theydesireto be informedas to any pointof law arisingin the
case, they may request the officerto conduct them into court,
where theinformationrequiredshallbe givenin thepresenceof,
or afternoticeto, the partiesor theircounsel.
(4 ) The exhibitionof machinery in a
plant being viewed by the jury in a
manner suggestedby the sheriffis im-
proper and ground for reversal.Hughes
v. GeneralElectricCo., 107 Ky . 485,
21 R. 1202; as to the operationof the
machinery by an interested witness,
Meir v.Weikel ,22 R. 953 .
(5 ) In Board of Internal Imp . v .
Moore ,23 R. 1885, it was held not to be
errorto take jury to court house yard
to view personalproperty.
§ 321. (1) Jury - how informed as to
law or evidence after retiring. After the
jury have been instructed, and have re-
tired for consultation, any information ,
writtenororal,sent tothem by thejudge
is a violation of the Code . Goode v .
Campbell,14 Bush 75.
(2) A jury may be allowedafterretir-
ing to return into court to inquire of a
witness what he had testifiedto, or said,
in any part of his testimony,and cross-examination would not be allowed . In
rarecasesa jury might afterretirement,
be permitted to re-examine a witness
respectinga factnot beforetestifiedto,
where the courtissatisfiedthatprobable
injusticewould otherwisebe done, but

proper. Thompson v.Poston, 1 Duv .389.
(3) After the jury had retired,a wit-
ness who had testifiedwas sent for by
them, admitted into the jury room and
re-examined without the knowledge or
consent of the court or parties; held
sufficientgrounds for a new trial. Lut-
trellv.M. & L. R. R. Co., 18 В. М. 291.
(4) The jury may by permissionof
the court take to their room or examine
a model used as evidence after they have
retired to deliberate . L. & N. R. R. v .
Berry,96 Ky .604,16 R. 722; but inL.H.
& St.LouisR. R. v.Morgan ,110Ky .740,
23 R. 121,the court held it to be rever-
sibleerrorto permitjury to takea dep-
ositionto theirroom over objectionof
adverse party, rulingthat thissection

TitleIX ] [§ 322-5Trial- Trial by Jury .
§ 322 [352] Dischargeof jury- causesfor. The jury may be dis-
chargedby the court on account of the sicknessof a juror, or
otheraccidentor calamity requiringtheirdischarge; or, by con-
sentof both parties, or, afterthey have been kept togetheruntil
itsatisfactorilyappears that there is no probabilityof their
agreeing.
§ 323 [353] Retrialof casesin which jury discharged. Cases in
whichthe jury are dischargedwithoutmaking a verdictshallbe
triedagain at such time as the court may direct.
§ 324 [354] Verdict- mannerofrendering- pollofjury. When the
juryhave agreed upon theirverdict, theirnames shallbe called
bythe clerkand the verdictbe rendered by theirforeman . When
theverdictisannounced ,eitherparty may requirethe jury to be
polled- which isdone by theclerkor courtaskingeach jurorif
itishis verdict. If any one answers in the negative, the jury
must again be sent out for further deliberation.
§ 325 [355] Verdict to be written and signed- clerk to read. The
verdictshallbe written,signedby the foreman, and read by the
clerkto the jury,and the inquiry made , whether or not itistheir
verdict. If any juror disagree, the jury must be sent out for
furtherdeliberation; but if no disagreementbe expressed, and
neitherpartyrequirethejurytobe polled,theverdictiscomplete
and the jury discharged from the case. (Provision when three-
fourthsof jury render,sec.2268,Ky .Stats.)

provisionof the Code that the verdict

classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 details procedures related to jury trials, including the discharge of juries, retrial of cases, and the rendering and acceptance of verdicts. It outlines conditions under which a jury can be discharged (sickness, accident, mutual consent, or inability to agree) and the process for polling the jury to confirm their verdict. It also mentions provisions for when three-fourths of the jury can render a verdict, referencing another section of the Kentucky Statutes.

§ 326 [356] Verdictsdescribedand defined. The verdict of a jury
isgeneralor separate-general [or special].
1. General verdict. A general verdictis that by which the
jurypronouncesgenerally,upon alltheissues,fortheplaintiff
or for the defendant .
2. Separate-general verdict. A separate-general verdictis
the finding, upon any of the issues, in favor of the plaintiffor
of the defendant .

by a jury, as shown in theiranswers to questionssubmitted to

Ky . Stat., sec.2268.
GENERAL VERDICT .
§ 326. (1) Verdict- defectsin plead-

imperfection or omission in any pleading,

(3) Verdict is not a judgment - the

Fuqua v.Mullen,13 Bush 467.
(4 ) Verdict- how construed. When a
question between A and B as to the

jury,andtheverdictisthattheproperty

done as charged in the petition. Baum

rendera verdictfortheplaintiffformore
than nominal damages .Rogers v.Aulick,
2 Duv .419 .
(7) In actionfordamages in a specified
sum , the court could not render judgment
for the amount claimed upon a verdiet

C., O. & S. W. R. R. v. Maddox , 19 R.
966 ; L. & N. R. R. v. Hartwell , 99 Ky .
436 ,18 R. 745.

classification.title: Verdicts described and defined.
classification.summary: This statute defines and describes different types of jury verdicts in legal proceedings: general verdicts, separate-general verdicts, and special verdicts. It also outlines rules regarding the sufficiency of verdicts, the ability of three-fourths of a jury to render a verdict, and how defects in pleadings can be cured by a verdict. It further discusses how verdicts are construed and their implications in various legal scenarios, including cases with absent proof or default judgments.
classification.reasoning: The statute provided defines and explains different types of jury verdicts in legal proceedings. It details the nature of general, separate-general, and special verdicts, along with rules for their validity and interpretation. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it appears to be part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Sections 328-330
classification.summary: These sections of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky detail rules for jury verdicts in civil cases. Section 328 addresses inconsistencies between general and special verdicts, stating the special verdict prevails. Section 329 requires juries to assess the amount of recovery when a general verdict entitles a party to money. Section 330 mandates that in actions for the recovery of specific personal property, the jury must assess the property's value and damages for its detention.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text pertains to civil procedure and jury verdicts in general. It outlines how courts should handle different types of verdicts and the assessment of damages or property value. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of statutes.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Page 194, § 331. Actions in tort - effect of trial by court.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures and effects of a trial by the court in actions for injury to person or character, allowing parties to waive a jury trial with court consent. If a jury trial is not demanded or is waived, the court makes decisions on issues and assesses damages, with its findings having the same effect as a jury's.

The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This statute, section 332 of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, outlines the procedure for trials by the court. It states that the court is not required to separately state its findings of law and fact unless a party requests it to allow for exceptions to decisions on questions of law. In such cases, the court must provide written conclusions of fact separately from conclusions of law.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details procedural rules for trials conducted by a judge rather than a jury. It focuses on the separation of findings of fact and conclusions of law when requested by a party for the purpose of appeal. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

offeror request of the adverse party. (Exceptions to deposi-

§333. (1) Amendment. An exception

(3) Depositions. When an exception

(6) Final orders. Final judgments,
whether upon issuesof law or fact,need

a demurrer to an answer is sustained

fendant, or where a peremptory instruc-

craft v. Duncan , 6 R. 651; Loving v.
Warren Co., 14 Bush 316, 20 R. 112.
Nor is it necessary to except to a void
order. Smith v . Blakeman , 8 Bush 476 .
(7) Instructions- offered by adverse
partymust be objectedto at timeandifobjectionis overruledand instruction
given an exceptionmust be taken at
time; if this is not done any error in
givinginstructionwillbe waived; there
must be both an objection and an ex-
ception; neitherof itselfissufficient. L.
& N. R. R. Co. v. Graves, 78 Ky . 74;
Cox v. Winston, 3 Met . 577; Reed v.
Com ., 7 Bush 641; Garrot v. Ratliff, 83
Ky.384,7 Ky.463; 107Ky.223,21R.
896; 14 Bush 316; 1 Duv. 387; 81 Ky .
51, 4 R. 596; but if the instructionis
givenby the courton itsown motion,no
objectionis necessary; an exceptionis
sufficient; and so if the court instructs
the jury by directionof both parties.
L. S. R. R. v . Hooe , 18 R. 521.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky, specifically Article 4 concerning Exceptions, details the procedural rules for taking exceptions to court decisions. It outlines when objections are necessary, how exceptions should be prepared and recorded, and the conditions under which errors will be disregarded. The text includes case law examples illustrating the application of these rules to various legal situations such as amendments, demurrers, depositions, evidence, final orders, and jury instructions.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically dealing with procedural rules for taking exceptions in court. It outlines the legal process for objecting to court decisions and preserving those objections for appeal. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or effect on any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code. Confidence is high because the content is purely procedural and lacks any racial element.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Section 334
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures and timelines for taking exceptions and preparing bills of exceptions in legal proceedings. It specifies when objections must be made, the time allowed for preparing a bill of exceptions, and conditions under which the time can be extended, such as the absence of the trial judge. It also references court rules and case law regarding the validity of bills of exceptions.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute, Section 334 of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, deals with procedural aspects of legal trials, specifically the taking of exceptions and the preparation of bills of exceptions. It outlines timelines, conditions for extensions, and rules for filing these documents. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute is purely procedural and does not appear to be related to Jim Crow laws. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence score, and the category is null.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title IX, § 335 Trial- Exceptions. Bill of exceptions - how prepared.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedural requirements for preparing and filing a bill of exceptions in Kentucky courts. It details how a bill of exceptions should be prepared, the timing for filing it (including considerations for motions for new trial and special judges), and the consequences of not adhering to these rules. It specifies that a bill of exceptions must be filed within a certain term and that delays without excuse can lead to its refusal.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text from the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky deals exclusively with the procedural aspects of preparing and filing a 'bill of exceptions' in legal proceedings. It specifies rules regarding the form, timing, and filing of these documents, as well as the roles of judges and court terms in this process. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of statutes.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title IX Trial - Exceptions
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the procedures for entering exceptions on the record and preparing bills of exceptions during trials. It details when a bill of exceptions is necessary, how it should be prepared, and the role of bystanders in attesting to the truth of the record when the judge refuses to sign a bill. It also covers the process of making various documents, such as deeds and wills, part of the official record.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details procedural aspects of legal trials, specifically concerning the recording of exceptions and the creation of bills of exceptions. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any other element that would indicate this statute is a Jim Crow law. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for 'is_jim_crow' and 'none' for 'racial_indicator'. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code. The confidence is high because the content is purely procedural.

classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for preparing and filing bills of exceptions in legal trials. It details the requirements for what a bill of exceptions must include, the timeline for its preparation, and the process for handling disagreements between a party and the judge regarding its content, including the use of bystander affidavits. It also states that errors not prejudicial to substantial rights will be disregarded.

There is no mention of race, segregation, or any other element that would indicate this statute is related to Jim Crow laws. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for 'is_jim_crow' with high confidence. The category is null as it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code from 1906. The racial indicator is 'none' as race is not mentioned.

classification.title: New trial defined causes for
classification.summary: This statute defines a new trial as a re-examination of an issue of fact after a jury verdict or court decision. It outlines the grounds for granting a new trial, including irregularities preventing a fair trial, misconduct of jury, party, or attorney, accident or surprise, excessive damages influenced by passion or prejudice, and errors in the assessment of recovery amount. It also references other provisions for new trials and provides case law examples.
classification.reasoning: The statute provided, § 340 of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, defines the grounds for granting a new trial in legal proceedings. It lists general procedural reasons such as irregularities, misconduct, accident, surprise, and errors in damages or recovery amounts. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title IX, Section 340: Trial - New Trial
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the grounds for granting a new trial in legal proceedings. These grounds include verdicts not sustained by evidence or contrary to law, newly discovered evidence, and errors of law occurring at trial. The text also includes case law interpretations regarding what constitutes sufficient grounds for a new trial, such as accident, surprise, or unavoidable absence, and what does not, such as mistake of counsel or lack of diligence.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 and details the procedural grounds for a new trial in legal cases. It discusses legal standards and case law related to evidence, errors, and unforeseen circumstances that might warrant a retrial. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 340
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 pertains to the grounds and procedures for obtaining a new trial in legal cases. It outlines conditions such as accident, surprise, and errors in law or evidence that may warrant a new trial. It also discusses the discretion of the court in granting new trials and the requirements for specifying errors.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses legal procedures related to obtaining a new trial. It details the conditions under which a new trial can be granted, such as accident, surprise, or errors in law or evidence. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest a discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 details grounds for granting a new trial in legal proceedings. It covers issues such as improper arguments by attorneys, irregularities by judges or juries, juror misconduct, juror bias due to relationship or prior service, and the admissibility of juror affidavits. It also addresses situations where a party was misled or prevented from making a defense.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 and discusses general legal procedures related to obtaining a new trial. It outlines various grounds for a new trial, such as improper arguments, jury irregularities, and juror misconduct. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 details the procedural requirements for motions for new trials in Kentucky courts. It outlines the sufficiency of grounds for appeal, the purpose and effect of such motions, and the conditions under which newly discovered evidence can be grounds for a new trial. It specifies that evidence must be newly discovered, important, not cumulative, and that reasonable diligence must have been exercised to discover it prior to trial. Affidavits are required to support claims of newly discovered evidence.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses legal procedures related to motions for new trials and the introduction of newly discovered evidence. It does not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is identified as 'codes' because it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906. The confidence is high because the content is purely procedural and lacks any racial indicators.

§ 341 [370] When not to be granted- two new trials. A new trial
shallnot be granted on account of the smallnessof damages inan
action for an injury to the person or reputation, or in any other
action in which the damages equal the actual pecuniary injury
sustained; nor shallmore than two new trialsbe granted to a
party upon the ground that the verdictis not sustained by the
evidence .
§ 342 [371] Applicationfor- when tobe made . The applicationfor
a new trial must be made at the term in which the verdict or deci-
sion is rendered; and , except for the cause mentioned in section
340, subsection7, shallbe withinthreedays aftertheverdictor
decisionis rendered, unless unavoidably prevented. (Party
granted may be requiredto pay costs,Ky .Stat., 899.)

see notes to secs.331, 332.
(61) Verdict not sustained by evi-
dence . Verdict will not be disturbed
because not sustainedby the evidence,
unlessit isclearlyand palpablyagainst
the weight of it. Thompson v. Thomp-

(62) If the plaintifffailsto set out a
good cause of action, and the defectis
not curedby answer (Fiblev.Caplinger,

no rightto sue, a verdictfor the plain-
tiffshouldbe setaside. Petty v.Malier,
14 В. М. 246 ; and see sec. 386 and notes
thereto as to when judgment may be
rendered against verdict.
§ 341. (1) Smallness of damages .
When inan actionforany injurytotheperson specialdamages are allegedand
proved, and the jury disregardthe evi-
dence and the law and findforthe plain-
tiffonly one cent, a new trialshould be
awarded. This sectiondoes not apply
to the actualpecuniarydamages result-ing directlyfrom the wrong . These
damages can be measured, and the rule
that a new trialshallnot be granted on
account of the smallness of the dam-
ages does not apply. Ray v.Jeffries,86

(2 ) Plaintiffis not entitled to a new

the same party , the court will not dis-
turb the thirdverdict,althoughthe judg-
ments in the first two cases were re-
versed for errorsof law, and the third
verdictisagainstthe weight of evidence.
L. & N. R. R. Co. v.Graves,78 Ky .74;
L. & N. R. R. v.Adams ,10 R. 713; L.&
N. R. R. v.Ballard,88 Ky .159,10 R. 735,
25 R. 15; 25 R. 74. See 28 R. 710.
§ 342. (1) Motion for new trial-
practice. A motion for a new trialmust
be made within three days afterthe
verdict is returned, whether the verdict
be generalor special. ImperialIns.Co.
v.Kiernan,83 Ky .468,7 R. 542; and al-
though a motion for judgment,notwith-
standingverdict,may be pending.Ruhr-
wein v.Gebhart,90 Ky .147,11 R. 969.
(2 ) The requirement that the motion
shall be made within three days means
three judicial days , and the day on
which the verdict or decision is ren-
dered, and the day on which the motion
is made , are both computed. Long v.
Hughes , 1 Duv . 387; White v. Crutcher,
1 Bush 472; Humphrey v. Walton, 2Bush 580. When judgment was ren-
dered on January 24th , a motion and
grounds for a new trialon January 27th

171; Newport News R. R. v . Thomas ,
96 Ky . 613, 16 R. 706 ,21 R. 767 .
(3) When motion is not made in three
days nothing but pleadings and findings
of jury can be consideredon appeal.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title IX, Article 6, Sections 345-346
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically Sections 345 and 346, deals with general provisions related to trials. Section 345 outlines the amount of damages recoverable, stating that a plaintiff can claim and recover any rate of damages to which they are entitled for the established cause of action. It also includes a note on vindictive damages. Section 346 clarifies that the provisions of this title, concerning trials by jury, also apply to trials by the court where nature permits. The text also includes references to other sections and case law regarding new trials and limitations.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses general legal procedures related to damages and trials. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The content is purely procedural and related to civil law. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906 - Article 7: Time of Trial
classification.summary: This article outlines the procedures for managing court dockets and scheduling trials in Kentucky courts. It details the creation and use of three dockets: the common docket for ordinary actions, the equity docket for equitable actions, and the motion docket for motions. It specifies how cases are entered, arranged, and when they stand for trial, including provisions for jury trials and the trial of issues of law in equity cases.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the administrative procedures for managing court dockets and scheduling trials. It does not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race. The focus is solely on the organization and timing of legal proceedings. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 368
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 provides notes and definitions related to judgments in legal proceedings. It covers topics such as when a party is required to bring money to court, the inability to assail erroneous judgments in collateral proceedings, the definition of a final order, the attackability of foreign judgments, the computation of interest on judgments, what a judgment should state, judgments against parties in trust or as heirs, the binding effect of judgments on parties and privies, judgments obtained by fraud, nunc pro tunc judgments, the court's power to amend records, the admissibility of parol evidence, personal judgments, and the concept of res judicata.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 and discusses general legal principles regarding judgments. It does not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly relate to racial segregation, discrimination, or the enforcement of Jim Crow policies. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a confidence of 1.0. The category is null as it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code. The racial indicator is 'none' as there is no mention or implication of race.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically sections 369 and 370, deals with general principles of judgments in legal proceedings. It outlines how judgments can be rendered for or against one or more parties in a lawsuit, and how an action can proceed against some defendants while a judgment is rendered against others. It also discusses the conditions under which a judgment acts as a bar to future actions, emphasizing that judgments based on technical defects or lack of jurisdiction do not preclude future suits. The text includes case law citations to illustrate these points.
classification.reasoning: The provided text from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 discusses general legal procedures regarding judgments in civil and criminal cases. It focuses on the technical aspects of how lawsuits can proceed and how judgments are rendered. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Dismissal of action without prejudice
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions under which a legal action can be dismissed without prejudice, meaning it can be refiled later. Dismissal can be initiated by the plaintiff before the final submission of the case, or by the court under specific circumstances such as the plaintiff's failure to appear, lack of necessary parties, failure to prosecute with diligence, or disobedience of court orders. The statute also clarifies that counterclaims or set-offs are not affected by dismissal, and that a dismissal agreed upon by the parties acts as a bar to future recovery on the same cause of action.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural rules for dismissing legal actions in Kentucky civil and criminal courts. It outlines the circumstances under which a plaintiff or the court can dismiss a case without prejudice. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest differential treatment based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906. The confidence is high because the text is purely procedural and lacks any racial component. The category is null because it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically page 222, discusses judgments in general, focusing on dismissals of actions and when such dismissals act as a bar to future actions. It details various scenarios, such as dismissals without prejudice, dismissals due to technical defects, lack of jurisdiction, or temporary disability of the plaintiff. It also covers situations where a dismissal might not prejudice other parties' rights, like in actions involving multiple creditors or specific property recovery. The text includes case citations to illustrate these legal principles.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines rules regarding judgments in legal proceedings, specifically addressing the trial of set-offs or counter-claims, judgments against part of defendants, and related procedural aspects. It clarifies that a defendant is entitled to a trial on a set-off or counter-claim even if the plaintiff dismisses the action or fails to appear. It also states that judgment can be rendered against any of several defendants if the plaintiff would have been entitled to judgment against them individually. The text includes case law annotations that further elaborate on these principles, such as the plaintiff's right to dismiss an action and the conditions under which a set-off or counter-claim can still be tried.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses procedural rules for civil and criminal judgments, specifically concerning set-offs and counter-claims. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The statute deals with general legal procedures applicable to all litigants. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky deals with judgments related to mortgages and liens. It outlines procedures for enforcing liens, foreclosing mortgages (though it states foreclosure is forbidden, which is clarified by definitions), and rendering personal judgments against defendants. It also covers the set-off of judgments and the rights of mortgagees.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky. The statutes discussed (sections 374-377) pertain to civil procedures regarding mortgages and liens. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The content is purely procedural and financial in nature. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Page 225
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically sections 378-80, deals with judgments upon failure to plead. It outlines procedures for enjoining the collection of judgments to prevent loss due to insolvency or non-residence, and for ascertaining facts (like taking accounts, proving facts, or assessing damages) when a party fails to plead. It also allows for judgment to be rendered for uncontroverted parts of a claim.
classification.reasoning: The provided text from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 pertains to civil procedure regarding judgments and pleadings. It outlines legal processes for handling cases where a party fails to respond or when parts of a claim are not disputed. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of statutes.

classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the procedures for rendering and entering judgments in legal cases. It details how damages are awarded, what information must be included in a judgment entry, and references various legal precedents and statutes related to judgment execution, amendment, and enforcement. The text focuses on the mechanics of legal judgments rather than specific substantive law.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically sections 389 and 390, which deal with the manner of giving and entering judgments. These sections describe legal procedures for awarding damages, specifying judgment details, and referencing case law. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The content is purely procedural and relates to the administration of justice in civil and criminal matters, not to the enforcement of discriminatory practices. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Infant other than married woman may have new trial - when.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions under which an infant, excluding a married woman, can seek a new trial for a judgment rendered against them. It specifies a twelve-month period after reaching the age of twenty-one to show cause against a judgment, with exceptions for torts, necessaries, or judgments on set-offs/counter-claims. It also notes that vacating such a judgment does not affect the title of a bona fide purchaser.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with legal procedures related to judgments against infants. It does not contain any explicit or implicit references to race or racial discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906.

classification.title: Conveyance by Commissioners
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the procedures for conveying real property through a commissioner appointed by the court. It details when such a conveyance can be made, including when a judgment orders it or after a sale is confirmed. It also specifies that the death of a party after a sale does not prevent the conveyance, which will have the same effect as if the party were alive. The text also references other sections and case law related to judgments and conveyances.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the legal process for conveying real property through a court-appointed commissioner. It covers the conditions under which such a conveyance can occur, the effect of a party's death on the process, and references to other legal procedures. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws from 1906.

classification.title: Revivor of Judgments

It also details when executions may be issued upon a judgment, even after the death of a party, and the conditions under which such executions can be quashed or enjoined.

It details legal mechanisms for transferring property titles and enforcing court orders. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The language and subject matter are purely procedural and legal, common to civil codes of the era. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Actions Against Absent and Unknown Defendants
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the legal procedures for actions against defendants who are absent or unknown. It details how to serve notice, the conditions under which a petition can be taken as true, the requirement for a bond before judgment, and how the court controls property if a bond is not given. It also covers examination of the plaintiff, set-offs, new trials, service of judgments, and limitations on personal judgments.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details procedural aspects of civil law concerning absent and unknown defendants. It does not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race, nor does it relate to any of the typical categories associated with Jim Crow laws (education, housing, marriage, public accommodations, incarceration, voting, labor). Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a confidence of 1.0, and the category is null. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws. The racial indicator is 'none'.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 408-9
classification.summary: These sections of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outline procedures for actions against absent and unknown defendants. Section 408 details the time allowed for a constructively summoned defendant to appear and defend, and the possibility of granting time to prepare for trial. Section 409 explains when a plaintiff's allegations in an action against a constructively summoned defendant will be taken as true if not denied by the defendant's answer, provided the plaintiff files an affidavit stating certain allegations are known to the defendant and cannot be proven otherwise. The text also includes annotations and case law related to these sections, covering aspects like compliance with constructive service laws, corporate domiciles, defense by non-residents, and proof requirements for written instruments.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text, sections 408 and 409 of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, deals with legal procedures concerning absent and unknown defendants. It outlines how such defendants can be notified, appear in court, and how allegations against them are handled. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. The procedures described are general legal mechanisms applicable to any defendant, regardless of race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Actions Against Absent and Unknown Defendants
classification.summary: This statute outlines the requirements for judgments against defendants who are constructively summoned and have not appeared. It mandates that a bond be executed by the plaintiff before a judgment is rendered, ensuring that any property or money obtained under the judgment can be restored to the defendant if the judgment is later vacated or modified. The statute also clarifies what constitutes an appearance by a non-resident defendant, including filing an answer, controverting an attachment, or prosecuting an appeal. It specifies exceptions, such as when the Commonwealth is a party plaintiff and the beneficial owner of the demand.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with civil procedure concerning absent and unknown defendants. It establishes a legal safeguard (a bond) to protect defendants who are not personally served and do not appear in court. There is no mention of race, and the procedures described are general legal principles applicable to any defendant, regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines procedures for actions against absent and unknown defendants. It details how courts can control property if a bond is not given, allows for plaintiff examination regarding their claim, and specifies that disclosed set-offs or counter-claims may be allowed. It also addresses the time allowed for a new trial, with exceptions for divorce cases, and discusses appeal rights for defendants constructively summoned.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text deals with legal procedures for handling cases where defendants are absent or unknown. It outlines court processes, property control, and defendant rights regarding new trials and appeals. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws.

classification.title: Actions Against Absent and Unknown Defendants
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures and rights of absent or unknown defendants in legal actions. It allows such defendants to move for a retrial within five years of a judgment, provided they give security for costs. Upon retrial, the court can confirm, modify, or set aside the original judgment and order the restoration of any money or property exchanged under it. This section does not apply to divorce judgments concerning the divorce itself. The statute also details the process for the Commonwealth to restore money received under a modified or set-aside judgment and specifies the time limits and conditions for defendants to seek a new trial after a judgment is served.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with procedural aspects of legal actions involving absent or unknown defendants. It provides a mechanism for these defendants to challenge judgments against them. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute appears to be a general procedural rule applicable to all defendants, regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically sections 416-419, deals with actions against absent and unknown defendants. It outlines the procedures for serving copies of judgments, how the title of property is affected by new trials, the creation of liens through attachment or judgment, and prohibits personal judgments against defendants who are constructively summoned and have not appeared. The accompanying case law further clarifies the nuances of good faith purchasers and the necessity of attachments for jurisdiction.

classification.title: Alimony and Divorce
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the legal procedures and requirements for actions related to alimony and divorce. It specifies that such actions are to be considered equitable, details the process for verification of pleadings and interrogatories, and mandates that statements in a divorce petition must be proven, including the residence of the parties. It also covers maintenance during an action, restoration of property, and annulment of divorce judgments.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky, 1906, specifically Title X, Chapter II, concerning Alimony and Divorce. The content of the statutes (§§ 420-427) details the legal framework for divorce and alimony proceedings. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The procedures described are standard legal processes for divorce and alimony, applicable to all citizens regardless of race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: These sections of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outline the procedures for settling the estates of deceased individuals. Specifically, § 429-30 details the requirements for a petition to sell real estate to cover debts when the personal estate is insufficient, including stating the amount and nature of debts and property. It also mandates notice to creditors to prove their claims before a commissioner, with publication requirements. The accompanying notes discuss related legal precedents concerning attorney fees in estate settlements and the rights of creditors to contest claims.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text (§ 429-30) and its accompanying notes deal with the legal procedures for settling the estates of deceased individuals. The content focuses on the financial and procedural aspects of distributing assets and paying debts. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code from 1906.
