classification.title: § 437 Settlement of Estates
classification.summary: This statute, part of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, outlines the requirements for creditors to make a demand and file an affidavit when settling a decedent's estate. It specifies exceptions to these requirements, such as when the personal representative is a non-resident or absent. The statute also details the sufficiency of affidavits, the proof needed for different types of claims (accounts vs. notes), and situations where demand and affidavit are not necessary. It explicitly states that the verification requirements do not apply to the Commonwealth.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the procedural requirements for settling estates and the verification of claims against them. It does not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is presented as part of a compiled code of statutes. The confidence is high because the text clearly focuses on legal procedures for estate settlement and lacks any racial component.

classification.title: Enforcing the Satisfaction of Judgments
classification.summary: This statute outlines the legal procedures for enforcing the satisfaction of judgments when a writ of execution (fieri facias) has been returned with no property found. It allows a plaintiff to initiate an equitable action to discover and subject any money, choses in action, or equitable or legal interests the defendant may possess. The statute also permits making parties defendants who are indebted to the defendant or hold property in which the defendant has an interest. It includes provisions for compelling the surrender of property and references related legal concepts and prior case law.
classification.reasoning: The statute provided details the legal process for enforcing judgments when a debtor has no visible property. It is a procedural statute related to civil law and debt collection. There is no mention of race, and the procedures described are general legal mechanisms applicable to any judgment debtor. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906. Confidence is high because the text is purely procedural and lacks any racial indicators.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 439
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically § 439, pertains to the enforcement of judgments. It outlines various legal procedures and conditions related to satisfying judgments, including actions upon return of no property, attachment of a decedent's estate, proper execution of legal documents, and proceedings against garnishees. It also details the requirements for petitions and the types of property that can be attached.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 and details legal procedures for enforcing judgments. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The content is purely procedural and relates to civil law. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Enforcing the Satisfaction of Judgments
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines procedures for enforcing judgments, including requirements for verified answers, allowing attachments without affidavit or bond in certain cases, creating liens on defendant's property through attachment or summons, and compelling the surrender of property by court order, with provisions for contempt of court.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details legal procedures for enforcing judgments, such as requiring verified answers, allowing attachments, and creating liens on property. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. The language and subject matter are purely procedural and civil in nature. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Summary Proceedings
classification.summary: This chapter outlines procedures for obtaining judgments on motion in various civil and legal contexts, including actions by sureties against principals, clients against attorneys, parties against sureties for costs, and parties against officers for money collected or property received. It details notice requirements and when motions are considered abandoned. The provided annotations clarify specific applications and limitations of these summary proceedings, such as their inapplicability to appeal bonds or certain surety bonds, and their use in recovering rent from land purchasers.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text and annotations describe procedural mechanisms for obtaining judgments through motions in civil and criminal cases. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The focus is entirely on legal procedures and the relationships between parties in legal actions. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Arbitrations and Awards
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the procedures and legal standing of arbitration and awards. It details how submissions to arbitration are made, the binding nature of awards, the process for appointing arbitrators, their powers and duties (including administering oaths and making written awards), and how awards are entered as court judgments. It also covers the court's power over awards on equitable principles and the arbitration of minor controversies within a justice of the peace's jurisdiction, including appeals.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text, found on page 256 of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code, deals exclusively with the legal framework for arbitration and awards. It outlines the procedural aspects of submitting disputes to arbitration, the roles and responsibilities of arbitrators, and the process by which their decisions become legally binding judgments. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory application based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

The statutes cover situations where a tenant refuses to vacate after their term expires, or when entry is made without consent.

The confidence is high because the text is clear and directly addresses property disputes without any racial implications.

It emphasizes that the core issue is actual possession at the time of entry, not the right of possession or title.

It also includes provisions for trial by judge if a jury is not demanded and discusses the requirements for the warrant's content and execution, referencing case law.

classification.title: Forcible Entry and Detainer
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for filing a traverse (an appeal or objection) against a jury's finding in a forcible entry and detainer case. It details the timeline for filing the traverse, the required bond with surety, and the process for staying proceedings and returning papers to the circuit court. It also specifies damages recoverable on the traverse bond if the traverser fails to prosecute the case successfully. Several case citations are provided to clarify specific aspects of the procedure.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the legal process for handling disputes related to forcible entry and detainer. It focuses on procedural aspects like filing a traverse, providing a bond, and the subsequent legal steps. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute appears to be a general procedural law applicable to all individuals involved in such legal disputes. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Caveats
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the process for entering a caveat to prevent the issuance of a land grant when another person claims a better right to the land. It details who can enter a caveat, the required statement and verification, the filing of a copy with the circuit clerk, and the subsequent legal proceedings, including summons, practice, judgment, and the prohibition of grant issuance until the caveat is disposed of. It also specifies when a plaintiff is not entitled to a caveat and that the court may require security for costs.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the legal process for filing a 'caveat' in land disputes. A caveat is a formal notice filed to prevent a legal proceeding or the issuance of a grant until a dispute is resolved. The text focuses entirely on the procedural aspects of land claims and does not contain any language, explicit or implicit, that suggests racial discrimination or segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of statutes from 1906. The confidence is high because the content is clearly about land law procedures and lacks any racial element. No human review is needed.

classification.title: Caveats
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures and requirements for filing and prosecuting a caveat, which is a legal objection to the granting of land. It details how a caveat is treated as a petition, the conditions for its dismissal, the requirements for submitting judgments to the land office, and the circumstances under which a grant will not be issued until the caveat is resolved. It also specifies when a plaintiff is not entitled to file another caveat and allows the court to require security for costs.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text (Section 473, Title X) details the legal process for filing and managing 'caveats' related to land grants. A caveat is a formal objection to a land grant. The text describes procedural aspects such as treating a caveat as a petition, summons, judgment submission, and the issuance of land grants. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would disproportionately affect a specific racial group. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it appears to be part of a compiled legal code from 1906. The confidence is high because the text is purely procedural and lacks any racial indicators. Human review is not needed.

classification.title: Writs of Mandamus and Prohibition
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for obtaining and prosecuting writs of mandamus and prohibition in Kentucky courts. It defines mandamus as a statutory writ, not a prerogative one, and details how it can be used to compel the performance of plain duties, but not to control judicial discretion or compel a court to act against its judgment. It also specifies that prohibition is a writ to prevent a judicial tribunal from exceeding its jurisdiction. The text includes case law examples illustrating the application of these writs.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the legal procedures for obtaining writs of mandamus and prohibition. These writs are procedural tools used in courts to compel or restrain actions. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any discriminatory practices within the text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it appears to be part of a compiled legal code from 1906. The confidence is high because the content is purely procedural and lacks any racial element.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 details the application and conditions for writs of mandamus and prohibition. It outlines when these writs can be granted or denied, specifying the legal duties of officers and entities, and providing numerous case examples to illustrate their application in various legal scenarios, such as compelling the recording of deeds, levying taxes, issuing warrants, and electing officers. It also clarifies situations where injunctions cannot override a mandamus order and when interpleader is not permitted.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 and discusses legal procedures related to writs of mandamus and prohibition. It details the circumstances under which these writs can be issued or denied, focusing on the legal duties of various public officials and entities. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the procedures and definitions for writs of mandamus and prohibition. It details when these writs can be granted, the nature of temporary orders, the definition of mandamus as a command to an executive or ministerial officer to perform or omit an act enjoined by law, and the right of the Commonwealth to appeal without security. It also defines prohibition and includes case notes related to the application and limitations of these writs.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details legal procedures for writs of mandamus and prohibition. These are general legal remedies and do not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The text focuses on the legal framework for compelling or preventing official actions. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of statutes.

classification.title: Writs of Mandamus and Prohibition
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the conditions under which writs of mandamus and prohibition can be issued. It details when these writs are appropriate to prevent inferior courts from exceeding their jurisdiction or to address situations where an appeal is not an adequate remedy. It also clarifies that these writs are directed at judicial tribunals, not legislative bodies.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses legal procedures related to writs of mandamus and prohibition. It focuses on the jurisdiction of courts and the proper use of these legal instruments. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code from 1906. The confidence is high because the text clearly pertains to legal procedure and lacks any racial component.

classification.title: Repealing or Vacating Charters, Etc.
classification.summary: This chapter outlines the legal procedures for bringing ordinary actions to repeal or vacate charters and to prevent the usurpation of an office or franchise. It details who can institute such actions, including the Attorney-General and Commonwealth attorneys, and specifies when legislative direction is necessary for municipal corporations, banks, and other companies. It also addresses the judgment to be rendered against a usurper and the recovery of fees received by them.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details legal procedures for repealing charters and addressing the usurpation of offices or franchises. It outlines the roles of legal officials and the necessary steps for initiating such actions. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it appears to be part of a compiled legal code from 1906. The category is 'other' as the statute's subject matter does not fit into the specific Jim Crow categories provided.

monwealth shall be institutedand prosecuted by the Attorney-

§ 486 [535] Personholdingoffice,when a usurper. A person who
continues to exercise an officeafter having committed an act, or
omitted to do an act,the commission or omission of which ,by law,
creates a forfeitureof his office, may be proceeded against for
usurpation thereof.
§ 487 [536] Usurper- judgment tobe renderedagainst. A person
adjudged to have usurped an officeor franchiseshallbe deprived
thereof by the judgment of the court, and the person adjudged
entitledtheretoshallbe placed in possessionthereof; but no one
shallbe adjudged entitledthereto, unless the actionbe instituted
by him . And the court shallhave power to enforceitsjudgment
by causing the books and papers, and allother things pertaining
to the officeor franchise,to be surrenderedby the usurper;and
by preventing him from furtherexercisingor using the same ;and
may enforce its orders by fineand imprisonment until obeyed.
(As to penalty againstusurper,see sec.1364,Ky .Stat.)

er have received fees and emoluments arising from the officeor

who may claim the same in the actionbrought to deprive him of
the officeor franchise, or in a separateaction. If no one be en-
titledto them , they may be recoveredby the Commonwealth , and
shall be paid into the public treasury.

classification.title: Usurper of office or franchise - action against.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the legal process for addressing individuals who unlawfully occupy a public office or franchise. It details who can initiate an action (the entitled person or the Commonwealth), the roles of Commonwealth attorneys and the Attorney-General in instituting such actions, and the consequences for a person adjudged to have usurped an office, including deprivation of the office, recovery of fees, and potential fines or imprisonment. It also clarifies when a person holding office can be considered a usurper.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses the legal procedures for dealing with individuals who unlawfully hold public office or franchises. It defines the process for initiating legal action, the responsibilities of legal officials, and the penalties for usurpation. There is no mention of race, nor any implicit racial implications in the text. The statute is part of a legal code, as indicated by the section numbering and the reference to 'Civil and Criminal Code 1906.pdf'. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Sale of real estate of infants and persons of unsound mind
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions under which the real estate of infants and persons of unsound mind can be sold by order of a court of equity. These conditions include paying debts of an ancestor or devisor, paying the individual's own debts, for the maintenance and education of a ward, for the maintenance of a lunatic and their family, or for reinvestment in other property. It also references related sections and case law.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the legal procedures for selling the property of individuals who are legally incapacitated (infants or those of unsound mind). There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The statute is purely procedural and related to property law for vulnerable individuals. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 provides notes and case law related to sales of real property, particularly concerning infants and persons under disability. It details legal procedures, requirements for sales, reinvestment of proceeds, and the powers of courts in such matters. The notes cover various scenarios like sales for support, improvements, mortgages, and settlements of estates, citing numerous Kentucky court cases.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically dealing with the legal procedures for the sale of real property belonging to infants and individuals under disability. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The focus is entirely on legal protections and procedures for vulnerable individuals. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Jointowners- real estate of may be sold.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions under which real estate jointly owned by two or more persons can be sold by order of a court of equity. It applies when shares are worth less than $100 each, or when the property is in possession and indivisible without impairing its value. It also details procedural aspects related to infant's real estate sales, assignee actions, and dower interests.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the legal process of selling jointly owned real estate, particularly when dealing with minors or indivisible property. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, page 278
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically page 278, details legal procedures and precedents related to the sale of real property, particularly concerning joint ownership, partitions, and the rights of various parties such as life tenants, remaindermen, infants, and lunatics. It outlines conditions under which property can be sold, the jurisdiction for such actions, and the legal requirements for sales, including the necessity of possession and the valuation of shares. Several case citations are provided to illustrate specific legal interpretations.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, which is a compilation of statutes. The content of page 278 specifically addresses legal procedures for the sale of real property in cases of joint ownership, partition, and the rights of various legal entities like life tenants, remaindermen, infants, and lunatics. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code. Confidence is high because the text is purely procedural and property-law related without any racial implications.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Section 491-2
classification.summary: This statute outlines procedures for the sale of real property in cases involving particular estates, reversions, and remainders, particularly when infants or individuals of unsound mind are involved. It details conditions under which such sales can occur, the reinvestment of proceeds, and the necessity of court decrees. It also notes that certain provisions were deemed unconstitutional if they allowed the sale of property against the wishes of a fee owner not under disability.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text (Sections 491 and 492 of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906) deals with the legal procedures for selling real property in specific situations involving life estates, reversions, remainders, and parties who are infants or of unsound mind. The text focuses on property law and civil procedure. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code. The confidence is high because the text is clear and does not contain any racial elements.

Specifically, it addresses the sale of land subject to a woman's dower right, allowing for compensation from the sale proceeds with or without her consent depending on her mental state and marital status.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Section 496-497
classification.summary: These sections of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky deal with the sale of real property, specifically addressing the rights and protections of women, infants, and persons of unsound mind. Section 496 outlines conditions under which a bond or privy examination is not required for sales, and when a sale can be ordered even if one joint owner does not consent. Section 497 details how purchase money for the shares of infants or persons of unsound mind should be handled, remaining a lien on the land until certain conditions are met, and specifies that proceeds received by a married woman are her separate property.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text from the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky pertains to the legal procedures for the sale of real property, particularly concerning the rights of women, infants, and individuals with unsound minds. It details financial arrangements, liens, and the handling of proceeds from such sales. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The statute's focus is on property law and the protection of vulnerable parties in legal transactions. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It details the appointment of commissioners, their duties in surveying and allotting land, the process of confirming or setting aside their reports, the execution of deeds, and the recording of these documents. It also covers contesting allegations, trial procedures, removal of cases between courts, and appeals. Case law annotations provide further clarification on specific aspects of land division and dower allotment.

It allows courts of equity to divide land when it is held under a deed or will that vests a life estate in multiple individuals or a trust for their benefit, with a remainder to their children or descendants. The statute also addresses situations where part of the land is located in another state, allowing the court to consider the total value and set apart land accordingly.

classification.title: Revivor of Actions
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for reviving legal actions when a party dies or loses their power to act during the course of a lawsuit. It details how an action can proceed with remaining parties, when judgments can be rendered between them, and how to revive an action if the right to sue or defend passes to a new party. It also includes case law interpretations of the term 'survives' and procedural aspects of revivor orders.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text deals with the procedural aspects of reviving legal actions after the death or cessation of power of a party. It is a general rule of civil procedure and does not contain any language or provisions that suggest racial discrimination or segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906. The confidence is high because the content is purely procedural and lacks any racial indicators.

classification.title: Proceedings to Reverse and Vacate Judgments
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the procedures for reversing, vacating, or modifying judgments rendered in the circuit court. It details the roles of the court rendering the judgment and the Court of Appeals, the grounds for such actions (errors in the record, clerical misprisions), and the procedural steps involved, including appeals and motions. It defines clerical misprision and specifies conditions under which judgments can be vacated or modified.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text pertains to legal procedures for reversing, vacating, or modifying court judgments. It outlines the process for appeals and the correction of clerical errors. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of statutes from 1906. The confidence is high because the content is purely procedural and lacks any racial element.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 517
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines grounds for rendering a judgment prematurely or against a person under disability. It details specific instances of 'misprision' (clerical errors or mistakes) that can occur in the legal process, such as rendering judgment before an action is ready for trial, against infants or persons of unsound mind without proper defense, or errors in the calculation of interest or inclusion of claims. It also covers situations where a judgment might be considered void due to lack of notice and the process for correcting clerical errors.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details procedural rules for rendering judgments in civil and criminal cases. It focuses on preventing premature judgments and ensuring proper legal procedures are followed, especially concerning individuals under disability. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Modification or vacation of judgment after term by court rendering.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions under which a court can vacate or modify a judgment after the term in which it was rendered. These conditions include granting a new trial for specific causes, vacating judgments against constructively summoned defendants, correcting clerical misprisions, addressing fraud by the successful party, rectifying erroneous proceedings against persons under disability (except coverture), and for unavoidable casualty or misfortune preventing a party from appearing or defending. It also addresses errors in judgments against infants.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with civil procedure regarding the modification and vacation of judgments. It lists general legal grounds for such actions, such as clerical errors, fraud, or unavoidable misfortune. There is no mention of race, and the provisions apply universally to all litigants. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 518
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines grounds and procedures for vacating or modifying judgments. It details various circumstances under which a judgment might be set aside, such as a party's inability to attend court due to illness or misfortune, the discovery of new evidence, or the defendant being of unsound mind. It also references related sections for clerical misprisions, common law judgments, and appeals.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses legal procedures for vacating judgments in civil and criminal cases. It outlines conditions such as illness of counsel, misfortune, or a party being of unsound mind as potential grounds for a new trial or modification of a judgment. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 pertains to proceedings to reverse and vacate judgments. It outlines various rules and conditions under which judgments can be attacked, vacated, or modified, including provisions related to judicial sales, limitations, married women's judgments, new trials, non-residents, and the court's power over its judgments after the term has expired. It also addresses the vacation of judgments after an appeal and the resistance of void judgments.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically dealing with legal procedures for reversing and vacating judgments. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The content is purely procedural and legalistic, focusing on the mechanics of the court system. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It also specifies conditions under which a judgment will not be vacated, such as the absence of a valid defense.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title XIII: Evidence - By Whom to be Produced
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the rules for the production of evidence in legal proceedings. It specifies that the party holding the affirmative of an issue must produce the evidence to prove it and that the burden of proof lies on the party who would be defeated if no evidence were given on either side. It also includes case law annotations related to the burden of proof in various legal contexts, such as alterations of notes, assault and battery pleas, contributory negligence, and claimant's bonds.

It details which party is responsible for presenting evidence in cases involving counter-claims, damages, denial of ownership, false imprisonment, fraud, property damage, infancy, railroad stock killing, legal presumptions, libel, malicious prosecution, negligence, and lack of consideration. It also notes that courts take judicial notice of official signatures and certain legal presumptions like death after seven years of absence.

classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically § 527, outlines rules regarding the genuineness of writings submitted as evidence in legal proceedings. It details when a writing can be read as genuine against a party unless its genuineness is denied by affidavit before trial. It also references other sections of the Kentucky Statutes related to exhibits, the definition of 'writing,' and how to make them part of the record. The subsequent subsections provide case law examples and explanations regarding the burden of proof in various legal scenarios, such as non est factum, payment, possession of notes, and settlements of fiduciaries.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the procedures for issuing and executing subpoenas, which are legal writs used to compel the attendance of witnesses in court or before a commissioner. It defines a subpoena and a subpoena duces tecum, details who can issue them, how they are executed, and the consequences for non-compliance, including punishment for contempt and potential liability for costs and damages. It also covers warrants of arrest to compel attendance and the production of prisoners for examination.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the legal process for compelling witness testimony through subpoenas. It outlines the mechanics of issuing, serving, and enforcing these legal documents. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The procedures described are standard legal mechanisms applicable to all individuals within the jurisdiction. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the procedures for issuing and executing subpoenas for witnesses in legal proceedings. It details who can issue subpoenas, how they are served, when a witness is not bound to appear, and the consequences of disobedience, including contempt of court and liability for costs and damages. It also describes the process of issuing a warrant of arrest to compel a witness's attendance.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the legal procedures for issuing and enforcing subpoenas for witnesses in civil and criminal cases. It covers aspects like who can issue a subpoena, how it should be served, conditions under which a witness is not obligated to appear, and penalties for non-compliance. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of differential treatment based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines procedures and punishments related to evidence production, contempt of court, and the handling of prisoners for oral examinations. It details how warrants for witnesses are issued, the penalties for contempt (fines and imprisonment), and the conditions under which a witness refusing to testify will be held. It also specifies how prisoners can be brought for examination and the legal protections afforded to witnesses regarding lawsuits outside their county of residence.

It does not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race. The focus is on the mechanics of legal proceedings and the enforcement of court orders. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Affidavit
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures and requirements for making and using affidavits in legal proceedings within Kentucky. It details where affidavits can be made (within or out of state), who can make them (parties, agents, attorneys), and specific rules for multiple parties and warning orders. It also touches upon the use of affidavits in motions for provisional remedies and alimony, and their inadmissibility in final equity trials.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses the legal process of affidavits, including who can make them, where they can be made, and their use in various legal motions. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute appears to be a procedural rule of general application within the legal system. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky details the rules and procedures for making and using affidavits and depositions in legal proceedings. It covers who can make affidavits (parties, agents, attorneys), what information their affidavits must contain, how affidavits must be subscribed and certified, and when depositions can be read as evidence. It also outlines the process for taking depositions, including who is authorized to take them, the manner of taking them, and exceptions to their use.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details procedural rules for affidavits and depositions within the Kentucky Civil and Criminal Code of 1906. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. The content is purely procedural and relates to the admissibility of evidence in legal cases. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the rules and conditions under which depositions can be used in legal proceedings. It specifies when depositions must be used, when they can be retaken, and the circumstances under which a witness's deposition may be read in court, such as the witness residing more than twenty miles away, being absent from the state, being deceased, or being unable to attend due to illness or imprisonment. It also touches upon the use of certified copies of depositions and the discretion of the court in allowing oral testimony.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details procedural rules for the use of depositions in legal cases. It does not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race. The rules apply generally to all parties and witnesses in legal proceedings. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of statutes.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title XIII, Officers Authorized to Take Depositions
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code outlines the procedures and conditions under which witnesses can be compelled to attend court for oral examination or have their depositions taken. It details when depositions can be taken, who is authorized to take them (examiners appointed by circuit judges), and the requirements for their use in court. It also specifies that examiners must be sworn in and hold office at the pleasure of the court.

No human review is needed.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code outlines the procedures for taking depositions in legal cases within Kentucky. It details who can administer oaths, where offices for examiners should be located, and the qualifications of officers authorized to take depositions both within and outside the state. It also specifies the methods for taking depositions, such as upon notice or interrogatories, and the requirements for notice, adjournments, and the transmission of deposition records.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details the procedural aspects of taking depositions in Kentucky courts. It specifies the roles of various legal officers, the locations for taking depositions, and the methods by which they can be conducted. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Section 566-568
classification.summary: These sections of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outline the requirements for providing notice to take depositions. This includes the content of the notice (written, signed, addressed to the party, specifying time, place, action, and witness name if out of county), what constitutes reasonable notice based on travel distance and preparation time, and provisions for adjournment. It also includes case law annotations regarding the reasonableness and sufficiency of such notices.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the procedures for taking depositions in legal proceedings. It specifies when depositions must be taken upon interrogatories, particularly in cases involving non-residents, persons under disability (excluding coverture or infancy and coverture combined), or numerous parties who have not appointed an agent or attorney. It also details the process for filing interrogatories, providing notice, and the clerk's role in filing cross-interrogatories if none are provided by the parties. The exception for divorce and alimony cases is noted.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details procedural rules for taking depositions in legal cases. It outlines conditions under which depositions must be taken on interrogatories, such as when parties are non-residents, under disability, or numerous. The text also describes the administrative steps involved in filing interrogatories and issuing commissions. There is no mention of race or any provisions that would inherently discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: General Rules of Examination
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code outlines the general rules for the examination of witnesses and the introduction of evidence during trials. It details the order in which parties should present their evidence, the court's control over interrogation methods, and rules regarding direct and cross-examinations, impeachment, and re-examination of witnesses. It also covers the separation of witnesses and compelling testimony from those present. The text includes provisions for taking depositions and the procedures for handling contempt of court during these processes.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details general rules of legal procedure concerning evidence and witness examination. It does not contain any explicit or implicit references to race, segregation, or differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906. The confidence is high because the content is purely procedural and lacks any racial dimension.

classification.title: Witness may be impeached by adverse party - how impeached.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the rules for impeaching a witness's credibility in legal proceedings. It specifies that a witness can be impeached by contradictory evidence, by showing prior inconsistent statements, or by evidence of their general reputation for untruthfulness or immorality. However, it prohibits evidence of particular wrongful acts, with the exception of convictions for felonies. The statute also details how to introduce contradictory statements and the limitations on cross-examination regarding collateral matters.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the general rules of evidence regarding witness impeachment. It does not contain any explicit or implicit references to race, nor does it establish any discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: These sections of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outline rules of examination for witnesses in legal proceedings. Specifically, § 598 details how a witness can be contradicted by evidence of prior different statements, requiring the witness to be questioned about these statements first. § 599 addresses the admissibility of evidence of a witness's good character, stating it's only admissible after their reputation has been impeached. § 600 covers the re-examination of a witness, noting that re-examination on the same matter requires court leave, but re-examination on new matters is permitted.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute sections (§§ 598-600) deal with general rules of evidence and witness examination in legal proceedings. They outline procedures for impeaching a witness's testimony and presenting character evidence. There is no mention of race, nor is there any indication of differential treatment based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws.

classification.title: Competency of Witnesses
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the general rules for the competency of witnesses in legal proceedings. It establishes that most individuals are competent to testify unless found incapable of understanding the facts by the court. It also lists several exceptions and modifications, including provisions related to spouses, minors, lunatics, parties against whom a claim is made, attorneys, priests, prisoners, assignors of claims, and the admissibility of certain types of evidence like book entries and letters. Specific subsections address atheists, children's intelligence, confessions, disqualifications due to perjury, expert evidence, grand jurors, handwriting, and law books.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text deals with the general rules of witness competency in legal proceedings. It outlines who can testify and under what conditions, with various exceptions and clarifications. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The statute appears to be a procedural rule of evidence applicable to all individuals regardless of their race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Husband and wife- exceptions.

It generally prohibits them from testifying about communications made during marriage or against each other. However, exceptions are made for actions concerning lost baggage and for situations where one spouse acted as an agent for the other in the absence of the other. It also references an amendment allowing testimony under specific agency circumstances.

The statute's focus is on marital privilege and exceptions to it. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow. The document type is 'codes' as it is presented as part of a compiled legal code (Civil and Criminal Code 1906).

It also covers the confidentiality of communications between attorneys and clients, and between spouses.

classification.title: Perpetuation of Evidence
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for perpetuating evidence in Kentucky. It allows individuals who expect to be parties to an action, or non-residents with an interest in real property, to file a petition in equity to preserve testimony. The petition must state the nature of the expected controversy, the names and details of the expected adverse party (if known), the names of witnesses and the facts to be proved, and any obstacles preventing the commencement of the action. The expected adverse party must be summoned, and if brought before the court, an order for witness examination may be made. Depositions are taken, certified, and kept by the clerk, with the petitioner responsible for all costs.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the legal procedures for perpetuating evidence, which involves preserving testimony for future legal actions. It outlines the requirements for filing a petition, summoning parties, and taking depositions. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The statute appears to be a procedural law applicable to all citizens. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence score and no racial indicator. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: Motions and Notices
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for serving legal notices in civil and criminal cases. It details who can serve a notice (an authorized officer or any person over sixteen not a party to the action), what the return of service must state (when and how it was served), and the requirements for the person serving the notice (official position for officers, affidavit for non-officers). It also specifies methods of service, including delivery, offering delivery, leaving with a family member, affixing to the door, or serving an agent/attorney if the person cannot be found or has no known place of abode. The court can also direct the mode of service in certain situations, such as when parties are numerous or have no known address or attorney. Finally, it addresses service on persons under disability, requiring service on their guardian, curator, or next friend, with exceptions for coverture and combined infancy/coverture.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural rules for serving legal notices. It covers who can serve, how service should be documented, and various methods of service. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The statute appears to be a general procedural rule applicable to all litigants. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Offer to Confess Judgment

If the plaintiff refuses the offer and does not recover more than the offered amount at trial, the plaintiff must pay the defendant's costs incurred after the offer. The offer itself cannot be used as an admission of liability or evidence in the trial.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title XIV, Executions and Distress Warrants, Sections 643-645
classification.summary: These sections of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outline procedures for handling claims and sales of property seized under execution. Section 643 states that a claimant or purchaser of property for which an indemnifying bond has been taken is barred from suing the officer if the surety was good at the time. They can, however, sue on the bond. Section 644 details the disposition of surplus proceeds from the sale of such property. Section 645 allows a claimant, other than the defendant, to suspend the sale of personal property by executing a bond to the plaintiff in the execution, promising to pay the value of the property and ten percent thereon if it's found subject to the execution.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details procedures related to property seizure and sales under execution, specifically concerning indemnifying bonds and claimant actions. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of differential treatment based on race. The language and subject matter are purely procedural and economic, related to civil law and debt collection. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of statutes.

classification.summary: These sections of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outline the procedures for the appraisement of property seized under execution or distress warrants. They detail the selection of appraisers, the administration of oaths, the creation of an appraisement bond, and the process for moving for judgment on such bonds. The statutes also address various scenarios related to these bonds, including those executed by landlords, joint property owners, and third parties claiming ownership.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 649-51
classification.summary: These sections of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 detail procedures related to property appraisement and the discharge of levies in execution of judgments. Specifically, § 649 addresses objections to property appraisement and jury determination of value. § 650 explains that giving a bond does not discharge a levy, but the obligor can move to discharge the bond and levy. § 651 outlines how these provisions apply to executions from inferior courts, including the return of indemnifying and claimant's bonds, and the process for motions and appeals before justices of the peace.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute sections (649-51) from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 deal with procedural aspects of property execution and bond filings in legal proceedings. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The language and subject matter are purely procedural and administrative, related to the enforcement of judgments and the handling of property claims. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906 - Title XIV: Executions and Distress Warrants
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code outlines procedures for distress warrants, which are legal processes to seize property for unpaid rent. It details how these warrants are handled, including the return of bonds to either a justice of the peace or a circuit court depending on the value of the property, and the tenant's ability to discharge the levy by posting a bond. The text also includes notes on legal precedents and forms related to these procedures.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details procedures for distress warrants related to rent collection and property seizure. It outlines legal processes, bond requirements, and court jurisdictions. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The statute appears to be a procedural civil law concerning landlord-tenant disputes and debt collection. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 654-660
classification.summary: These sections of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky detail procedures for distress warrants and executions. They cover motions for judgment on bonds, defenses allowed (such as rent not being due or property being exempt), jury trials, judgments for rent and damages, suspending warrants with bonds, abolishing ancient trials of right of property, and proceedings for levying executions on joint property. The notes refer to case law interpreting these statutes.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text (§ 654-660) from the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky deals with civil procedures related to distress warrants and executions. It outlines the legal processes for landlords to recover rent and for creditors to seize property. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The procedures described are general legal mechanisms applicable to all citizens. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Proceedings by Sureties
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the legal procedures for sureties. It details how a surety can sue their principal or co-surety after a debt matures, and the modes of proceeding, including provisional remedies. It also addresses the process when an execution is levied on the property of a joint owner, stating that the property cannot be sold or the owner deprived of possession without consent, but rather appraised and the execution returned, allowing the creditor to enforce their lien through an action in equity. The heirs of a surety can also obtain relief under this section.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text pertains to legal proceedings involving sureties and joint ownership of property in civil and criminal matters. It details the rights and actions available to sureties and creditors in relation to debt and property liens. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

It also includes case law annotations related to the execution of process by various officers, including sheriffs, coroners, jailers, constables, and special bailiffs, and the conditions under which they can act.

It specifies how clerks should indorse papers with filing dates and the return dates of certain legal documents. The annotations discuss the roles and limitations of various law enforcement officers in executing these processes. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Miscellaneous Provisions
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines miscellaneous provisions related to legal procedures. It covers topics such as the authority of deputies to act for ministerial officers, how a majority can act when authority is conferred on several, the substitution of affirmation for oaths, the mode of counting days between acts, the execution of new bonds for defective ones, and the swearing of sureties as to their sufficiency. It also addresses discovery actions, successive actions on the same contract, costs for unnecessarily multiplied actions, the abolition of the writ of ne exeat, provisional remedies granted by two justices, appearance upon execution of an attachment bond, the description of unknown defendants, stating other liens in actions to enforce liens, actions against sureties after principal's bankruptcy, provisions for the sale of real and indivisible personal property, terms of sale, purchaser bonds, and the release of liens on sold real property.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text is from the 'Miscellaneous Provisions' chapter of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky. The content of the sections (678-699) deals with procedural aspects of the legal system, such as the powers of deputies, how majorities can act, oaths, bonds, and the execution of legal processes. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Section 692-3, 692, 693
classification.summary: These sections of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 deal with procedural matters in legal actions. Section 692-3 addresses amendments to petitions when a defendant's name or residence is discovered or unknown. Section 692 outlines the process for enforcing liens on property, requiring plaintiffs to state other liens and making other lienholders defendants. It also details how proceeds from property sales are distributed. Section 693 concerns actions against sureties after the principal has declared bankruptcy, outlining how the court should determine the liability of the sureties.

classification.title: Provisions as to sales of real property- indivisible property- other liens.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for selling real property to satisfy debts. It details how courts should determine if property is divisible without impairing its value, the process for selling indivisible property, and how to handle multiple liens on a property. It also specifies that plaintiffs must identify other lienholders and make them defendants, and that sales should not prejudice existing lien rights. The statute includes provisions for the pro rata satisfaction of debts secured by the same or equal-rank liens.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the legal procedures for selling real property to satisfy debts and manage liens. It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly refers to race, segregation, or differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code (Civil and Criminal Code 1906).

classification.title: Indivisible personal property may be sold - payment of excess.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedure for selling indivisible personal property to satisfy a debt. If the property cannot be divided without impairing its value, the officer must sell the entire property, even if it brings more than the debt. The excess amount is to be paid to the defendant, or the bond for it made payable to him. The statute also includes several case citations related to property sales, redemption rights, and liens.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses the sale of indivisible personal property for debt payment. It details the process of selling the entire property if it cannot be divided without loss of value and how any excess proceeds should be handled. The statute and its accompanying case citations focus on property law and debt collection procedures. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Time, place and terms of sale of property - advertisement.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the requirements for public sales of property made under court order. It specifies that sales must be public, on reasonable credit (not less than three months for personal property and six months for real property), and advertised according to the court's direction. Unless otherwise specified, sales should occur at the courthouse door, and the notice must state the sum for which the sale is to be made. It also references other sections of the Kentucky Statutes regarding appraisement and newspaper advertisements.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the procedural requirements for property sales under court order, including notice, credit terms, and location of the sale. There is no mention of race or any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code (Civil and Criminal Code 1906).

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 696
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically § 696, details various procedural aspects related to the sale of land, including appeals, appraisement requirements, the conduct of sales by commissioners, and the validity of such sales under different circumstances. It covers rules regarding the necessity of appraisement, the qualifications and actions of appraisers, the role of the commissioner in conducting sales, and conditions under which sales may be set aside or re-offered.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, which is a compilation of statutes. The specific section, § 696, discusses procedural rules for land sales, including requirements for appraisement, the conduct of sales by commissioners, and grounds for setting aside sales. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The rules appear to be general procedural guidelines applicable to all parties involved in land sales within the legal system of Kentucky at that time. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with high confidence, and the category is null.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 provides miscellaneous provisions related to sales conducted by commissioners. It details the discretion of commissioners in making sales, the requirements for the description of land to be sold, and conditions under which sales may be set aside due to erroneous judgments, errors in procedure, inadequacy of price, or irregularities. It also covers fees, terms of sale, and the confirmation of sale reports.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically dealing with miscellaneous provisions related to sales conducted by commissioners. The content focuses on procedural aspects of property sales, such as the discretion of the commissioner, the description of the property, grounds for setting aside a sale (like inadequacy of price or procedural errors), and the confirmation of sale reports. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow laws, with a high confidence score, and the category is null.

classification.title: Bond of purchaser and effect of interest on.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the requirements for purchasers of property sold under court order. It specifies that purchasers must provide a bond for the price, with surety approved by the officer making the sale. The bond will bear interest from its date at the rate of the judgment. These bonds have the force of judgments, and no replevy is allowed on executions issued upon them; sales must be for cash. The statute also includes several notes referencing case law related to the advertisement, appraisement, payment, and potential relief for purchasers in various circumstances, such as defective titles or misrepresentations.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the financial and legal aspects of property sales conducted under court order. It details the procedures for purchasers to provide bonds, the interest rates applicable, and the enforcement of these bonds. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The statute appears to be a procedural rule for civil court sales and does not fall under the scope of Jim Crow laws. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for 'is_jim_crow' and the category is null. The document type is 'codes' as it is presented as part of a compiled code (Civil and Criminal Code 1906).

It details the duties and liabilities of purchasers, commissioners, and courts in the process of property sales, including aspects like redemption rights, confirmation of sales, payment of purchase price, and appeals. The notes refer to various Kentucky court cases that interpret these provisions.

It outlines the rights and responsibilities of parties involved in such sales, including purchasers, commissioners, and the court. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines rules regarding the purchase of personal and real property sold under court order. It details when a purchaser is entitled to the property, how liens on real property are released upon payment of purchase money, and addresses various legal precedents and conditions related to judicial sales, including the application of caveat emptor, the effect of judgment reversals on purchasers' titles, and compensation for improvements or rent in cases of reversed judgments.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses the legal procedures and rights associated with the purchase of property sold under court order. It covers aspects like entitlement to property, lien release, and the implications of judgment reversals on purchasers. There is no mention of race, nor any implicit or explicit racial discrimination or segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the mode of proceeding in Quarterly, Police, County, and Justices' Courts. It details how legal proceedings are regulated by the code, the direction and execution of process, the duties of judges and clerks, docket keeping, pleadings, summons service, trial procedures, evidence, subpoenas, venue, jury trials, new trials, record keeping, interrogatories, judgments, set-offs, provisional remedies, and the levying and sale of land under execution.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically Chapter I, "Mode of Proceeding In," which details the procedural rules for various lower courts in Kentucky. The content focuses entirely on the mechanics of legal proceedings, such as how processes are issued and served, how trials are conducted, and how judgments are handled. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 details the procedures for issuing and serving legal process in various courts, including quarterly, police, city, mayor's, and justice of the peace courts. It outlines who can serve process (sheriff, coroner, constable, marshal, or a special agent appointed by a justice under specific conditions) and clarifies the duties and powers of judges and clerks in managing court dockets and issuing process. It also specifies that only one docket is required for all actions and motions.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses the procedural aspects of legal document service and court administration within the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906. It details the roles of various court officers and the mechanics of issuing and serving legal documents. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title XVI, Chapter 705-10: Quarterly, Police and Justices' Courts
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines procedural rules for quarterly, police, and justices' courts. It details when pleadings can be oral or must be written, based on the amount in controversy (fifty dollars being the threshold). It also specifies the time for summons service to authorize a trial, procedures when an action is called for trial, how evidence is taken in equitable actions, how subpoenas are served, and the district in which an action is to be tried. The accompanying notes provide case law interpretations and clarifications on these procedural aspects.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text and accompanying notes detail procedural rules for lower courts in Kentucky. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. The content focuses solely on legal procedures and jurisdiction. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 711-715
classification.summary: These sections of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outline the procedures for determining the place of trial for civil and criminal cases in quarterly and justice courts, including provisions for multiple defendants, transient defendants, jury trials, new trials, and record-keeping. Specifically, § 711-5 discusses venue based on defendant residence, § 711 [833] addresses venue with multiple defendants in different districts, § 712 [884] covers venue for transient defendants, § 713 [835] details jury trial eligibility and impaneling, and § 714 [836] specifies the time for granting new trials. § 715 [837] mandates record-keeping by judges and justices.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details procedural aspects of the justice and quarterly courts in Kentucky, specifically concerning venue, jury trials, and new trials. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The sections focus on the mechanics of legal proceedings rather than the rights or treatment of specific racial groups. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title XVI, Sections 716-721
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code outlines procedures for filing interrogatories, judgments against parties refusing to testify, renewal of lost judgments, and handling set-offs or counterclaims in quarterly, police, and justice courts. It also addresses the transfer of cases when counterclaims exceed court jurisdiction.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906 - Appeals From Their Judgments
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines procedures for appeals from judgments of inferior courts, specifically addressing how land can be levied on and sold when execution is returned with 'no property' found. It details the process of obtaining certified copies of judgments and executions from inferior courts and filing them with the circuit court to gain similar remedies as if the judgment were originally rendered in the circuit court. It also introduces Chapter II, which covers appeals from these judgments, including how they are taken, transmitted, tried, and the associated costs.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details legal procedures related to civil judgments, executions, and appeals from inferior courts to the circuit court. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The procedures described are general legal processes applicable to all litigants. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Appeals From Their Judgments
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for filing an appeal from a judgment. It details the requirements for the appealing party, including producing a certified copy of the judgment and costs, executing a bond with sufficient sureties, and the clerk's role in issuing an order to stay proceedings and transmit original papers. It also specifies how the appellee will be summoned. A provision for appeals to the circuit court based on the amount in controversy is mentioned but noted as repealed. Several case citations are included to clarify aspects of appeals, such as determining the amount in controversy, appeal bonds, attachments, dismissal of appeals, and jurisdiction.
classification.reasoning: The statute describes the procedural aspects of appealing a court judgment. It details the steps involved in filing an appeal, the required documentation, and the roles of the clerk and parties involved. There is no mention of race, and the procedures described are general legal processes applicable to all litigants. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 725-6
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the procedures for appeals from judgments. It details the transmission of original papers to the appellate court, when appeals stand for trial, how they are tried, and the jurisdiction of courts in appeal cases. It also covers practice on appeal, the powers of the circuit court on appeal, the effect of transferring a case by appeal, the burden of proof, and the dismissal of appeals.

It focuses on the mechanics of transferring cases, the jurisdiction of different courts, and the conduct of trials on appeal. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly create racial distinctions or segregation. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: § 734. Appeal matter of right- how granted.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedure for bringing a judgment from an inferior court to the Court of Appeals. It specifies that an appeal is a matter of right and can be granted by the court rendering the judgment during the term it was made, or by the clerk of the Court of Appeals thereafter upon application and filing a copy of the judgment. It also references other sections related to the effect of tender before appeal, judgments that will not be reversed, final orders, appeals from county courts, and forms for granting appeals.
