classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 details various aspects of appeals to the Court of Appeals in Kentucky. It covers when an appeal must be applied for, the effect of errors in appellant names, the lower court's right to grant or refuse appeals, and specific types of judgments and orders that are appealable, such as those related to habeas corpus, final judgments, attachments, receivers, dower, land division, ad quod damnum, police attendance, divorce allowances, disbarment of attorneys, removal of jailers, tax collection, road applications, demurrers, dismissals for want of jurisdiction, new trials, removal of causes to federal court, confirmation of sales, and exceptions to reports. It also includes appeals from rules requiring transfer of attachment suits, refusal to make parties, administrator's settlements, damages on injunction dissolution, invalid bail bonds, and service in other counties.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 and details procedural rules for appeals to the Court of Appeals. It outlines various scenarios and types of judgments or orders from which an appeal can be taken. There is no mention of race, nor any implicit racial distinctions in the application of these legal procedures. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Appeals to Court of Appeals
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines various decisions and orders from circuit and county courts that are appealable to the Court of Appeals, as well as those that are not. It details specific types of judgments, such as those involving merchant's licenses, claims between creditors, executor's decisions, and the dismissal or continuation of suits. It also lists judgments and orders that are explicitly non-appealable, including the quashing of summons returns, certain judgments for money payment, orders requiring election of causes of action, and the dissolution of injunctions.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky and details the rules and procedures for appeals to the Court of Appeals. It lists various types of court decisions that can or cannot be appealed. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any other discriminatory practices typically associated with Jim Crow laws. Therefore, this statute is classified as 'no' for being a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 734
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the jurisdiction of the Court of Appeals based on the amount in controversy for appeals. It details the monetary thresholds required for appeals by both plaintiffs and defendants, and specifies conditions under which jurisdiction is established or denied, such as in cases involving land titles, taxation, or injunctions.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses the jurisdictional limits of the Court of Appeals based on the monetary value of the case. It details rules for appeals, including the amount in controversy, set-offs, counter-claims, and specific types of cases like those involving land or taxation. There is no mention of race, nor any implicit or explicit racial discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code from 1906.

classification.title: Appeals to Court of Appeals
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the procedures and conditions for appealing cases to the Court of Appeals. It defines appellant and appellee, specifies when appeals are permissible (e.g., in cases involving title to land, trespass where title is in issue, mandamus, prohibition, office, franchise, or freehold), and discusses rules regarding parties to an appeal, payment of judgments, presumptions in favor of judgments, res judicata, and void judgments. It also touches on the court's power to issue writs and the consequences of errors in party names.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the legal process for appeals to the Court of Appeals in Kentucky. It covers procedural aspects, definitions of parties, and conditions under which appeals can be made. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of statutes from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Section 736-737
classification.summary: These sections of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outline the procedures for appeals to the Court of Appeals. They detail when a summons for the appellee is necessary, the process for constructive summons, the appointment of an attorney for constructively summoned appellees, and the preparation of transcripts of records for appeals. Specifically, subsection 3 of section 737 mandates that the entire record must be copied if any appellees were constructively summoned and did not appear or were not actually summoned to answer the appeal, and were defendants in the original action.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details procedural aspects of legal appeals within the Kentucky court system. It outlines requirements for summons, appearance, and transcript preparation. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The language used is purely procedural and applies to all litigants. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

The annotations refer to various court cases that interpret these procedural rules.

classification.title: Supersedeas on Appeals
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures and conditions for obtaining a supersedeas to stay proceedings on a judgment during an appeal. It details the requirements for supersedeas bonds, the role of the clerk in issuing supersedeas, and provisions for discharging a supersedeas if the bond is insufficient. Notably, it specifies that the provisions for supersedeas on appeals do not apply to judgments granting, modifying, perpetuating, or dissolving injunctions. In such cases, the court has discretion to suspend, modify, or continue the injunction during the appeal, with provisions for review by the Court of Appeals.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title XVIII, Supersedeas on Appeals
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 details the legal procedures and effects of a supersedeas, which is a court order that suspends or stays a lower court's judgment pending an appeal. It covers aspects like the discharge of a supersedeas, its effect on proceedings, the appellee's rights, and its application to injunctions and writs of mandamus. It also discusses damages awarded on appeals and the role of supersedeas bonds and sureties.
classification.reasoning: The provided text discusses legal procedures related to appeals and supersedeas bonds. It does not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race. The content is purely procedural and administrative within the legal system. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Supersedeas bond - conditions of.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions for issuing a supersedeas on appeals in Kentucky courts. It requires the appellant to provide a bond, approved by the clerk, to cover costs and damages, and to satisfy the original judgment if affirmed. The bond also covers rents, hire, or damages to property during the appeal if the appellee is kept out of possession. The notes provide case law interpretations regarding the liability of obligors and sureties on these bonds.
classification.reasoning: The statute and its accompanying notes deal with the procedural aspects of appealing court judgments in Kentucky, specifically concerning the requirement and conditions of a supersedeas bond. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is presented as part of a compiled legal code (Civil and Criminal Code 1906).

quent change in the law, and willnot

plained of must affect the substantial

roneous . Huffaker v . Bank , 13 Bush
644; Bowman v . Holloway , 14 Bush 426 ;
and see,further,secs.134,338,providing
that errors not substantial will be dis-
regarded.
(5) A judgment against a divorced
wife , and also against her late husband ,
for her debt ; as to him , to be levied on
any propertyreceivedfrom her during
marriage , although erroneous as to him
isnot prejudicialto her. Joyes v.Ham-

neously rejected, the court willconsider

Walrath v. Viley, 1 Bush 266; Millett
v . Parker , 2 Met . 608 ; Tudor v . Tudor,

a party to testifywillnot authorizea

ceptionsto depositionson the ground

vant will not warrant a reversal, when

evant . Root v . Merriwether , 8 Bush
397. Nor is an error in overrulinga

classification.title: Errors must be substantial to authorize reversal - additional record may be filed.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions under which a judgment can be reversed or modified by the Court of Appeals. It states that a reversal or modification is only permissible for errors that prejudice the substantial rights of the complaining party. The court may also allow the filing of an additional portion of the record under certain conditions. The text also includes annotations and case law related to this section, discussing various scenarios where errors were or were not considered substantial enough to warrant reversal.
classification.reasoning: The statute text provided deals with general legal principles of appellate review, specifically concerning what constitutes a reversible error. It focuses on the substantiality of errors and their prejudice to a party's rights, rather than any racial classifications or discriminatory practices. There is no mention of race, nor any implicit indication of racial bias in the application of these legal standards. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: Dismissal of appeal - enforcement of judgment for part of demand, effect on appeal.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures and conditions under which an appeal can be dismissed. It specifies that an appellee can move to dismiss an appeal if it was improperly granted or if the appellant's right to prosecute it has ceased. The motion requires written grounds and cannot be heard without the appellant's consent before the trial date, except with proper notice. It also clarifies that enforcing a judgment for only a portion of the demand does not prevent the appellant from appealing the unrecovered portion.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text pertains to civil procedure regarding the dismissal of appeals. It details the legal process for challenging an appeal and the conditions under which it can be dismissed. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically § 759, outlines procedures for appeals in legal cases. It details how to handle appeals taken for delay, the process of affirming judgments, and conditions under which appeals can be barred or continued. It also references previous sections and case law related to appeals.

The specific section, § 759, deals with legal procedures related to appeals. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any other element typically associated with Jim Crow laws. The content is purely procedural and relates to the judicial process for appeals. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Rules of court- mandate when to issue- petition for rehearing.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the rules and procedures for the Court of Appeals in Kentucky, including the authority to make rules for business dispatch, order preservation, case arguments, and the timing of motions and petitions for rehearing. It specifies that no mandate shall issue or decision become final until thirty days after the decision, excluding Sundays, unless the court directs otherwise. It also details procedures for suspending mandates and allowing petitions for rehearing during court vacations or recesses. The statute also notes that provisions regarding mandate issuance apply only to civil cases, with criminal cases having immediate mandate issuance.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural rules for the Kentucky Court of Appeals, specifically concerning the issuance of mandates and the process for petitions for rehearing. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest differential treatment based on race. The statute appears to be purely procedural and administrative in nature, governing the operations of the court. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow laws, with a high confidence score. The category is null because the statute is not related to any of the specified Jim Crow categories. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code (Civil and Criminal Code 1906).

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title XVIII, § 762
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the powers of the Court of Appeals to enforce its mandates upon inferior courts and officers through fines and imprisonment. It also details procedures for the return of cases to lower courts after an appeal, including notice requirements and restitution of property under erroneous or void judgments. The section references several case precedents related to these procedural matters.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details procedural rules for the Kentucky Court of Appeals and lower courts regarding the enforcement of judgments and mandates. It discusses legal processes, case remands, and restitution of property. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest a discriminatory intent or effect based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is identified as 'codes' because it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 763-4
classification.summary: These sections of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outline the conditions under which the Court of Appeals will or will not reverse or modify a judgment from an inferior court. Section 763 details that judgments which can be set aside or modified by the original court after the term has passed, void judgments, or judgments against constructively summoned defendants who did not appear, will not be reversed by the Court of Appeals until a motion to set aside or modify has been made and overruled in the inferior court. Section 764 specifies that upon affirmance or dismissal of an appeal from a judgment for the payment of money that has been superseded, 10% damages will be awarded against the appellant. It also adds a provision for 10% damages against insurance, railroad, or other corporations not created under Kentucky law, even if the judgment is not superseded, as amended in 1888.

There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code from 1906. The confidence is high because the text clearly deals with legal procedure and lacks any racial component.

They detail how indictments are handled, the process for removing prosecutions between courts, and the local jurisdiction of circuit and justices' courts. Section 16 specifically allows circuit court judges to require grand juries to state in indictments whether misdemeanors occurred within the local jurisdiction of city or police courts.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The focus is purely on legal process and court authority. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the procedures and conditions under which arrests can be made, both by peace officers and private individuals. It details when an arrest can be made with or without a warrant, the authority of officers to cross county lines in pursuit, and the specific circumstances under which private citizens can make an arrest. It also includes case law annotations clarifying these provisions.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the legal procedures for making arrests in Kentucky in 1906. It outlines the authority of peace officers and private citizens to arrest individuals under various circumstances, such as in obedience to a warrant, when a public offense is committed in their presence, or when they have reasonable grounds to believe a felony has been committed. The text also includes annotations from court cases that interpret these provisions. Crucially, there is no mention of race or any differential treatment based on race within this specific section. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the procedures for examining charges in an examining court. It details the magistrate's authority to examine charges, commit to jail or hold to bail, and the process for examination, including notice to the county attorney, objections to the magistrate, adjournments, disposition of the defendant, bail procedures, witness management, exclusion of spectators, recording testimony, and the discharge or commitment of the defendant. It also covers the return of papers to the clerk of the trial court and who may hold examining trials.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details the procedural aspects of examining courts and the roles of magistrates in the legal process. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute appears to be a general procedural law applicable to all individuals within the jurisdiction. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It details how charges are stated, the right to counsel, notification of the county attorney for felonies, and the process for replacing a magistrate if an objection is sustained. It also covers the allowance of bail during adjournments and the possibility of holding examining trials on Sundays.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details procedural aspects of the examining court system in Kentucky in 1906. It focuses on the rights of the defendant, the role of the magistrate and county attorney, and the process of objection and adjournment. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The procedures described appear to be general legal processes applicable to all defendants. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: County judge exclusive jurisdiction in homicide
classification.summary: This act, from 1886 and included in the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code, grants county judges exclusive jurisdiction as examining courts in all homicide cases. It outlines procedures for holding examining trials at the county seat and specifies who can preside if the county judge is absent or unable to preside, including justices of the peace or police judges. It also clarifies that one justice of the peace can hold an examining court and that county judges do not receive fees for these services.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the jurisdiction and procedures for examining courts in homicide cases. It specifies the roles of county judges, justices of the peace, and police judges in these proceedings. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The focus is purely on judicial procedure and jurisdiction. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It details the conditions under which bail can be taken, the qualifications of bail, and the procedures for applying for and taking bail bonds. Notably, it exempts the judge of the city court of Louisville from receiving fees for services as an examining court, instead providing a salary.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the procedures and authorities for taking bail. It specifies which peace officers (sheriff, deputy, or coroner in specific circumstances) are authorized to take bail bonds and details the conditions under which bail bonds will be considered valid, even if there are minor irregularities. It also clarifies the duties of the defendant and bail in ensuring appearance for examination or trial.

It specifies which officials can accept bail and the conditions for the validity of bail bonds. There is no mention of race or any differential treatment based on race within this text. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: Surrender of defendant discharges bail.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process by which bail can be discharged by surrendering the defendant. It specifies that at any time before the forfeiture of their bond, bail can surrender the defendant, or the defendant can surrender themselves to the jailer of the county where the prosecution is pending. This surrender must be accompanied by a certified copy of the bail bond, and the jailer must detain the defendant as if on commitment. Upon receiving a written acknowledgment of the surrender from the jailer, the bail is exonerated.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the legal procedure of surrendering a defendant to discharge bail. It does not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly relate to racial segregation, discrimination, or any other aspect of Jim Crow laws. The text focuses solely on the mechanics of bail and its release. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence score. The category is null as it's not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title V, Chapter 87-88
classification.summary: These sections of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outline the procedures for bail surrender. Section 87 details how bail can arrest or have a defendant arrested for surrender, either directly or by authorizing a peace officer or other designated person. Section 88 explains the process for returning money deposited in lieu of bail upon the defendant's surrender to the jailer, requiring a certificate of surrender and notice to the Commonwealth's attorney.

The language and subject matter are purely procedural and apply to all defendants regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled Civil and Criminal Code.

classification.title: Deposit of Money in Lieu of Bail

classification.title: Forfeiture of Bail
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the procedures and conditions for the forfeiture of bail bonds when a defendant fails to appear in court as required. It details when bail is forfeited, the process of forfeiture, attachment against the bail's property, and conditions under which a forfeiture may be discharged or remitted by the court. It also includes case law examples of situations that would exonerate bail, such as the defendant being arrested by federal authorities, serving in the Federal army, or being prevented from appearing by the Commonwealth's actions.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky, specifically concerning the forfeiture of bail. The content focuses on legal procedures related to a defendant's failure to appear in court and the consequences for their bail bond. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The statute deals with general criminal procedure applicable to all defendants. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Section 93
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky, specifically § 93, deals with the forfeiture of bail. It outlines various scenarios and legal precedents regarding the liability of sureties when a defendant fails to appear in court. The notes discuss conditions for forfeiture, the role of the court and governor in remission, bail during trials, continuances, changes of venue, multiple indictments, and the effect of arrest or imprisonment in other states or by mob violence on bail obligations. It also clarifies that a defective indictment does not void the recognizance.

classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the procedures for the forfeiture of bail bonds. It details how summons are issued against bail, the process for attachment against the property of bail if the summons is not executed, and the legal proceedings that follow, including the waiver of previous irregularities by the appearance and agreement of parties. It also specifies pleading requirements for the defense and conditions for appeal.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the legal procedures for handling forfeited bail bonds in Kentucky. It outlines the process of issuing summons to bail, the possibility of attachment against their property, and the subsequent legal actions. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled Civil and Criminal Code.

It details conditions under which a forfeiture can be discharged, how forfeited money is credited, and the court's discretion in remitting bail amounts before judgment. It also includes case law annotations related to these provisions.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The statute deals with general legal processes applicable to all defendants. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Recommitment After Giving Bail or Depositing Money
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions under which a defendant, who has already posted bail or deposited money, can be arrested and recommitted to jail. These conditions include failure to appear, insufficient bail (due to death, removal from the state, or general insufficiency), or being indicted for a non-bailable offense. The clerk is to issue a warrant for arrest, and the defendant may be bailed again under specific circumstances.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with procedural aspects of criminal law related to bail and recommitment. It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly targets any racial group, nor does it establish separate facilities or differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it appears to be part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: Grand Jury, Its Powers and Duties
classification.summary: This section outlines the powers and duties of the grand jury, including its selection, formation, investigation of offenses, access to public records and prisons, securing witness attendance, the role of the foreman, evidence the jury may hear, compelling testimony, advice from the court and Commonwealth's attorney, the presence of individuals during examinations, when indictments should be found, secrecy of proceedings, disclosure of testimony by grand jurors, their non-responsibility for acts or votes except for perjury, and the handling of papers from examining courts and resubmissions.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is a table of contents and a snippet of a legal code from 1906. It details the structure and functions of the grand jury system. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices that would classify it as a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it refers to compiled statutes. The confidence is high because the content is purely procedural and administrative regarding the legal system.

classification.title: Grand Jury, Its Powers and Duties
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the powers and duties of grand jurors. It details when a grand juror can be compelled to disclose testimony (for consistency checks or perjury proceedings), their non-responsibility for statements or votes except in cases of perjury, the process for laying examining court papers before the grand jury, and the conditions under which a dismissed charge can be resubmitted to another grand jury. It also includes annotations from court cases related to these provisions.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural aspects of grand jury proceedings, including the disclosure of testimony, juror accountability, and the handling of dismissed charges. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute appears to be a general procedural rule applicable to all individuals regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the procedures for the finding of an indictment by a grand jury. It details when an indictment can be resubmitted, the effect of failing to indict at a succeeding term, the definition of an indictment, the requirement for nine jurors to concur, the foreman's endorsement, the listing of witnesses' names, and the manner of presenting the indictment to the court and its filing by the clerk. It also includes case law annotations regarding the exoneration of bail upon failure to indict, the court's power to change charges, dismissal of prosecutions by examining courts, and the effect of failure to dispose of an indictment.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details the procedural aspects of the criminal justice system, specifically concerning indictments and grand juries. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws from 1906.

county to which a change of venue istaken. Ky . Stat., sec. 1177, and defend-
ant can not objectto it because found
inthatcounty. Parker v.Com .,12 Bush
191; Jenning v . Com ., 13 R. 79.
(7) See further, sec. 158 and notes.
§ 119. (1) Construction of section.
This sectionis mandatory, and the in-
dictment must be indorsed a "true bill"
and signed by the foreman. Oliver v.
Com ., 95 Ky . 372 , 15 R. 662 ; Pence v .
Com ., 95 Ky . 618, 16 R. 148; Com . v.
L. & N. R. R. , 17 R. 562 ; 86 Ky . 313, 9
R. 481; but the rightto objectto an in-
dictment because defective in these re-
spectsis waived by failingto demur or
move to set it aside. Pattersonv.Com .,
86 Ky .313,9 R. 481; and see Pickerelv .
Com ., 17 R. 120 .
(2 ) Foreman of grand jury- oath to
jury. Ky . Stat., sec.2250.
§ 120. (1) Names of witnesses ex-
amined should be written on indictment,
and a failureto do so isground for set-
tingitasideif motion ismade in proper
time as provided in sec. 157. Sutton v.
Com ., 97 Ky . 308, 17 R. 184; but this
rulewill not be appliedwhen itappears
name isomitted by oversightor witness
isnot material. Com . v.Glass, 107 Ky .
160, 21 R. 819 ; and witnesses whose
names are not on indictment or in sub-
poena for Commonwealth may be called
on to testify. Underwood v . Com ., 27
R. 8 ; 28 R. 642 .
(2) Practice- Objectionto indictment
because names of witnessesdo not ap-
pear on itshouldbe made by motion to
quash. Com . v.Brewer, 113 Ky .217, 24
R. 72 .
(3) Witnesses that were not examined
by grand jury may be added to names
on indictment before it is returned . Mc-
Intirev.Com .,26 R. 469.
§ 121. (1) Presenting indictment .
When the record shows that an indict-
ment is returnedinto court as required
by Code and orderedto be filed,the fail-
ure of the clerk to sign the indorsement
on back of indictment to the effect that
itwas filedin open court willnot au-
thorizeitsdismissal. Pattersonv.Com .,
86 Ky .313,9 R. 481; Pearce v.Com ., 10
R. 178 .
(2) Clerk'sindorsementof filingof in-
dictment is not essential, and when
omitted may be supplied. Pence v.
Com .,95 Ky .618,16 R. 148.
(3) An indictmentfor grand larceny
for stealing sheep was indorsed on its
back and describedin the orderfilingit
as an indictmentfor "sheep stealing."
Held sufficientidentificationof the in-
dictment. Com . v. English, 6 Bush 431.
(4) It isnot necessaryto the validity
of an indictment that it should show
upon its face that it had been presented
on oathby thegrand jury. Jane v.Com .,
3 Met . 18 .
(5) See further, sec. 158 and notes.

classification.title: The Finding of an Indictment
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the procedures for finding and presenting an indictment. It specifies the number of grand jurors required to concur (nine, with a note referencing the Constitution for twelve), the requirement for the foreman to indorse the indictment as 'a true bill' and sign it, and the necessity of listing the names of examined witnesses on the indictment. It also details the process of presenting the indictment to the court and filing it with the clerk as a public record, along with notes on discontinuance, lost indictments, and new indictments after a change of venue.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural aspects of finding and filing an indictment in Kentucky's legal system. It focuses on the mechanics of the grand jury process, the role of the foreman, and the documentation required for a formal accusation. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute appears to be a standard procedural rule within the criminal justice system. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence score, and the category is null. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws.

§ 122 [121,128,129] Requisitesof an indictment. The indictment
must contain-
1. Titleof prosecution,name of court and parties. The title
of the prosecution, specifyingthe name of the courtin which
the indictment ispresented and the names of the parties.
2. Statement of acts constitutingoffense. A statementof
the actsconstitutingthe offense, in ordinaryand conciselan-
guage, and in such a manner as to enablea personof common
understanding to know what is intended; and with such degree
of certaintyas to enablethecourtto pronouncejudgment,on
conviction, accordingto the rightof the case. (Facts that
must be stated with certainty, sec. 124.)
§ 122. (1) Abortion. It is necessary
that an indictment for abortion should
allegethatthe mother was quickwithchild, and thatthepotionwas adminis-
tered with the intentionto destroy the
lifeof the child, and that such was its
effect. Mitchellv. Com ., 78 Ky . 204;
but where the indictment isfor the death
of the mother as the result of an abor-
tion,itisnotnecessarytoallegethatde-ceasedwas "quick" with child. Peoples
v . Com ., 87 Ky . 487, 10 R. 517.
(2) Accessory. An accessorybeforethefactisin legalcontemplationguiltyofmurder ifguiltyat all; and an indict-
ment in which the offensecharged is
murder is good, although the particular
circumstancesof the offenseas set out
in the indictmentconstitutethe person
an accessory before the fact and not a
principal. Stricklinv. Com ., 83 Ky .
566, 7 R. 627 ; and see further as to ac-
cessoriesbeforeand afterfact,Tullyv.
Com ., 11 Bush 154; Tullyv. Com.,13
Bush 142; Ablev.Com .,5 Bush 698.
(3) See further,as to accessorybefore
the fact, Ky . Stat., sec. 1128; afterthe
fact, Ky . Stat., sec. 1129.
(4 ) Aiders and abettors. When two
personsarejointlyindictedformurder,one as principaland the otherasaider
and abettor,theonechargedasprincipal
may be foundguiltyof aidingandabet-
ting, and the one charged with aiding
and abettingmay be foundguiltyas
principal. Benge v.Com .,92 Ky .1,13
R. 308; Gaskins v. Com .,97 Ky .494,17
R. 352; 110 Ky .356,22 R. 1845.
(5 ) Aider and abettor in crime created
by statutepunishable. Com . v. Carter,
94 Ky .527,15 R. 253;overrulingStamper
v. Com ., 7 Bush 612; and see Freyv.
Com ., 83 Ky .191,7 R. 174.
(6) An indictmentagainstA andB,

It emphasizes the need for ordinary and concise language that enables a person of common understanding to grasp the intended meaning and allows the court to pronounce judgment upon conviction. The text also includes annotations referencing specific legal cases and statutes related to abortion, accessories, and aiders and abettors in criminal offenses.

It focuses on the structure and content of indictments to ensure clarity and legal sufficiency. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of the 'Civil and Criminal Code 1906'.

- propertywas sufficientlyallegedin an
indictmentthat charged that the house

(12) An indictment chargingthat of-
fense was committed "feloniouslyand

(15) Assault and battery. An assault

Cornelisonv.Com .,84 Ky .583,9 R. 230.
(16) An indictmentchargingthat the
defendant"did in sudden heat and pas-

(17) Under an indictment for a simple

holding that an assaultand battery is
not necessarilya lower degree of an as-
saultwith intentto kill.
(18) Bigamy . An indictmentfor big-
amy chargingthat "Eliza Davis on the
day ofMay ,1877,inthecounty and
State aforesaid, and before the finding
of this indictment, having a husband
then living, unlawfully married John
Mackey ," isnot sufficient; itshouldaver

out the name of the first husband or

rulingCom.v.Whaley,6 Bush266;andsee Ky . Stat ., sec. 1216.
(19) "It is immaterial in an indict-
ment forbigamy at what particulardate
the firstmarriage occurred, if the ac-
cusedhad at time of second marriage a
livingwife to whom he had been previ-
ously married." Faustre v. Com ., 92
Ky . 34, 13 R. 347 ; and see as to imma-
terialvarianceinname of firstwife,note
3, section 124.
(20) The offenseof bigamy committed
by marrying in another State while the
former marriage is in force is not an
offense against the laws of this State.
Johnson v . Com ., 86 Ky . 122, 9 R. 419.
(21) Bribery . As to sufficiencyof in-
dictmentfor,seeKy. Stat.,secs. 1586,1587; and Johnsonv.Com.,90 Ky.53,
12 R. 20; Cheek v . Com ., 87 Ky . 42 , 9 R.
880;Com .v.Selby,87Ky .594,10R.621;

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky details the form and requisites of an indictment for various offenses, including false swearing and forgery. It outlines the specific allegations required for a valid indictment, citing relevant case law and statutes. It also touches upon indictments for gaming offenses.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky and discusses the legal requirements for indictments in criminal cases. It covers offenses such as false swearing, forgery, and gaming. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any other element typically associated with Jim Crow laws. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with high confidence. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code. The category is null because it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Section 122 - Notes to Title VI: Form and Requisites of an Indictment
classification.summary: This section provides notes and case law interpretations regarding the form and requisites of indictments in Kentucky criminal proceedings, specifically addressing sufficiency of allegations for various offenses such as operating a faro bank, gaming, housebreaking, infanticide, injury to property, and larceny. It details what constitutes a sufficient indictment for each offense based on previous court rulings.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically section 122, which deals with the form and requisites of an indictment. The content discusses the legal sufficiency of indictments for various criminal offenses like operating a faro bank, gaming, housebreaking, infanticide, and larceny. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any other element typically associated with Jim Crow laws. Therefore, this statute is classified as 'no' for being a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 provides notes and references to various legal statutes and case law concerning the form and requisites of indictments for a range of offenses. It details the specific elements required for a valid indictment for crimes such as rape, robbery, selling liquor, and trespass, citing relevant Kentucky statutes and court decisions. It also covers general principles of indictment, such as the use of multiple counts and the handling of unnecessary particularity in averments.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, which is a compilation of statutes. The content focuses on the legal requirements for drafting indictments for various criminal offenses. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The confidence is high because the text clearly pertains to legal procedure and not racial segregation or discrimination.

Title VI ] [§ 123-24Form and Requisites of an Indictment .
§ 123 [122] Form of indictment. It may be substantiallyof the
followingform :
The Commonwealth of Kentucky
against
George Smith }
Franklin Circuit Court .
The grand jury ofFranklincounty,in the name and by the
authorityof the Commonwealth of Kentucky, accuseGeorge
Smith of the crime of [heregivethe name of the offense, ifit
haveone,suchas treason,murder,manslaughter,arson,larceny,
rape,etc.,or thelike,or,ifithave no generalname ,then a brief
general descriptionas given by law ], committed as follows,
viz.: The said George Smith , on the day of - , 1853,in
the county aforesaid [here insert the acts constitutingthe
offense], against the peace and dignity of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky . (Sce otherforms ,pages 679-687.)
§ 124 [123]Factsconcerningwhich itmust be directand certain. The
indictment must be direct and certain as regards-
1. The party. The partycharged.
2. The offense. The offensecharged.
3. The county. The county in which the offensewas com-
mitted .
4. The circumstancesof the offense. The particularcircum-
stancesof the offensecharged,ifthey be necessaryto constitute
a complete offense.
(84) Weapon . Under an indictment
chargingthat defendant carried"con-
cealeddeadly weapons, to-wit, a bowie
knife, and also a dirk or dagger," proof
that he carried either will authorize a
conviction. Com .v.Howard ,3 Met .407;
Ky . Stat., sec. 1309.
(85) Willful killing. Under sec. 1151,
Ky .Stat., see Conner v. Com ., 13 Bush ,
714 .
(86) Wounding . IndictmentunderKy .
Stat.,sec.1166,need not charge that act
was done feloniously. Cundiffv. Com .,
86 Ky . 196, 9 R. 537 ; and Burns v .Com .,
3 Met . 13; Flint v.Com .,81 Ky .186,5 R.
51; Gratzv .Com .,96 Ky .162,16 R. 465;
as to wounding in sudden heat and
passion, as describedin sec. 1242, Ky .
Stat.,see Com . v.Hawkins , 11 Bush 603 ;
Sosh v. Com ., 4 R. 254 ; Com . v . Duncan ,
91 Ky . 592, 13 R. 162.
§ 123. (1) Form . It is not necessary
now that an indictment founded on a
statute should conclude "against the
formof thestatute." Com .v.Kennedy,
15 В. М. 531; nor is it necessarywhen
theindictmentchargestime offensewas
committed to add "in the year of our
Lord ," or the letters A. D. Com . v .
Traylor , 20 R. 97.
(2) It isnot necessarythat the style
of the court, or the case, or the formal
part of the indictmentshallbe repeated
in each count when there is more than
one count . Greenwood v . Com ., 11 R.
220 ; and see 106 Ky . 602 , 21 R. 222 .
(3) Caption of indictment, omitting
name of county , does not render indict-
ment bad . Johnson v . Com ., 12 R. 835 .
§ 124. (1) Certainty in stating offense.
A convictionfor stealingPhilipDaniel's
hog can not be sustained under an in-
dictment for stealingStephen Daniel's
hog. Hensley v. Com ., 1 Bush 11. An
indictmentformurder describedthe per-
son murdered as "Barbara Kriel ." The
proof disclosed her name to be "Marga-
ret Kriel ." The variance was held not
material. Kriel v. Com ., 5 Bush 362;
see secs . 125-128 .
(2 ) On the trialof an indictment for
stealingthehorseofA ,evidencethatthe
horse stolen belonged to B does not war-
rant a conviction, unless the offense be

classification.title: Form and Requisites of an Indictment
classification.summary: This statute outlines the required format and content for indictments in Kentucky criminal proceedings. It specifies that an indictment must clearly state the Commonwealth of Kentucky, the defendant's name, the court, the county, the offense charged, and the specific acts constituting the offense. It also details that the indictment must be direct and certain regarding the party charged, the offense, the county, and the circumstances of the offense.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural requirements for drafting a legal indictment in Kentucky. It focuses on the clarity and specificity of the charges against an accused individual, ensuring that the indictment is direct and certain regarding the party, offense, county, and circumstances. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Section 125
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 addresses errors in the name of a defendant in an indictment. It states that such errors will not invalidate an indictment if the defendant is otherwise sufficiently described. It provides several case examples illustrating this principle, including variances in a wife's name in a bigamy trial, the importance of stating the correct county for the offense, and the sufficiency of indictments for carrying concealed weapons or for assault. It also touches on the specificity required for indictments for statutory offenses and the treatment of surplusage.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses the technical requirements and procedural aspects of indictments in criminal cases. It focuses on how errors in naming a defendant or describing an offense can be handled without invalidating the indictment. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws from 1906.

classification.title: Form and Requisites of an Indictment
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the requirements for indictments in Kentucky. It specifies that an indictment should charge only one offense, with exceptions for offenses committed in different modes or by different means, which can be alleged in the alternative. It also details procedures for correcting a defendant's name if their true name is discovered after indictment and before execution. The text includes several case citations illustrating these principles.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural requirements for indictments in Kentucky's legal system. It focuses on the structure and content of indictments, including how to charge offenses and handle name discrepancies. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines rules for the joinder of offenses in a single indictment and specifies that erroneous allegations regarding the person injured or the owner of property are not material if the offense is otherwise sufficiently described to identify the act. It lists specific offenses that can be charged together, such as larceny and receiving stolen property, or robbery and burglary. It also provides case law examples and interpretations related to these procedural rules.

It includes case law examples illustrating these principles, such as the flexibility in proving the date of an offense unless the time is a material ingredient, and how variances in dates are handled in prosecutions.

§ 132 [134] Libel- allegationsof indictmentfor. An indictment
for libelneed not setforthany extrinsicfacts, for the purpose of
showing the application, to the party libeled, of the defamatory
matter ; but it is sufficientto stategenerallythat the same was
publishedconcerninghim . (As to jurisdiction,see sec.21.)
§ 133 [135] Written instrument- descriptionof. If a written in-
strument, which is the subjectof an indictment for forgery, lar-
ceny or other offense, have been withheld or destroyed by the
act or procurement of the defendant, and such destruction or
withholding be allegedin the indictment,and proved on the trial,
a misdescriptionof the instrument is immaterial.
§ 134 [136] Perjuryor subornationof necessaryallegations. In an
indictment for perjury, or subornation of perjury, it is sufficient
to set forth the substance of the controversyor matter in respect
to which the offensewas committed , and in what court and be-
fore whom the oath allegedto be falsewas taken, and that the
court, or the person before whom it was taken, had authorityto
administer it, with proper allegationsof the falsityof the matter
on which the perjury is assigned; but the indictment need not
set forth the pleadings, record or proceedings with which the
oath isconnected, nor the commissionor authorityof thecourt
or person beforewhom the perjury was committed .
§ 135. Larcenyorembezzlementofmoney - sufficiencyofindictment.
In an indictment for the larceny or embezzlement of money , or
United Statescurrency,or bank notes,itissufficienttoallegethe
larcenyor embezzlement of the same without specifyingthe coin,
number , denomination or kind thereof.

Title VI ] [§ 136Form and Requisites of an Indictment .
§ 136 [137] Words of statutedefiningoffenseneed not be strictly
pursued. The words used in a statute to define an offense need
notbe strictlypursued in an indictment,but other words convey-
ingthesame meaning may be used.
§ 136. (1) Statute- indictmentunder.
"Where the words of the statute are de-
scriptiveof the offense, the indictment
will be sufficient if it shall follow the
languageand expresslycharge the de-
scribed offense of the defendant . But
thisruleappliesonly to offenseswhich
are complete in themselves, when the
acts set out in the statute have been
done or performed." Davis v. Com ., 13
Bush 318; Ward v. Com ., 14 Bush 233 ;
Mitchell v . Com ., 88 Ky . 349, 10 R. 910;
108 Ky .59, 21 R. 1444.
(2) An indictment under the "Kuklux
law" will be sufficientif it charges the
offense in the words of the statute . Sell-
ers v. Com ., 13 Bush 331 ; Buchanan v .
Com ., 95 Ky . 334, 15 R. 738 ; Ky . Stat-
utes, section 1223.
(3) "Where the words of the statute
aredescriptiveof the offense,the indict-
ment should follow the language and
expresslycharge the described offense
against the defendant , or it will be de .
fective." Com . v. Tanner, 5 Bush 316;
Com . v. Turner , 8 Bush 1 ; Conner v .
Com ., 13 Bush 714.
(4 ) "When a statute contains provisos
or exceptionsin distinctclauses, it is
not necessaryto statein the indictment
that the defendant does not come within
theexceptionsortonegativetheprovisos
it contains. Com . v . Bull, 13 Bush 656 ;
Com . v. Bierman, 13 Bush 345; but "if
the statutecreatingan offensecontains
in its enacting clauseexceptions, it is
necessary to negative such exceptions in
the indictment so as to show that the de-
fendant does not come within any of
them ." Conner v. Com ., 13 Bush 714;
108 Ky . 6,21 R. 1483.
(5) Under the statuteprovidingthat
it should be an offenseto carry concealed
deadly weapons except by certainper-
sons, who were specified, it was not
necessary to state that the defendant
did not come within any of the excep-
tions. Com . v.McClanahan ,2 Met .8.
(6 ) "If a statute refers to a common
law offenseby itspopularname,as,forinstance,'robbery, and proceedsto im-
pose a penaltyfor itscommission, it is
not sufficientto charge the accused with
its commission in the statutoryterms
alone; in such cases the pleadermust
definetheoffenseby statingthe common
law elements necessaryto its commis-
sion." Mitchellv.Com ., 88 Ky . 349, 10
R. 910.
(7 ) An indictment for an offense cre-
ated by statute need not charge that the
acts complained of were done " feloni-
ously" unlessthe statuterequiresthat
the act should be done feloniously in or-
der to constitutethe offense. Higgins v .
Com ., 94 Ky . 54 , 14 R. 729 ; Cundiff v .
Com ., 86 Ky . 196, 9 R. 537 ; 110 Ky . 516,
22 R. 1929 .
(8 ) An indictment for the violationof
a penal statute need not indicate the
statute upon which it is founded ; it is
sufficientto bring the case within the
provisions of the statute. Powers v .
Com .,90 Ky . 167, 11 R. 964.
(9 ) If the statute declares, that it
shall be an offense if the act is " unlaw-
fully" done,it need not be charged that
accused acted maliciously, willfullyor
feloniously; ifhe be chargedin the lan-
guage of the statute creating the crime
and inthemannerrequiredbyby it,then
thechargeiscomplete." Higginsv.Com.,94 Ky .54,14 R. 729.
(10) An indictment under sec.2 ,art.6 ,
chap.29,Gen. Stat. [now sec. 1166,Ky .
Stat.],isgood,althoughitfailstocharge
that the person wounded "did not die
thereby," and failsto allegethat thepis-
tol was " loaded with a leaden bullet or
other hard substance." Burns v . Com .,
3 Met . 14 .
(11) An indictmentchargingthat "the
defendantdid unlawfully, feloniously
and maliciously,with intentto killhim,
cut and wound one PhilipYeiser," issuf-
ficientunder sec.2,art.6,chap.29,Gen.
Stat . [now sec. 1166, Ky . Stat.]. Flint
v.Com .,81 Ky . 186,5 R. 51; 96 Ky . 162,
16 R. 465 .
(12) Aider and abettor in statutory
crime may be punishedunlessitclearly
appear that itsprovisionswere only in-
tended to apply to party actuallycom-
mittingcrime.Com .v.Carter,94 Ky .527,
15 R. 253; overrulingStamper v. Com .,
7 Bush 612 .
(13) Although an offenseisnot charged
in the precisewords used in the statute
in defining it, if it is alleged in words
conveying the same meaning it will be
sufficient. Taylor v.Com ., 3 Bush 508;

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the procedures for a defendant's arraignment and plea. It details the grounds for setting aside an indictment, including errors in jury formation or presence of unauthorized individuals in the grand jury room. It also addresses misdemeanor judgments by default and the defendant's right to trial in absentia. Notably, it includes a case (Com. v. Wright) where a white person indicted by an all-white grand jury could not complain about the exclusion of Black individuals, as a prior statute declaring only white persons competent as jurors had been declared unconstitutional. However, it also states that jury commissioners are not bound to select negro jurors, but no otherwise competent citizen should be excluded based on race or color.
classification.reasoning: The statute itself is a procedural code governing criminal proceedings. While it does not enact a Jim Crow law, it references and discusses the legal implications of race in jury selection, specifically mentioning the exclusion of 'negroes' and the unconstitutionality of a statute that limited jury service to 'white persons'. The explicit mention of race in the context of jury composition and the discussion of past discriminatory practices makes the racial indicator 'explicit'. However, the core function of the statute is procedural, not discriminatory in its current form, hence 'is_jim_crow' is 'no'. The confidence is high because the statute's primary purpose is clear, and the racial references are contextual rather than prescriptive of discriminatory practices within this specific section.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines procedures for when an indictment is set aside. It details the process of resubmitting the case to a grand jury, the defendant's custody status, and the conditions under which a defendant would be discharged if a new indictment is not found. It also includes annotations referencing previous court decisions and related statutes concerning grand jury proceedings and indictments.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details procedural aspects of the criminal justice system, specifically concerning the handling of indictments that have been set aside. It outlines the steps for resubmitting a case to a grand jury and the implications for the defendant's custody or bail status. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The statute appears to be a general procedural rule applicable to all defendants. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Section 161-165
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the procedures for a defendant's pleadings in a criminal case. It details when a motion can be overruled, requiring the defendant to plead. The allowed pleadings are a demurrer or a plea. It specifies how these pleadings should be entered in court and recorded, and provides the forms for a demurrer, a plea of guilty, a plea of not guilty, and a plea of former acquittal or conviction. It also lists the conditions under which a demurrer is proper, such as jurisdictional issues, non-conformity with requirements, multiple offenses, failure to state a public offense, or the presence of a legal defense or bar.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural aspects of criminal court proceedings, specifically focusing on the defendant's pleadings (demurrer and plea). It outlines the legal mechanisms for challenging an indictment or entering a defense. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled Civil and Criminal Code.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title VI, Sections 166-168
classification.summary: These sections of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outline procedures for handling cases where a court lacks jurisdiction over an offense. Section 166 details the process when a demurrer for want of jurisdiction is sustained, including the transfer of papers and the defendant if the offense is a felony and committed within another court's jurisdiction in the state. Section 167 extends these provisions to situations where lack of jurisdiction is discovered during a trial. Section 168 addresses the misjoinder of offenses in an indictment, allowing for the dismissal of one offense.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text deals with procedural aspects of criminal law, specifically concerning court jurisdiction and the handling of indictments with multiple offenses. There is no mention of race, and the procedures described are general legal processes applicable to all defendants. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws. Confidence is high because the content is clearly procedural and lacks any racial component.

classification.title: Arraignment and Pleadings by Defendant
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the procedures for a defendant's plea in court. It specifies that a plea of guilty must be entered by the defendant in person, and that such a plea can be withdrawn before judgment. A plea of not guilty is defined as a denial of all material allegations in the indictment, allowing for the introduction of defense evidence. It also states that an acquittal or conviction serves as a bar to further proceedings for the same offense.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural aspects of entering pleas in criminal cases within the Kentucky legal system. It covers the requirements for entering a guilty plea, the possibility of withdrawing pleas, and the effect of pleas of not guilty and prior judgments. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. The statute appears to be a general procedural rule applicable to all defendants. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically § 176, details legal principles related to former jeopardy, arrest of judgment, and the effect of prior convictions or acquittals on subsequent prosecutions. It outlines when a previous legal proceeding can bar a new one, including cases of acquittal, discharge of a jury, and the definition of legal jeopardy. It also touches upon degrees of offenses and the implications of jury discharge.

classification.title: Acquittal or conviction bars all degrees of offense; Dismissal that does not operate as bar.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the legal principle of double jeopardy in Kentucky. It states that a conviction or acquittal for an offense, including any of its degrees, bars another prosecution for the same offense. It also specifies that certain dismissals of an indictment by the court, such as on demurrer or for objections to form or substance, do not bar another prosecution for the same offense. The text includes case law annotations related to trials under town ordinances, trials in United States courts, and situations involving multiple indictments or variances between indictments and proof.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text, "§ 177 [177] Acquittal or conviction bars all degrees of offense. If an offense consists of different degrees, a conviction, or acquittal, by judgment upon a verdict, shall be a bar to another prosecution for the offense in any of its degrees. (Degrees of offense defined, sec. 263.) § 178 [178] Dismissal that does not operate as bar. The dismissal of the indictment by the court, on demurrer, except as provided in section 169, or for an objection to its form or substance taken on the trial, or for variance between the indictment and the proof, shall not bar another prosecution for the same offense.", along with its annotations, deals with procedural aspects of criminal law, specifically concerning double jeopardy and the effect of dismissals on subsequent prosecutions. There is no mention of race, nor any implicit racial discrimination in its text or annotations. The document type is identified as 'codes' because it is presented as part of a compiled legal code (Civil and Criminal Code 1906). Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Mode of Trial
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the procedures for trying legal issues. It specifies that issues of law are tried by the court. Issues of fact are tried by the court if the punishment is limited to a fine of sixteen dollars, and by a jury in all other cases. It also states that a defendant on trial for a felony must be present, while a misdemeanor trial can proceed in the accused's absence.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Section 184
classification.summary: This statute outlines the conditions under which a defendant can be tried in their absence for misdemeanors, and the procedures for defendants in felony trials regarding custody and bail. It specifies that misdemeanor trials can proceed without the defendant's presence, while felony trials require the defendant's presence unless they are on bail and consent to remain so until final submission to the jury. It also details what happens if a defendant escapes custody during a felony trial.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text (Section 184 of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906) deals with procedural aspects of criminal trials, specifically concerning the presence of the defendant. It differentiates between trials for misdemeanors and felonies regarding the defendant's required presence. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws. The confidence is high because the text clearly focuses on legal procedure without any racial implications.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 189
classification.summary: This statute outlines the rules for postponing criminal trials in Kentucky. It specifies that if a defendant requests a continuance due to the absence of a material witness, the Commonwealth's attorney does not have to admit the truth of the witness's expected testimony. Instead, the attorney only needs to admit that the witness would testify as alleged. The affidavit can then be read as a deposition, subject to exceptions. The Commonwealth's attorney can still present evidence to controvert the affidavit or impeach the absent witness. However, the court can grant a continuance if it deems it necessary for the ends of justice, unless the Commonwealth's attorney admits the truth of the alleged facts.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with procedural aspects of criminal trials, specifically the granting of continuances due to absent witnesses. It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly discriminates based on race. The rules apply to all defendants in criminal or penal actions. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is from a compiled code from 1906.

It details how juries are summoned and selected, the role of the clerk in drawing names, and how the panel is filled when jurors are excused or challenged. It also mentions the possibility of summoning jurors from adjoining counties and the sheriff's authority to compel attendance.

It also details the appointment of special bailiffs for summoning jurors and the compensation for jurors summoned from other counties.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural aspects of jury selection and summoning in Kentucky courts in 1906. It covers how juries are formed, the roles of sheriffs and other appointed individuals in summoning jurors, and the process for obtaining jurors from neighboring counties when local impartiality is in question. There is no explicit or implicit mention of race, racial segregation, or any discriminatory practices against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is classified as 'codes' because it is part of a compiled legal code (Civil and Criminal Code 1906).

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906 - Challenging the Jury
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the procedures and grounds for challenging a jury panel or individual jurors in legal proceedings. It details the types of challenges (to the panel or individual juror), when they must be made, who can make them, and the process for determining their validity. It covers grounds for challenging the panel, such as substantial irregularities in selection or summoning, and grounds for challenging individual jurors, including actual and implied bias. It also specifies the order of challenges between the Commonwealth and the defendant.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details the legal procedures for challenging juries in Kentucky courts as of 1906. It focuses on the mechanics of jury selection and challenges, such as grounds for challenge, timing, and the roles of the prosecution and defense. There is no mention of race, ethnicity, or any discriminatory practices based on these characteristics within the text itself. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled Civil and Criminal Code. The confidence is high because the text is purely procedural and lacks any racial indicators.

classification.title: Challenging the Jury

It details the types of challenges (peremptory and for cause), when they must be made, and the number of peremptory challenges allowed to both the defendant and the Commonwealth. It also lists general causes for challenging a juror, such as lack of qualifications, prior felony conviction, or mental/physical incapacity.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The rules apply equally to all parties involved in a legal case. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is presented as part of a compiled legal code (Civil and Criminal Code 1906).

classification.title: Challenging the Jury
classification.summary: This section of the code outlines the grounds for challenging potential jurors in criminal and civil cases. It details conditions for actual bias (a juror's state of mind preventing impartial judgment) and implied bias (specific relationships or prior conflicts with parties). It also lists general competency requirements for jurors (citizenship, age, sobriety, discretion) and exemptions for certain professions. The text notes that the fact a person not competent served on a jury does not automatically invalidate a verdict, nor can exceptions be taken after the jury is sworn.
classification.reasoning: The provided text discusses the legal procedures for challenging jurors in court cases. It defines 'actual bias' and 'implied bias' and lists general qualifications and exemptions for jury service. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would disproportionately affect Black individuals. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it appears to be part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: Challenging the Jury
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the grounds for challenging a juror in a criminal case. It details reasons such as prior service on a grand jury, coroner's jury, or a trial jury for the same offense, or service in a civil action related to the indictment. It also includes a provision for challenging a juror who holds conscientious opinions that would prevent them from finding a defendant guilty when the offense is punishable by death. The code also specifies the procedure for challenging jurors, including who challenges first and how challenges are tried.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the legal procedures and grounds for challenging potential jurors in criminal proceedings. It focuses on impartiality, prior involvement in related cases, and conscientious objections. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is identified as 'codes' because it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 discusses rules of jury trial, specifically concerning the character of the accused and witnesses. It details when and how the character of the accused can be put into issue, the admissibility of evidence regarding character, and the impeachment of witnesses. It also touches upon circumstantial evidence and the expenses related to exhuming bodies for evidence. Notably, it states that proof of the bad character of any of the family of the accused should not be admitted, and that the Commonwealth cannot introduce evidence of particular facts to affect the credibility of defense witnesses, such as claims of belonging to a clique to swear "negroes out of any offense charged against them."
classification.reasoning: The statute is part of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906. While the majority of the text discusses general rules of evidence regarding the character of the accused and witnesses, subsection (10) contains a quote that explicitly mentions 'negroes' in the context of a defense strategy: "that all of the witnesses introduced by the defendant belonged to a clique that were banded together to swear negroes out of any offense charged against them." This explicit mention of a racial group in a legal context, even if it's within a quote illustrating a defense tactic rather than a direct legal prohibition or mandate, indicates a racial dimension. However, the statute itself does not enact segregation or discrimination. It is a procedural rule. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow. The confidence is high because the racial mention is explicit, but the statute's primary function is not Jim Crow legislation. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906

It covers topics such as incompetent evidence, limiting the effect of testimony, illegal liquor selling, malice in manslaughter cases, motive for crime, the testimony of prisoners, provocation, questions that degrade a witness, rape cases, records of former trials, the admissibility of certificates and records, res gestae, and the separation of witnesses. It does not contain any provisions that are inherently Jim Crow laws.

It details various aspects of jury trial conduct and evidence admissibility. After a thorough review of the text, there are no explicit or implicit mentions of race, racial segregation, or any other discriminatory practices that would classify it as a Jim Crow law. The content is purely procedural and evidentiary in nature. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with high confidence, and the category is null.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically § 221, contains notes and case law related to jury trials. It covers topics such as the presence of witnesses in the courtroom, the admissibility of testimony from former trials (especially for deceased witnesses), and the admissibility of threats as evidence. It does not contain any provisions related to racial segregation or discrimination.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906. I have reviewed the content of § 221 and its associated notes. The text discusses procedural aspects of jury trials, including witness testimony, the reproduction of testimony from former trials, and the admissibility of threats as evidence. There is no mention of race, racial segregation, or any discriminatory practices. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

lowed to createany presumption againsthim or her.

allowed to testifyshallnot be allowed to testifyin chiefafter
any other witness has testifiedfor the defense .
§ 3. Repealed . If two or more persons are jointlyindicted,
they may testifyfor each other, unless a conspiracy ischarged
in the indictment and proven to the satisfactionof the court.
(See referencenext section.)
§ 4. Repealed. If a conspiracy is charged in the indict-
ment and proven to the satisfactionof the court, then each de-
fendant named in the indictment may testifyon his own behalf,
asabove providedin sections1 and 2 ofthisact.] (Sections3
and 4 repealedby act1894.)

son in furtheranceof justice,permit them to offerevidence upon

structthe jury on the law applicableto the case, which shallal-
ways be given in writing.

classification.title: Conduct of the Jury Trial
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the procedures for jury trials, including the defendant's right to make a statement, present evidence, and the rules for offering rebutting evidence. It also details the requirement for written jury instructions and addresses the defendant's right to testify in their own behalf, with specific provisions for jointly indicted defendants and cases involving conspiracy charges. The text also includes references to other statutes regarding the exclusion of young spectators and the separation of witnesses.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural aspects of jury trials in Kentucky courts, including the order of statements, introduction of evidence, and jury instructions. It also addresses the defendant's right to testify. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The statute appears to be a general procedural rule applicable to all defendants. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow status with high confidence. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws. The category is null because it is not a Jim Crow law.
