classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 225 - Notes to Title VI: Conduct of the Jury Trial
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky provides notes and case law references related to jury instructions during trials. It covers topics such as the weight of affirmative testimony, accomplice testimony, instructions given after arguments begin, aiders and abettors, alibi defenses, assault and battery instructions, assuming facts, bigamy, self-defense, character impeachment, circumstantial evidence, confessions, consequences of one's acts, correction of instructions, deadly weapons, and defenses of dwelling and others. It does not contain any provisions related to racial segregation or discrimination.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is a section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky, specifically detailing notes and case law related to jury instructions in trials. A thorough review of the text reveals no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory practices based on race. The content focuses entirely on legal procedures and evidentiary considerations within the judicial system. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It details how courts should instruct juries on various legal principles and the weight of evidence, citing numerous Kentucky Supreme Court cases.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky, specifically concerning jury trial instructions. The content discusses legal principles related to murder, manslaughter, malice, and the jury's duty to follow the court's exposition of the law. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any other element typically associated with Jim Crow laws. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

It covers topics such as mutual rencontre, negligence, killing of officers, oral instructions, presumptions of prejudice, prolix instructions, protection and defense of family, rape, reasonable doubt, reckless use of firearms, self-defense, and the rights of an accused on their own premises. It does not contain any provisions that are explicitly or implicitly related to Jim Crow laws.

It details various legal principles and precedents concerning jury instructions, defenses, and specific criminal offenses. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that suggest differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Conduct of the Jury Trial
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code outlines procedures for jury trials, including the order of arguments, defendant custody during felony trials, and proceedings when an offense is committed out of jurisdiction or out of state. It also includes notes on limitations of argument, improper arguments by counsel, and when verdicts will not be set aside due to attorney misconduct.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details procedural aspects of jury trials in Kentucky courts. It covers the order of arguments, defendant custody, and jurisdictional issues related to offenses. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 232-234
classification.summary: These sections of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky deal with procedural aspects of jury trials. Section 232 outlines proceedings when a higher grade of offense is proven during a trial. Section 233 details when an indictment may be quashed and the jury discharged, which can operate as an acquittal. Section 234, which was repealed by an act in 1894, addressed the competency of jointly indicted defendants as witnesses for each other, with an exception for charges of conspiracy. The notes following section 234 provide case law interpretations and clarify the repeal of the original provision.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text and accompanying notes discuss procedural rules for criminal trials, specifically concerning the handling of different offense grades, the quashing of indictments, and the testimony of jointly indicted defendants. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly create racial distinctions in the legal process. The statute is a procedural code, not a Jim Crow law. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with high confidence. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code. The category is null because it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 240-41
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines rules regarding the corroboration of confessions and accomplice testimony in criminal trials. It states that a confession made out of court requires additional proof of the offense, and a conviction cannot be based solely on the testimony of an accomplice without corroboration. It also details various legal precedents and interpretations related to the admissibility and weight of such evidence.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses rules of evidence in criminal trials, specifically concerning confessions and accomplice testimony. It outlines legal requirements for corroboration to ensure fair convictions. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is presented as part of a compiled code from 1906.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Title VI, § 242-43 Conduct of the Jury Trial
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the requirements for corroborating evidence in criminal trials, particularly concerning accomplice testimony. It specifies that the uncorroborated testimony of an accomplice is insufficient for conviction and details when a court must instruct a jury to acquit if these corroboration requirements are not met. It also addresses the dismissal of indictments by the Commonwealth's Attorney.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, § 244
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the conditions under which a jury must be kept together during a trial. For capital offenses, jurors must remain together after acceptance. For other felonies, they may separate before submission but must be kept together afterward. For misdemeanors, separation is permitted until final discharge, or the court can order them to be kept together. The section also includes case law regarding the dismissal of indictments and jury separation.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text, § 244 of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, deals with the procedural aspects of jury trials, specifically when jurors must be kept together. It differentiates between capital offenses, other felonies, and misdemeanors regarding jury separation. The text also includes case law related to the dismissal of indictments and jury conduct. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code. The confidence is high because the content is clearly procedural and lacks any racial element. Human review is not needed.

classification.title: Conduct of the Jury Trial
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code outlines the procedures for jury trials, including the duties of officers in charge of the jury, admonitions given to the jury by the court, provisions for jury rooms and expenses, and the papers juries may take into deliberation. It details rules to ensure jury impartiality and prevent external influence during deliberations.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the procedural aspects of jury trials, focusing on the conduct and management of juries. It covers aspects like the oath of officers in charge, court admonitions to the jury, jury room provisions, and what evidence juries can take. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute appears to be a general procedural rule applicable to all jury trials regardless of the race of the parties involved. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Verdict - how rendered
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the procedures for rendering and receiving a jury's verdict. It details when a jury must be brought into court, how their names are called, and how the foreman declares the verdict. It also includes case law annotations regarding verdicts returned on Sundays, the prisoner's presence, the clerk reading the verdict, correcting verdicts, and recommendations for punishment credit.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details the procedural aspects of jury verdicts in Kentucky courts. It covers how a verdict is delivered, the timing of its reception (including on Sundays), and the roles of the court and jury in the process. There is no mention of race, and the procedures described are general to all legal proceedings, not specific to any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws.

classification.title: Verdict

It details how juries should present factual conclusions and how the court uses these to render judgments. It also specifies how different degrees of offenses, such as homicide, assault, larceny, arson, burglary, and attempts, are categorized and how punishments are determined based on these degrees.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Section 263
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 discusses legal principles related to verdicts in criminal cases, specifically focusing on offenses such as malicious wounding, assault with intent to rob, breach of the peace, homicide, housebreaking, lottery promotion, malicious stabbing, conspiracy, embezzlement, rape, adultery, robbery, and larceny. It details how defendants can be found guilty of lesser included offenses or different degrees of the same offense, and how convictions for one offense can bar prosecution for another. The text is a compilation of case law annotations related to these criminal statutes.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically section 263, which deals with legal principles related to verdicts in criminal cases. The content discusses various criminal offenses and how they relate to each other in terms of prosecution and conviction, such as lesser included offenses and degrees of crimes. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws.

classification.title: Verdict
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines rules for jury verdicts in criminal cases. It details how offenses can be included within a charged offense, the limits on conviction degrees, verdicts for multiple defendants, the process of polling the jury, and proceedings related to a plea of insanity. It also includes case law examples illustrating these principles.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details procedural aspects of jury verdicts in criminal cases, including how offenses are defined, how juries can find guilt or innocence for multiple defendants, and procedures for polling the jury and handling insanity pleas. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The text is purely procedural and legalistic. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

It details the grounds for a new trial, such as the jury receiving evidence out of court or the verdict being decided by lot, and the effect of granting a new trial, which places the parties in the same position as if no trial had occurred. It also covers the grounds and procedures for arresting a judgment.

The statute appears to be a procedural rule applicable to all defendants, regardless of race. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence, and the category is null. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws.

classification.title: New Trial and Arrest of Judgment
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the grounds for a new trial and arrest of judgment. It details conditions such as verdicts against the law or evidence, newly discovered evidence, and unfair trials due to jury misconduct. It also specifies procedural requirements for defendants to raise these issues, including the necessity of timely objections and exceptions during the trial. The text references several court cases to illustrate these points.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses legal procedures related to new trials and the arrest of judgment in criminal and civil cases. It outlines general legal principles and procedural rules that apply to all defendants, regardless of race. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of statutes from 1906.

classification.title: New Trial and Arrest of Judgment
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code outlines the procedures and grounds for motions for a new trial and for the arrest of judgment. It specifies when such motions must be made, that grounds must be in writing, and defines the limited circumstances under which a judgment can be arrested (i.e., when the indictment fails to state a public offense within the court's jurisdiction). It also includes case law annotations related to these procedures.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details procedural aspects of the legal system concerning new trials and the arrest of judgment. It does not contain any language or provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race, nor does it relate to the typical categories associated with Jim Crow laws such as segregation, voting rights, or marriage. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Sections 281-282
classification.summary: These sections of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outline the rules and procedures for bills of exceptions in legal proceedings. Section 281 specifies that certain court decisions, such as those on challenges to the panel, for cause, motions to set aside indictments, and motions for a new trial, are not subject to exception. Section 282 details the process for preparing, settling, and signing a bill of exceptions, referencing provisions in the Civil Code and emphasizing the need for all instructions to be included. It also includes notes on various aspects of exceptions, such as the requirement to show expected testimony when evidence is rejected, the need for objections to adverse party's motions, the court's stance on insufficient evidence, improper arguments, and exceptions to instructions.

classification.title: Appeals - To the Court of Appeals - Felonies
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky discusses the conditions under which the Court of Appeals can reverse a felony conviction. It outlines that an error must appear on the record and that the substantial rights of the accused must have been prejudiced by the error. It also clarifies that errors must be properly excepted to at the time they occur and that certain matters, as defined in section 281, cannot be excepted to and therefore do not constitute reversible errors under section 340. The text includes case law citations to illustrate these points, particularly regarding the admission or rejection of evidence and the sufficiency of evidence to sustain a verdict.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is a legal commentary on procedural aspects of felony appeals within the Kentucky court system, specifically referencing sections of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906. It details the requirements for reversing a conviction based on errors in the record, the importance of proper exceptions, and the admissibility and relevance of evidence. There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code. Confidence is high because the text is purely procedural and lacks any racial indicators.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Section 341
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the requirements for appealing a judgment in felony cases to the Court of Appeals. It specifies that a judgment will not be reversed for errors in jury instructions unless the bill of exceptions contains all instructions given and demonstrates that the law was not correctly or fairly presented. It also details what constitutes reversible errors, such as errors in peremptory challenges, and the necessity of objections and exceptions for appeals. Several case examples are cited to illustrate these points.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text (Section 341 of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906) deals with procedural aspects of criminal appeals, specifically concerning jury instructions and the requirements for a bill of exceptions. It does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly refers to race, segregation, or differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of statutes. The confidence is high because the text is purely procedural and lacks any racial indicators. Human review is not needed.

classification.title: Appeals - To the Court of Appeals - Felonies
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines procedures for appeals in felony cases. It specifies that appeals terminate upon the death of the accused. It also details the process for defendants who are already serving a penitentiary sentence when an appeal is filed, stating they remain incarcerated during the appeal unless discharged by time served or pardon. Upon reversal and a new trial order, the defendant is to be returned to the county jail. The statute also addresses credit for time served in penitentiary on a subsequent conviction after a reversal and new trial.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details procedural aspects of felony appeals within the Kentucky legal system in 1906. It covers the termination of appeals upon death, the handling of incarcerated defendants during appeals, and credit for time served. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute appears to be a general procedural law related to the criminal justice system and not a Jim Crow law. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence score, and the category is null.

classification.title: Jurisdiction of Court of Appeals in Misdemeanors
classification.summary: This statute outlines the appellate jurisdiction of the Court of Appeals in Kentucky for criminal actions and prosecutions for misdemeanors. It specifies that the court only has jurisdiction if the judgment involves a fine exceeding fifty dollars, imprisonment exceeding thirty days, or if the judgment is for the defendant in cases where such penalties might have been inflicted. It also notes that defendants must surrender themselves or supersede the judgment to enter a motion for a new trial or prosecute an appeal.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details the appellate jurisdiction of the Court of Appeals in Kentucky concerning misdemeanors. It sets financial and imprisonment thresholds for appeals. There is no mention of race, and the provisions appear to apply generally to all defendants. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

It specifies the timing for filing appeals, the requirement for lodging the record within sixty days, and conditions under which appeals can be taken by the Commonwealth or the defendant, including those involving infamous offenses or fines. It also clarifies that certain proceedings, like those for forfeited bail bonds or tavern-keeper bond breaches, are civil actions and not subject to these misdemeanor appeal rules.

There is no mention of race, ethnicity, or any differential treatment based on these characteristics. Therefore, it does not appear to be a Jim Crow law. The document type is identified as 'codes' because it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

It details conditions for suspending execution of judgments, the process for appeals by the Commonwealth, and grounds for reversing judgments. Specifically, it states that judgments of acquittal for offenses punishable by imprisonment cannot be reversed, and that judgments will be reversed for errors of law that prejudice the substantial rights of the defendant.

classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures and requirements for obtaining security to keep the peace or for good behavior. It details the amounts of surety required, the duration for which it can be mandated (not exceeding one year), the process of returning bonds to the circuit court, and the conditions under which a defendant may be discharged or committed to jail for failure to provide surety. It also specifies that a magistrate or court can take surety after a defendant has been committed and that a defendant will be discharged if the prosecutor fails to appear or if there are no reasonable grounds to believe the defendant will commit an offense.
classification.reasoning: The statute pertains to general legal procedures for maintaining peace and good behavior, applicable to all individuals regardless of race. There are no explicit or implicit references to race, nor are there any provisions that suggest differential treatment based on race. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906. The confidence is high because the text clearly describes a general legal process without any racial component.

classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines procedures for breaches of bond, specifically concerning failure to appear in court, convictions for breach of peace, and convictions for felony. It also details the arrest and confinement of insane, drunken, and disorderly persons, outlining the duties of peace officers in arresting such individuals and their subsequent disposition.

The language and subject matter are general to law enforcement and judicial procedures. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of statutes.

classification.title: Arrest of Insane and Disorderly Persons
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for peace officers to arrest and detain insane, drunken, or disorderly persons. It details how these individuals should be brought before a magistrate, their temporary confinement, and the magistrate's powers to order restraint, commitment to jail, or require surety for good behavior. The statute also specifies notification procedures for the city or county attorney regarding insane persons to initiate proceedings for their commitment to a lunatic asylum.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses the arrest and disposition of individuals deemed insane, drunken, or disorderly. It focuses on public order and mental health procedures. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The procedures described are general and apply to any person fitting the described categories, regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Habeas Corpus
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code outlines the procedures and conditions for issuing and executing a writ of habeas corpus. It details who can issue the writ, the requirements for petitioning, the form and return of the writ, and the process for trial and judgment. It also specifies circumstances under which a person detained may or may not need to be produced, and exceptions to being imprisoned for the same cause after release.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details the legal process for a writ of habeas corpus, which is a fundamental legal right to challenge unlawful detention. The statute itself does not contain any explicit or implicit racial classifications or discriminatory provisions. It applies to any individual seeking to challenge their detention. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code from 1906.

It also addresses the possibility of changing the return day and requiring a bond before issuing the writ.

The statute appears to be a procedural law governing a fundamental legal right, applicable to all individuals regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Habeas Corpus
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures and legal framework for issuing and responding to writs of habeas corpus in Kentucky. It details how parties can challenge their imprisonment or detention, the process for hearings, judgments, and costs, and the conditions under which a person released on habeas corpus cannot be re-imprisoned for the same cause. It also addresses situations where a judge may admit a prisoner to bail or remand them to custody if found guilty of an offense, and allows for writs to be issued against religious or other associations detaining married women or infants.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the legal process for habeas corpus writs. It outlines the rights of individuals to challenge unlawful detention and the procedures for judges to hear such cases. There is no mention of race, nor any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. The statute appears to be a general procedural law applicable to all individuals. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906, Section 570 and Subdivision 2, Sections 3147-3148
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code outlines the jurisdiction and powers of courts, specifically focusing on the commitment and discharge of accused individuals, the taking of bonds, and the presentation of accused persons for trial within twenty-four hours. It also details the court's power to impose fines and penalties, including imprisonment and hard labor, with limitations on imprisonment duration and fines without a jury, unless waived. Cases involving child custody are to be tried by the court. Subdivision 2 addresses cities of the second class, granting their police courts exclusive original jurisdiction over city ordinance violations and concurrent jurisdiction with circuit courts and justices of the peace for pleas of the Commonwealth (excluding felonies). These police courts can take recognizances and enforce compliance, with powers similar to examining courts for committing offenders for trial. It also specifies jurisdiction over petit larceny and vagrancy.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details general court procedures, jurisdiction, and penalties for criminal offenses and ordinance violations. It outlines the powers of courts in handling arrests, trials, and sentencing, as well as specific jurisdictional grants for police courts in cities of the second class. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The language used is procedural and applies generally to all individuals within the jurisdiction of these courts. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Jurisdiction of Courts
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the jurisdiction of police courts in certain cities. It details procedures for handling vagrancy and petit larceny, including the requirement for recognizances to be made to the police court instead of the circuit court. Convicted individuals are to be sent to labor in the city prison or workhouse for a specified period. The statute also grants police judges jurisdiction over riots, routs, and unlawful assemblies within the city limits, and outlines the process for arresting offenders. Additionally, it grants the court power to take recognizances for appearance and to keep the peace, and defines the territorial jurisdiction of the court to be co-extensive with the city limits, extending to the Ohio and Licking rivers.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the jurisdiction and procedures for police courts in Kentucky cities, focusing on offenses like vagrancy, petit larceny, and public disturbances. While it describes the enforcement mechanisms and penalties, there is no explicit or implicit mention of race. The provisions apply generally to all individuals within the specified cities, regardless of their racial background. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the jurisdiction of police courts in cities of the third and fourth classes in Kentucky. For cities of the third class, it details exclusive jurisdiction over city ordinance violations and concurrent jurisdiction with justices of the peace for state law violations within city limits. It also covers jurisdiction over bail and recognizances, and prosecutions for ordinance violations occurring outside city limits. For cities of the fourth class, it describes the powers and jurisdiction of the city court judge, including acting as a conservator of the peace, ordering arrests, requiring bail, and taking recognizances.

It outlines their authority to prosecute violations of city ordinances and state laws, handle bail and recognizances, and act as examining courts. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute appears to be a general administrative and procedural law concerning municipal courts. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Courts Having Continuous Session
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures for circuit courts in counties with a population under 150,000 that constitute separate judicial districts. It details the continuous session requirements, places of holding court (including cities of the second class and county seats), jurisdiction, jury selection, and publication of legal notices. It also specifies how cases will be docketed and tried based on defendant residency and allows for plaintiff choice in certain cases involving foreign corporations, non-residents, and common carriers.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the administrative and procedural aspects of circuit courts in specific types of counties. It details where and when courts will be held, how cases will be docketed, and how legal notices will be published. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The focus is purely on judicial administration and jurisdiction based on population and location. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Change of Venue
classification.summary: This section of the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906 outlines the procedures and conditions for changing the venue of a legal action. It details the application process, the requirement for verified affidavits, the role of counter-affidavits, and the court's discretion in deciding the change. It specifies that the change should be to an adjacent county and outlines the terms and conditions, including the payment of removal expenses within ten days. classification.title: Change of Venue in Criminal Cases
classification.summary: This statute outlines the process for changing the venue of a criminal trial. It allows either the defendant or the Commonwealth to apply for a change of venue if a fair trial cannot be obtained in the original county. The trial can be moved to an adjacent county or, if necessary, to the most convenient county where a fair trial is possible. The statute also details conditions and objections related to venue changes, including the burden of proof on the applicant and the discretion of the court.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the procedural aspect of changing the venue for criminal trials. It provides a mechanism for ensuring a fair trial by allowing cases to be moved if the local county is prejudiced. There is no mention of race, and the provisions apply to all defendants and the Commonwealth equally. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is presented as part of a compiled code (Civil and Criminal Code 1906).

LAWS OF THE UNITED STATES UPON THE SUBJECT OF THE

CHAPTER VII .
RULES OF THE COURT OF APPEALS .
The followingwere ordered to be recorded as rules of practice
of the court :
1. Divisionof courtintotwo departments- sessions- dutiesof chief
justice. In accordance with section118 of the Constitution, this
court,afterJanuary 1,1895,willbe dividedintotwo departments,
each one of which shallconsistof threejudges,besidesthe chief
justice, who shallpresideover each department . Each division
shallsiton alternatedays during each week, when not in joint
session, to hear arguments and motions and deliveropinions.
Opinions shallbe deliveredas the judgment of the court without
referenceto the department deliveringthem . When the chief
justiceisabsent,or,ifpresent,from any causefailstopreside,the
judge next oldestin commission shallpreside with each depart-
ment,and shallrequirethepresenceofa judgefrom eitherdepart-

It includes templates for 'Merchant's Account' and 'Work and Labor' claims, outlining the necessary information to be included in a petition, such as plaintiff and defendant names, the nature of the debt, amounts owed, and requests for judgment and interest. It also includes a partial petition for 'Slander'.
classification.reasoning: The provided text consists of sample legal petition forms from the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky. These forms are templates for initiating civil lawsuits, specifically for debt collection (merchant's account, work and labor) and defamation (slander). There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws. The confidence is high because the content is purely procedural and lacks any racial element.

classification.title: Assignee against Assignor
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code provides a form for a petition where an assignee is suing an assignor. The assignee is seeking to recover the amount paid for a promissory note after the original debtor defaulted and had no property to satisfy the judgment. The form outlines the necessary details to include in the petition, such as the names of the parties, the details of the note, the judgment obtained, and the execution returned unsatisfied.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is a form for a civil petition concerning the assignment of a promissory note. It details a legal process for recovering funds when a debt is not paid. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a Civil Code. The confidence is high because the content is purely procedural and legal, with no racial element.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906 - Indemnifying Bond
classification.summary: This section of the Civil Code outlines the legal form for a petition related to an indemnifying bond. It details the process for a plaintiff to sue defendants who executed an indemnifying bond to a sheriff or constable. The bond was intended to cover damages to a claimant of property that was seized and sold under an execution. The petition states that the defendants broke the conditions of the bond by failing to pay the damages sustained by the plaintiff, who was the owner of the seized property.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is from the Civil and Criminal Code of 1906, specifically detailing the form for a petition concerning an indemnifying bond in civil litigation. The statute describes a legal process involving property seizure and sale under execution, and the requirement for a bond to protect against potential damages to claimants. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory intent or application based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code. The confidence is high because the text is purely procedural and lacks any racial component.

624
CIVIL CODE FORMS—PETITIONS.

**To Set Aside Sale Made to Prefer a Creditor.**
[See Ky. Stat., secs. 1910–1917, and notes thereto.]

John Smith, Plaintiff,
*vs.*
James Jones and Henry Allen, Defendants.
}
CIRCUIT COURT.
Petition in Equity.

The plaintiff, John Smith, states that the defendant, James Jones, by his promissory note, dated —, 189—, and executed and delivered to plaintiff, agreed to pay him, six months thereafter, — dollars, with six per cent interest thereon from date. Said note is filed as part hereof. No part of said debt has been paid. He states that defendant Jones is indebted to other persons in a large amount, and that in contemplation of insolvency, and designing to prefer defendant Henry Allen to the exclusion of his other creditors, defendant, when he did not have sufficient property to pay his debts, did, on —, 189—, and within six months next before the institution of this action, sell and deliver to said Allen [here describe property] of the value of — dollars, to satisfy a debt due by said Jones to said Allen, and created long before —, 189—.

Wherefore plaintiff prays that said sale be adjudged to operate as an assignment of all the property of defendant Jones for the payment *pro rata* of his debts, and that such orders be made as may be necessary to protect this plaintiff and the other creditors of defendant, and for all other proper relief.
J. R., Atty. for Plff.

**Division of Land.**
[See sec. 499 of Code, and notes thereto.]

Wm. Walker, Plaintiff,
*vs.*
Sam Walker and Tom Walker, Defendants.
}
CIRCUIT COURT.
Petition in Equity.

The plaintiff, Wm. Walker, states that he and the defendants, Sam Walker and Tom Walker, own each an undivided [here state interest owned by each] interest in a tract of land in — county, bounded and described as follows [here insert boundary], and containing — acres. They received said land by [here state person from whom land was received and how plaintiff and defendants became the owners of it]. He files as part hereof, marked "A," copies of the evidences of the title to said land. He says that no other persons own any interest in said land, and that he and the defendants are each entitled to an undivided [state interest of each] thereof.

[If the land is subject to dower and it is desired to allot the dower as well as divide the remainder of the land, the petition should state facts, and the owner of dower interest should be a party plaintiff or defendant, and the petitionition should aver that after setting apart dower interest the other parties are entitled to the remainder, according to their respective interests.]

Wherefore plaintiff prays that commissioners be appointed to divide said land between plaintiff and defendant, allotting to each [here state interest each is entitled to], according to quantity, quality and value [if dower is to be allotted the prayer of the petition should be changed to conform to the facts and should ask that one-third of the land, including the mansion house and the out-buildings, be set apart to the widow, and the remainder divided between the other parties according to their interests, stating them]. He prays that the costs of division be apportioned between the parties hereto in proportion to their respective interests, and for all proper relief.
J. R., Atty. for Plff.

classification.title: CAPIAS PRO FINE
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the legal procedures for issuing a 'Capias Pro Fine,' which is a writ to arrest an individual who has failed to pay a fine and costs assessed against them in a penal prosecution. It provides forms for executions upon judgments from both the Circuit Court and a Justice of the Peace, detailing the commands to sheriffs and constables to take the body of the convicted person until the fine and costs are satisfied.
classification.reasoning: The provided text describes a legal writ ('Capias Pro Fine') used for the enforcement of fines and costs in criminal prosecutions. It details the procedural aspects of arresting an individual who has not paid their dues to the Commonwealth. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled code of laws. The confidence is high because the text is purely procedural and lacks any racial component.

classification.title: Writ of Habeas Corpus
classification.summary: This statute outlines the procedures and forms for issuing a writ of habeas corpus in Kentucky. It details the information required in the writ, how to change the return date, how to notify the applicant of such changes, and the bond required to ensure the detained person does not escape and to cover costs. The statute provides a template for the writ itself, specifying the recipient, the detained individual, the court, and the date and time for the hearing. It also includes provisions for modifying the hearing date and requiring a bond from sureties.
classification.reasoning: The provided text details the legal process for a writ of habeas corpus, which is a fundamental legal right used to challenge unlawful detention. The statute itself is procedural and does not contain any language that explicitly or implicitly discriminates based on race. It applies to any individual being detained, regardless of their race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of the 'Civil and Criminal Code 1906'.

classification.title: Clerks of Courts
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code outlines the duties and responsibilities of Clerks of Courts. It details their roles in issuing executions on judgments, filing papers, handling specific legal actions like forcible entry and detainer, appointing guardians ad litem, keeping indexes of judgments and transcripts, issuing injunctions, and managing trust estates. It also covers the issuance and service of summons, the administration of oaths, and the preparation of transcripts. The text also touches upon the application and construction of the code itself.

It covers aspects such as who can give depositions, when they can be used, the process of taking them (including interrogatories and notice), the powers of officers conducting depositions, and fees associated with the process. It also details provisions for depositions of prisoners and for perpetuating testimony.

There is no mention of race, racial classifications, or any provisions that would suggest discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky pertains to legal procedures involving infants (minors). It covers various aspects such as their privileges in the Court of Appeals, time limits for appeals, amendment of assignments of errors, and verification of pleadings. It also details the roles and responsibilities of guardians, committees, next friends, and guardians ad litem in representing infants in legal actions, including their ability to sue, defense for infants, appointment, duties, compensation, and limitations on judgments against them. The text also touches upon injunctions related to infants and the service of summons and notice on them or their representatives. Additionally, it mentions provisions for non-resident infants and the sale of their real estate.

classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines the procedures and rules for changing the venue (location) of legal proceedings in circuit courts. It details how costs are paid, the order of different types of changes, and the transmission of original papers and transcripts. It also covers challenges to juries, including actual and implied bias, and the process for peremptory challenges. The text also touches upon the jurisdiction of circuit courts, including appellate jurisdiction and the jurisdiction over offenses committed in different counties or across bounding rivers.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906 - Examining Court and Execution Sections
classification.summary: This excerpt from the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky details procedures related to examining courts, including witness examination, exclusion of spectators, and the substance of testimony. It also covers aspects of execution of judgments, such as confinement periods for fines, replevin, sheriff's returns, suspension of death sentences, and supersedeas bonds for appeals in misdemeanors. Sections on exemptions and exceptions are also present, along with definitions and procedures for felonies, including bail, presence of the defendant, and joint defendants' trials. Fines and their execution, as well as abatement of nuisances, are also addressed.

classification.title: Forms Grand Jury - Criminal
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines various forms and procedures related to the grand jury in criminal proceedings. It covers topics such as notices, orders of court, judgments on conviction, recognizances, security to keep the peace, subpoenas, summons, supersedeas, and warrants of arrest. It details the duties and responsibilities of grand jurors, including the requirement for nine jurors to concur, their duty to disclose offenses known, and their secrecy obligations. It also addresses the formation of the grand jury, the evidence they may receive, and grounds for setting aside indictments.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is a section from the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky, specifically detailing forms and procedures for the grand jury in criminal cases. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices against any racial group. The content is purely procedural and relates to the functioning of the justice system. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: Civil and Criminal Code 1906 - Pleading Prev't'n Pub. Offense
classification.summary: This section of the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky outlines procedures for pleading in criminal offenses, particularly focusing on prevention of public offenses. It details requirements for oral pleadings in police and justice courts, the necessity of pleadings for judgments, and conditions under which former acquittals or convictions serve as a bar. It also covers the removal of prosecutions, security to keep the peace, issuance of subpoenas and summonses, and the jurisdiction and procedures of police and city courts, including warrants, continuances, and trials. The text also touches upon the prevention of public offenses through security measures and the duty of officers in cases of riots and unlawful assemblies.

It details procedural aspects of criminal law, including pleading, court processes, and the prevention of public offenses. There is no mention of race or any provisions that explicitly or implicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is part of a compiled legal code. Confidence is high because the content is purely procedural and lacks any racial component.

classification.title: Riots & Unlawful Assemblies - Criminal
classification.summary: This index entry from the 1906 Civil and Criminal Code of Kentucky lists sections related to riots and unlawful assemblies. It covers the duties of officers to suppress such events, the jurisdiction of justices of the peace, the role of the military in suppression, and punishments for neglecting duties or failing to aid officers. It also details procedures for arresting rioters who do not disperse and when the military may use force. The index also includes entries for 'Security to Keep the Peace', 'Robbery', 'Routs', 'Sanity', 'Senate of Kentucky', 'Search Warrant', and 'Setting Aside Indictment'.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is an index to a legal code, specifically referencing sections on 'Riots & Unlawful Assemblies' and related legal procedures. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any discriminatory practices that would classify it as a Jim Crow law. The content pertains to general criminal law and public order. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with high confidence. The category is null as it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'codes' as it is an index within a compiled code.
